findings.problem: Course/coach names containing '&' render the literal entity '&' on the catalog cards (and elsewhere). queries.ts builds DB.catalog with already-escaped values (queries.ts:1430 `name: esc(pickLang(...))`, `coach: esc(...)`, `format: esc(...)`, `modality: esc(...)`), but S.catalog re-escapes them.
findings.rootCause: Double-escape: S.catalog interpolates esc(c.name)/esc(c.coach) over values that queries.ts already passed through esc(). Same class as the leaderboard double-escape fixed in a062cd5.
findings.userImpact: On primary discovery surface a course literally reads 'Oratoria &amp; Speaking' (already & in capture, doubles again here), undermining the premium feel and trust. Affects every name with '&'.
findings.recommendation: In app/lib/scr-extra.ts S.catalog.card, drop the redundant esc(): use ${c.name}, ${c.coach}, ${c.code} (code is not esc'd in queries so keep esc(c.code)). Verify against queries.ts:1430 which fields are pre-escaped and only esc the raw ones. Do the same audit, not blanket-strip.

findings.problem: The 'Personas' section of search is permanently empty for the student role — the only role that can reach search. S.search reads DB.students (scr-extra.ts:297), but queries.ts only populates base.students inside the isTeacher block (queries.ts:1514-1518); for a STUDENT it is undefined, so (DB.students||[]) yields [] every time. Result count and the people group are silently always courses-only.
findings.rootCause: Data contract mismatch: search consumes a teacher-only collection. Students hit search via the topbar input (Aula.tsx:953-955), but never have DB.students; teachers/admins have it but use dedicated roster screens instead.
findings.userImpact: A student searching a classmate's name gets 'Sin resultados' even when the person exists. The advertised people-search is dead for its primary audience — a broken feature presented as working.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) expose a role-safe, privacy-scoped people index to students in queries.ts (e.g. cohort/leaderboard-derived rows with name+level only, same opt-in rules as leaderboard) and point S.search at it; or (b) if people-search is out of scope for students per PRD, remove the 'Personas' section from S.search and reword the page-sub to 'cursos' so the count and copy are honest. Prefer (b) if no privacy-cleared people source exists.

findings.problem: The Achievements card's footer button is a verbatim duplicate of the hero's primary CTA — same icon, same label, same onclick (scr-core.ts:308 reuses na.onclick/na.cta from the hero at 96-114). The page now shows the identical primary action twice above the fold, and the hero CTA is the visually dominant navy/green button.
findings.rootCause: CNV-05 added a 'close the gap' CTA but bound it to the exact same action object as the hero instead of a distinct, achievement-specific next step.
findings.userImpact: Redundant choice violates 'exactly ONE obvious next action' (the screen's own stated PRD rule, scr-core.ts:54). Two identical buttons add visual noise and momentarily make users wonder if they differ.
findings.recommendation: In scr-core.ts achievements card, either remove the duplicate button entirely (the hero already carries that CTA) or repoint it to a genuinely different, badge-relevant action (e.g. onclick=go('badges') 'Ver insignias'). Removal is the simpler, higher-value fix.

findings.problem: Above-the-fold the student dashboard stacks: hero + 4 KPI tiles + skill radar (6 bars) + 3 reco cards + (right rail) upcoming + debate rank + achievements + leaderboard strip. That is 8+ distinct card regions and ~25 discrete data points competing on first paint; the capture also flags the 4 KPI cards as very tall on mobile (heavy scroll for 4 numbers).
findings.rootCause: Every PRD §4.2 module is rendered at full weight simultaneously with no progressive disclosure or de-emphasis of secondary modules.
findings.userImpact: First-time/non-power users face >7±2 simultaneous regions; the 'one next step' intent is diluted by equally-weighted cards. On mobile the KPI row alone is a full screen of scroll.
findings.recommendation: (1) Collapse the 4 KPI tiles to a 2x2 compact grid on mobile (already a candidate in capture) — adjust .grid.g-4 responsive rule in app/styles/responsive.css. (2) Visually subordinate the reco + leaderboard blocks (smaller heading weight / muted) so hero + next-session read as primary. No data removal needed; this is hierarchy, not deletion.

findings.problem: Every catalog card renders a price slot that is always 'Gratis': queries.ts forces price:0 for all courses (queries.ts:1430 `price: 0` per the membership-value PRD §13.1), so the `c.price > 0` branch in S.catalog (scr-extra.ts:191) is dead and every card shows a green 'Gratis' badge.
findings.rootCause: Per-course sale is disabled (PRD §13.1) yet the card template still carries price-display logic, producing a uniform low-information 'Gratis' badge on every card.
findings.userImpact: 'Gratis' on 100% of cards is noise that implies a pricing axis that does not exist, and slightly cheapens the 'prestige/alto rendimiento' brand the tokens explicitly target. The dead `price>0` branch is latent debt.
findings.recommendation: Remove the price slot from S.catalog.card and replace with a more useful signal (format/modality chip, like the dashboard reco card already shows at scr-core.ts:183), or a single 'Incluido en tu membresía' note shown once on the page header rather than per card. Drop the dead price>0 branch.

findings.problem: In the leaderboard-fallback 'Tu actividad reciente' block the dashboard re-escapes activity titles with esc(a.title) (scr-core.ts:332), but DB.activity.title is already esc()'d in queries.ts:1439. A title containing '&' would render '&'. (The parallel S.progress 'Subidas recientes' is correct — it uses ${ev.title} without re-escaping at scr-profile.ts:126.)
findings.rootCause: Inconsistent escaping convention between the two consumers of DB.activity; dashboard double-escapes, progress does not.
findings.userImpact: Same '&' artifact as CORE-01 but only on activity rows containing '&' in the leaderboard-empty fallback — low exposure, real defect.
findings.recommendation: In scr-core.ts leaderboard fallback, change esc(a.title) to ${a.title} and esc(a.detail) to ${a.detail||''} to match the already-escaped contract (and the progress screen). Keep esc(a.when) only if when is raw (it is whenLabel, not esc'd — verify; queries.ts:1440 `when` is not esc'd so keep esc there).

findings.problem: The grade letter and 'En revisión'/'—' states are conveyed by color+badge only, and the coach-feedback expandable row (.fb-row, scr-learn.ts:661) is a visually-associated <tr> with no programmatic link to its parent grade row. Screen-reader users hear an orphaned 'Comentario del coach' block with no binding to the activity it grades, and the A/B letter color (ok green vs sky) is the only differentiator between letter tiers.
findings.rootCause: Feedback is rendered as a sibling table row rather than an aria-associated detail; letter status relies on the C.badge tone color without a non-color text cue beyond the letter glyph itself (which is fine) but the row association is missing.
findings.userImpact: Non-visual users cannot reliably tell which activity a coach comment belongs to; the table reads as alternating data/comment rows with no relationship.
findings.recommendation: Give the main <tr> an id and the .fb-row an aria-label referencing the activity name (e.g. `Comentario del coach sobre ${activity}`), or restructure feedback as a <details>/disclosure inside the activity cell. Minimal: add aria-label to the fb-row alert in scr-learn.ts:661.

findings.problem: Locked badge cards differentiate earned vs locked purely via a lock glyph + reduced opacity ('.locked' class) and a corner icon; the medal art and name remain, but there is no text status for assistive tech distinguishing 'Ganada' from a locked badge — only the earned cards get a visible 'Ganada' badge (scr-profile.ts:164), locked cards get just a lock IC with no accessible label.
findings.rootCause: Locked state is communicated by icon+opacity without an equivalent text/aria label.
findings.userImpact: Screen-reader users hear the badge name and description for a locked badge identically to an earned one, with only a bare lock icon (no label) to distinguish — the achievement/locked distinction is lost non-visually.
findings.recommendation: Add an accessible label to the locked corner marker in scr-profile.ts:164 (e.g. wrap the lock IC with aria-label='Bloqueada' / a visually-hidden 'Bloqueada' span), mirroring the visible 'Ganada' on earned cards.

findings.problem: The quiz answer options (.q-opt) and question-jump dots (.q-dot) are plain <div>s with click listeners attached in mount(); they have no role, no tabindex, and are never focusable. A keyboard or screen-reader user literally cannot select an answer or navigate questions — the single most important interaction in the Learn flow is mouse-only. The global [A11Y-01] Enter/Space handler in Aula.tsx (line 960) only fires for elements matching [role=button][tabindex], which these never get (unlike the .mitem and <tr> rows in scr-core.ts which DO get role/tabindex).
findings.rootCause: q-opt/q-dot were built as styled divs with imperative click wiring in S.quiz.mount() and were never given role/tabindex, so they fall outside the project's own [A11Y-01] keyboard-activation contract.
findings.userImpact: Keyboard-only and assistive-tech students cannot take any quiz. There is no fallback. This blocks course completion (quizzes gate progress) for an entire access class.
findings.recommendation: In S.quiz.mount() paint(), render each .q-opt with role="radio" tabindex="0" inside a role="radiogroup" (aria-labelledby the question), and each .q-dot with role="tab"/button tabindex="0"; add an aria-checked/aria-current state. Either reuse the [A11Y-01] handler (give them role=button tabindex=0) or add keydown(Enter/Space + Arrow keys) handling in mount(). Add :focus-visible is already global so focus ring comes for free once they are focusable.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-learn.ts → S.quiz.mount() paint() (.q-opt / .q-dot rendering, ~lines 448-453); contract in app/components/Aula.tsx line 960

findings.lens: Nielsen (match real world, error prevention) / Exploratory QA
findings.problem: currentQuiz() (lines 33-49) falls back, when the clicked lesson has no quiz, to the FIRST quiz with questions in ANY enrolled course — then a second fallback to any quizByLesson entry. A student who clicks 'Examen — Unidad 3' on a lesson whose quiz is not yet published silently gets served a DIFFERENT unit's exam, with that other exam's title, and an attempt POSTs to that quiz id. There is no signal it is the wrong exam.
findings.rootCause: The fallback chain prioritizes 'show some quiz' over 'show the right quiz or nothing', conflating empty-state avoidance with correctness.
findings.userImpact: Student takes and is graded on the wrong exam; priorQuizAttempt() matching by lesson title will also mis-associate the best score. Silent data/correctness defect on a graded, progress-gating surface.
findings.recommendation: Scope currentQuiz() to the active lesson only: if window.__quizLesson resolves to a lesson with no quiz, return null and render the existing 'No hay examen disponible' empty state (already present at lines 372-384). Drop the cross-course and 'any quizByLesson entry' fallbacks, or guard them behind an explicit same-lesson check.

findings.lens: Content reduction / Nielsen (match real world) / Honesty
findings.problem: The 'Calificación' card hardcodes an identical rubric for EVERY 100-point assignment — Estructura (CWI) 30 / Claridad y voz 25 / Evidencia 25 / Tiempo y cierre 20 (lines 196-199). This is presented as if it were this specific activity's grading breakdown, but it is a static debate-speech template shown regardless of what the assignment actually is (e.g. a written essay or a research upload). For non-100-point assignments it correctly falls back to a generic message — proving the 100-point branch is fabricated specificity.
findings.rootCause: A demo placeholder rubric was left inline in the render template instead of reading a real rubric from L (e.g. L.rubric).
findings.userImpact: Students optimize toward criteria that may not apply; coaches grade on different criteria than shown. Erodes trust the moment a student notices two unrelated assignments share the same '30/25/25/20' rubric.
findings.recommendation: Render the rubric only from real data (L.rubric / L.rubricItems). If absent, use the same honest generic message already used for non-100-point assignments ('Tu coach la revisará y te dará una nota con feedback'). Remove the hardcoded array at lines 197-198.

findings.problem: Heading semantics are inconsistent across the cluster. S.lesson correctly uses <h1 class="page-title">. But S.quiz and S.assignment render their page title as a <div class="page-title"> (no heading element at all), and S.quizResults and S.player start at <h2> with no <h1> on the page. The quiz question itself (.q-text, lines 449) is a styled div, not a heading, so SR users get zero heading landmarks while taking an exam.
findings.rootCause: page-title is a visual class applied to whatever element is convenient; only the lesson screen was upgraded to a real <h1>.
findings.userImpact: Screen-reader users cannot navigate by heading; the document outline is broken (h2 with no h1) on 3 of 4 learn screens, failing WCAG 1.3.1 / 2.4.6 expectations and breaking the consistency the rest of the app earns.
findings.recommendation: Make the top page title an <h1> on quiz/assignment/quizResults/player (reuse .page-title styling on an <h1>). Render the quiz question prompt as an <h2>/<h3> instead of <div class="q-text">. Align with S.lesson which already does this.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-learn.ts → S.quiz.render() line 406 + paint() line 449, S.assignment.render() line 147, S.quizResults.render() line 537, S.player.render() line 606

findings.problem: Line 514: const tone = passed ? (pct >= 90 ? 'ok' : 'ok') : ... — both branches of the ternary return 'ok', so the intended '90%+ honors' distinction never affects the ring/badge color. The 'Aprobado con honores' text (line 536) shows but the visual tone is identical to a plain pass, so the honors moment is visually flat.
findings.rootCause: Copy-paste error: the honors branch was meant to use a distinct tone (e.g. a gold/achievement token per the brand book) but both arms were left as 'ok'.
findings.userImpact: The achievement moment (90%+) is under-celebrated — a missed reinforcement on the exact screen designed to motivate. Also a code smell that signals the branch is untested.
findings.recommendation: Either collapse to const tone = passed ? 'ok' : (pct >= 50 ? 'warn' : 'danger') and drop the dead inner ternary, OR make 90%+ use the achievement token (--otr-gold) for the ring so honors reads visually. Pick one; do not ship a no-op ternary.

findings.problem: For a REAL lesson the in-lesson outline (aside.lesson-outline) collapses to a single dead anchor: {title} (line 604). It is a no-op link that duplicates the page title and provides no navigation — the 'En esta lección' rail only does real anchor-jumping for the legacy demo prose (#s1/#s2/#s3). So in production every real lesson shows a TOC rail containing one fake link to itself.
findings.rootCause: The outline was built for the demo prose with hardcoded section anchors; real lessons (contentHtml) have no parsed headings, so the code substitutes a single self-referential placeholder rather than hiding the rail.
findings.userImpact: Wasted ~220px sticky rail on desktop showing a clickable-looking link that does nothing (violates 'links go somewhere'); adds visual noise and a Fitts/Jakob mismatch. Apply the disappear test: if this single-item TOC vanished, no user would miss it.
findings.recommendation: For real lessons either (a) hide the outline rail entirely (let lesson-wrap go single-column) when there are no in-content headings, or (b) parse <h2>/<h3> from L.contentHtml at render and build real #anchor links. Do not render a self-link with onclick=return false. Fix in S.lesson.render() lines 601-607.

findings.problem: Completion is handled two different ways across two screens for the same act. In S.player the 'Marcar como completada' button sits in the header row next to a 'Volver' button (lines 609-612), while in S.lesson the same control lives down in the outline aside (lines 609-613). Same data-action, two placements. Additionally the player has both a header 'Volver' (line 610) and a footer 'Volver al curso' when there is no next item (nextLabel, line 591) — two 'back to course' affordances on one short screen.
findings.rootCause: player and lesson are separate builders that each grew their own completion + back-nav affordances without a shared placement convention.
findings.userImpact: Recognition-over-recall is undermined: a student who learned where 'mark complete' lives on a lesson must re-hunt for it on a video. Duplicate 'Volver' choices add Hick's-law overhead on a screen whose job is 'watch, then continue'.
findings.recommendation: Standardize: put 'mark complete' in the same location on both (recommend the primary action row near the bottom/next-button, since completion logically follows consuming the content). On player, drop the header 'Volver' and keep a single back path; let the footer hold {mark complete}{next/back to course}.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-learn.ts → S.player.render() lines 604-618; app/lib/scr-core.ts → S.lesson.render() lines 595-614

findings.lens: Status visibility / Cognitive load (Tesler) / Error prevention
findings.problem: During the quiz there is no persistent 'answered X of Y' summary or save state — progress is encoded only in small numbered dots, and all answers live in a mount() closure (answers object, line 427). Navigating away (or an accidental go()) discards every answer with no warning and no resume. The only completeness feedback is a toast at finalize time telling you how many are missing (line 466), after the user thinks they are done.
findings.rootCause: Quiz state is ephemeral local state with no draft persistence and no always-visible progress counter; the system pushes the burden of tracking completion onto the user (anti-Tesler).
findings.userImpact: On a multi-question exam, a mis-click on the sidebar loses all work. Power users get no at-a-glance 'how close am I'; first-timers only discover unanswered questions at the end. Increases anxiety on a graded surface.
findings.recommendation: (1) Add a small always-visible 'Respondidas N/total' counter near the dots or the finalize button. (2) Persist answers to sessionStorage keyed by quiz.id so a teardown/navigation can restore. (3) Optionally confirm before leaving with unsaved answers. Lands in S.quiz.render()/mount(), lines 412-419 (counter) and 421-489 (state).
findings.file: app/lib/scr-learn.ts → S.quiz.render() lines 412-419 and S.quiz.mount() lines 421-489

findings.problem: The voice-recorder controls (#rec-reset, #rec-toggle, #rec-play, lines 166-168) are icon-only buttons whose only accessible name is a title attribute. title is not reliably announced by screen readers and is invisible to touch users. The recorder waveform bars and timer also have no aria-live, so a SR user gets no feedback that recording started/stopped or how long it has run (status text in #rec-status is updated but the node is a plain div, not a live region).
findings.rootCause: Icon buttons rely on title for naming and the dynamic status div is not marked as a live region.
findings.userImpact: Blind/low-vision students cannot reliably operate or get feedback from the primary audio-submission path (audio recording is core to an oratory product). WCAG 4.1.2 (name) and 4.1.3 (status messages) gaps.
findings.recommendation: Add aria-label to #rec-reset/#rec-toggle/#rec-play (e.g. 'Grabar', 'Reproducir', 'Descartar') and update toggle's aria-label when it flips to pause. Add role="status" aria-live="polite" to #rec-status (line 164) so 'Grabando…'/'Grabado mm:ss — guardado' are announced.

findings.problem: When a coach has not configured submitKinds, the assignment shows all three submission methods stacked at once — a dark voice recorder, a file dropzone, and a free-text area — each labeled 'O…' (Or…). That is three co-equal primary inputs plus a rubric card plus a previous-entry card, presenting a wall of equally-weighted choices for what is usually one intended submission type.
findings.rootCause: Default-on for all three kinds (showRec/showFile/showText all true when subKinds is null, lines 96-98) with no progressive disclosure.
findings.userImpact: First-time/non-technical students face decision paralysis (which one do I use?) and a long scroll; the 'O' prefixes hint at exclusivity but everything is visible simultaneously. Heavier than Stripe/Notion would ship for a single submission.
findings.recommendation: Default to a single segmented control (Grabar / Subir / Escribir) that reveals one input at a time, defaulting to the most common (record for an oratory product). Keep all three only when the coach explicitly enabled multiple. Lands in S.assignment.render() lines 95-189.

findings.problem: The debate history cards (viewHistory) and tournament cards (viewTournaments) are clickable <div class="tile click"> elements carrying data-debate / data-tournament-card, but they have no role="button", no tabindex, and no aria. The screen's mount() wires a plain click listener (root.querySelectorAll('[data-debate]')), so a mouse opens the ballot modal but a keyboard user cannot focus or activate the card at all. The app's own [A11Y-01] handler in Aula.tsx (line 960) only fires .click() for elements matching '[role="button"][tabindex]' — these cards match neither, so they are completely keyboard-inoperable.
findings.rootCause: Clickable behavior was attached via data-attribute + JS listener without the semantic/keyboard scaffolding the rest of the app standardized on (role=button + tabindex=0), so the central A11Y-01 keydown bridge never picks them up.
findings.userImpact: Keyboard-only and screen-reader users cannot open a debate ballot or inspect a tournament — a primary interaction of the flagship screen is unreachable without a mouse. Fails WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard).
findings.recommendation: In scr-debate.ts add role="button" tabindex="0" (and an aria-label like 'Ver ballot vs <opponent>') to the history tile (line 225, data-debate) and the tournament card (line 355). The existing Aula.tsx onKey bridge will then activate them on Enter/Space — or add an explicit keydown handler in mount() alongside the click listener. Tournament cards that are purely informational (no data-tournament action) should NOT get role=button; only the ones that open detail.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-debate.ts (viewHistory line 225 tile; viewTournaments line 355 tile) + verify against Aula.tsx onKey line 960

findings.problem: The hub fans out into six top-level sub-tabs (Resumen, Mis debates, Práctica, Leaderboard, Torneos, Analytics) defined in TABS (line 27). Resumen already surfaces a 'Debates recientes' list (links to history), a 'Próximo evento' card (links to tournaments), and KPIs derived from history; Práctica embeds a leaderboard-derived 'rivales cerca de tu rating' finder plus a 'Ver leaderboard completo' link. So Overview, History, Practice, Leaderboard and Tournaments heavily overlap — the same data is re-presented across five destinations.
findings.rootCause: Each PRD §6 capability was given its own tab rather than being composed; the result duplicates content and forces the user to learn six locations for ~3 conceptual jobs (my standing, my rounds, compete).
findings.userImpact: First-time and non-power users face six choices with unclear differentiation (Resumen vs Mis debates vs Práctica all show debates/ratings), inflating learnability and cognitive load and slowing the find-the-action path.
findings.recommendation: Merge to 3-4 tabs: fold 'Práctica' partner-finder into Leaderboard (it is just leaderboard rows filtered by rating), and consider merging Torneos into Resumen's 'Próximo evento' as an expandable section, OR drop the redundant Resumen 'Debates recientes' block since 'Mis debates' is one tab away. Re-evaluate each tab with 'if it disappeared, would users miss it?' — Práctica's unique asset is only the PF timer.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-debate.ts — TABS array (line 27) + renderTab (line 447); viewOverview (line 161) recentCard/nextEventCard overlap

findings.problem: Analytics record rows read the wrong key. recordRow (line 378) computes name via r.format || r.side || r.name, but the data contract documented at the top of the file (lines 6-11) and reinforced by the inline [auditoría] note (line 384) says byFormat rows are {name,wins,losses,draws}, NOT {format,...}. The fields actually shipped by queries/getAppData determine which branch wins. If the live rows carry {name,...} this happens to work; but the bySide cards rely on r.side which is also not in the documented {name,...} contract. The criteria card separately reads c.criterion || c.name (line 437) and the ballot scores read s.criterion (line 555) — three different key conventions for 'the label' inside one file.
findings.rootCause: The DB→render contract drifted: the header comment documents {name}, the audit comment claims the live key is {format}/{side}, and the criteria/ballot code uses {criterion}. No single source of truth, so fallbacks paper over a real schema mismatch.
findings.userImpact: If the live shape ever differs from the guessed order, format/side/criterion labels render blank (esc('')), giving silent empty rows in the premium Analytics surface with no error — the worst kind of data bug because it looks intentional.
findings.recommendation: Pin the contract in one place: decide the canonical key (recommend {name}) and normalize at the data layer (queries.ts / getAppData) so recordRow can read a single r.name. Remove the speculative r.format||r.side fallback chain and the contradictory header vs audit comments. Add a non-null assertion or visible '—' placeholder so a missing label is detectable, not silent.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-debate.ts — recordRow (line 378-388), critCard (line 437); align with queries.ts analytics shape

findings.problem: This file ships its own openModal() (line 621) and its own .seg segmented-button wiring (line 631) and its own modal foot markup, duplicating the modal machinery already centralized in Aula.tsx (multiple openModal-style helpers at lines 102, 151, 238, 276, 427, 553). It works because the global MutationObserver enhancer ([A11Y-03], Aula.tsx line 967) retro-fits aria-modal/focus-trap/Escape onto ANY .modal-scrim — but the screen-local modal is the only one that re-implements segmented buttons and the data-ok/data-x lifecycle by hand, creating drift risk (e.g. the local okBtn loading state uses textContent='Guardando…' while no shared spinner).
findings.rootCause: The flagship screen predates or bypassed the shared modal helper and reinvented it, so modal behavior is maintained in two places.
findings.userImpact: Mostly maintainer-facing, but users can see subtle inconsistency (button labels, error placement .dm-err vs .fm-err) versus modals opened elsewhere in the app; any future fix to the shared modal won't reach this one.
findings.recommendation: Extract a single openModal/openFormModal helper (it already exists in Aula.tsx) and have scr-debate.ts call it instead of its private copy at line 621. At minimum, delete the redundant .seg wiring (line 631) since a shared helper should own it. The A11Y enhancer already covers aria, so this is purely de-duplication.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-debate.ts — openModal (line 621-658) duplicates Aula.tsx modal helpers

findings.lens: Status visibility / error prevention (Nielsen) / data integrity
findings.problem: Tournament registration optimistically mutates client state and never reconciles. In mount() (line 495), on a successful POST the code does (DB.tournaments||[]).forEach(t=>{ if(t.id===id) t.registered=true }) and repaints. There is no re-fetch, so the badge flips to 'Inscrito' based purely on a 200 — if the server later changes status (waitlist, capacity), the UI lies until a full reload. Worse, the record-debate flow's onDone callback (openRecordDebate line 615) just calls repaint() which re-runs getDebate() against the SAME in-memory DB.debate — a newly recorded debate will NOT appear in 'Mis debates' or move KPIs until the app refetches, yet the toast says 'Debate registrado' implying it landed.
findings.rootCause: The screen optimistically patches local DB but relies on no global refresh()/refetch after writes, unlike other write paths in Aula.tsx that call refresh().
findings.userImpact: After registering a debate the user sees a success toast but the new round is absent from history and the rating/KPIs are unchanged — they may re-submit (creating duplicates) or distrust the product. Classic 'did it actually save?' failure.
findings.recommendation: After a successful POST /api/debates (line 613) and POST /api/tournaments (line 502), call the global refresh() / re-hydrate getAppData before repaint(), instead of (or in addition to) the local mutation. Then 'Mis debates' and KPIs reflect the new round and the tournament badge reflects server truth.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-debate.ts — mount() tournament handler (line 502-505) and openRecordDebate onOk/onDone (line 613-616)

findings.problem: Several decorative-but-informational badges sit below readable size/contrast. The recent-form pills in heroPanel render result deltas at 11px (line 143) and the 'Forma reciente' tiles encode WIN/LOSS only by a single colored letter (W/L/D) on color — color alone conveys outcome (WCAG 1.4.1). In the practice finder the proximity badge uses font-size:10.5px (line 308) and in Analytics the W/L/D legend badges are 10.5px (line 390). The diff badge toggles var(--ok) green vs --otr-sky which is ALSO green (tokens.css: --otr-sky aliases --otr-green) — so 'close match' (ok) and 'further match' (sky) are nearly the same hue, defeating the encoding.
findings.rootCause: Heavy reliance on tiny tinted badges and on the green/green alias collision (--otr-sky === --otr-green) means two 'different' states render as the same color and below the recommended ~12px floor.
findings.userImpact: Low-vision and color-blind users cannot reliably read 11px/10.5px badges or distinguish the WIN/LOSS letter pills and the ±diff proximity tiers; the green-vs-green distance badge is meaningless.
findings.recommendation: (1) Add a text/aria label to form pills (title is present but the visible letter is the only cue — keep the W/L/D letter AND keep a non-color cue). (2) Raise the 10.5px/11px badges to >=12px. (3) For the proximity badge (line 308) replace the ok/sky pair with two genuinely distinct tokens (e.g. ok green vs warn gold) since --otr-sky is green and visually identical to ok.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-debate.ts — heroPanel form pills (line 141-143), viewPractice finder badge (line 308), recordRow legend (line 390)

findings.lens: Match real world / minimalist aesthetic / content reduction
findings.problem: The hero leads with 'Tu rating Glicko-2' and '±350 RD · provisional' (heroPanel line 120-125). For the Spanish-first student / non-technical / elderly persona, 'Glicko-2' and 'RD' (rating deviation) are unexplained jargon presented as the single largest, most prominent element on the flagship screen. The provisional alert helps, but the algorithm name and the RD figure are Optional/Noise to ~95% of users — they cannot act on them.
findings.rootCause: Implementation detail (the rating system) leaked into the primary UI label instead of being abstracted ('Tu rating' with the math hidden behind a tooltip).
findings.userImpact: First-time/non-technical users see a big number labeled with an opaque algorithm and a cryptic '±350 RD', raising cognitive load and undermining the 5-second comprehension test of the page's primary metric.
findings.recommendation: Relabel the eyebrow to 'Tu rating' (drop 'Glicko-2' into a small info tooltip). Hide the raw '±RD' behind the existing title tooltip pattern or render it only when non-provisional. Keep the human-readable tier badge as the prominent secondary. This is a pure content cut, no layout change.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-debate.ts — heroPanel eyebrow (line 120) and RD line (line 125)

findings.problem: The 'Registrar un debate' action appears in three different button styles for the same action: white-on-navy in the hero (line 146), btn-primary in history header/empty (lines 219,248), and btn-soft 'Registrar resultado de práctica' in practice (line 297) which routes to the SAME openRecordDebate flow but with a different label and a hidden 'Fuente' field (isPractice forces source=OTR). A user cannot tell from the labels that 'Registrar un debate' and 'Registrar resultado de práctica' open essentially the same form.
findings.rootCause: One action got three entry points with three visual weights and two labels, differentiated only by an internal forcedSource flag.
findings.userImpact: Mild recognition burden and inconsistent affordance weighting for the screen's main write action; the hero's white button reads as more important than the identical primary buttons elsewhere.
findings.recommendation: Standardize the primary record action to one button style (btn-primary) and keep the hero variant only if the navy panel requires the inverse treatment. Clarify that practice-registration is the same flow (e.g. a single 'Registrar' with the source preselected) rather than a separately-named action.

findings.problem: The PF practice timer is fragile across navigation. mountPfTimer (line 661) stores its interval on window.__pfTimer and hooks window.__recTeardown, but the active phase/remaining state lives only in mount-scoped closures (idx, remaining, running). Any repaint — including switching sub-tab and coming back, which calls repaint()->render()->mount() (lines 471-477) — destroys and recreates the timer at 0:00. A user who starts a 4-minute Constructive, peeks at the Leaderboard tab, and returns loses their running clock with no warning.
findings.rootCause: Timer state is closure-local and the screen's repaint fully re-renders innerHTML, so there is no persistence of in-progress timer state the way __debateTab persists the active tab.
findings.userImpact: Power users running a real timed practice lose the timer on any tab switch — a silent data-loss-of-context that makes the timer untrustworthy for its actual use case.
findings.recommendation: Persist timer state on window (like __debateTab): store {idx,remaining,running,startedAt} and rehydrate in mountPfTimer so a return to the Práctica tab resumes. Alternatively warn/confirm before leaving while running. Minimal version: keep the interval alive and re-bind to fresh DOM nodes on repaint instead of resetting idx=-1.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-debate.ts — mountPfTimer (line 661-745) closure state vs repaint (line 471)

findings.problem: The coach cards are the PRIMARY interaction on the grid, but each is a plain `<div class="tile click" data-coach="...">` (coachCard, scr-marketplace.ts:189). They have no role, no tabindex, and no keyboard handler. The only global keyboard activator in Aula.tsx (line 960) fires solely for `[role="button"][tabindex]`, so the cards cannot receive focus or be opened by Enter/Space. A keyboard or screen-reader user cannot open any coach profile — the entire marketplace is unusable without a mouse.
findings.rootCause: coachCard renders a clickable div but never marks it as a button; the screen relies on a mouse-only `click` listener (mount, line 512) and the codebase's [A11Y-01] convention (role+tabindex) was not applied here.
findings.userImpact: Keyboard-only and screen-reader users are fully blocked from browsing/opening coaches — the core task of the cluster. AT announces the card as inert text.
findings.recommendation: In coachCard (scr-marketplace.ts:189) add `role="button" tabindex="0" aria-label="Ver perfil de ${esc(c.name)}"` to the `<div data-coach>`. This piggybacks on the existing Aula.tsx Enter/Space handler at line 960 (which calls .click()), so no new JS is needed. Apply the same to the package/day/slot chips if they are `<button>` already they are fine; confirm pkg buttons are `<button>` (they are).

findings.problem: Interactive/important green text uses `--action` (#2CAA20), which is 3.05:1 on white and 2.83:1 on cream — below the 4.5:1 AA threshold for normal text. Affected: the "Ver perfil" link (class="sky" → .sky{color:var(--action)}, scr-marketplace.ts:213), the "Marketplace" eyebrow (.eyebrow{color:var(--action)}, scr-marketplace.ts:243), and every price figure (class="cc-pct" → color:var(--action-hover) #1E8C16 ≈ 3.9:1, screens.css:42). The design system already ships `--otr-green-text` (#176B11 = 6.67:1) created expressly for green text, so this is avoidable drift.
findings.rootCause: .sky, .eyebrow and .cc-pct were defined against the saturated brand green (--action / --action-hover) rather than the AA-safe text token --otr-green-text; the marketplace inherits those classes.
findings.userImpact: Low-vision users and anyone on a bright screen struggle to read the only inline CTA ("Ver perfil"), the section label, and prices — the prices are the single most important data point for a marketplace decision.
findings.recommendation: Repoint the text-only green to the AA token: change `.sky{color:var(--action)}` (app.css:24) to `color:var(--otr-green-text)`, and `.course-card .cc-pct{...color:var(--action-hover)}` (screens.css:42) / the reused .cc-pct figures to `--otr-green-text`. The eyebrow (.eyebrow, app.css:118) should also move to --otr-green-text. Pure-green stays fine for fills/icons; only text must change.

findings.lens: Accessibility (WCAG 1.3.1 Info & Relationships / heading order) / Scanability
findings.problem:
Neither screen exposes a real heading structure. The grid title is `
Coaches
` (renderGrid:244); on the profile the coach name is `` (renderProfile:441) and every section label ("Sobre …", "Reseñas", "Reserva tu sesión", "Credenciales", "Especialidades") is a bold `` (lines 462,469,370,464,465). A screen-reader user gets no heading landmarks to navigate by and no programmatic page title; the visual hierarchy is faked with font-weight only.
findings.rootCause: Screens are HTML-string builders that style text as headings via class/`<b>` instead of semantic `<h1>`/`<h2>` elements.
findings.userImpact: AT users cannot jump between sections (no H-key navigation); the document outline is empty. Also weakens SEO/semantics vs an enterprise bar.
findings.recommendation: Make the grid title an `<h1>` (or `<h1 class="page-title">`) in renderGrid (line 244) and the coach name an `<h1>` in renderProfile (line 441). Promote the card section labels ("Sobre …" 462, "Reseñas" 469, "Reserva tu sesión" 370) to `<h2>`/`<h3>` keeping the existing inline font-size. This is structural markup only — no visual change.

findings.problem: When a coach has no published availability, availRows() synthesizes a generic lun–sáb 9–18 window (line 124). But slotsFor() filters out anything inside the 12h LEAD_MS (line 142,150). On the current day or when the next eligible day's morning is within 12h, the user can land on a selected day whose slots are all filtered, showing "Sin horarios libres este día — prueba con otro" (line 398) while step 1 (package) and step 2 (day) look complete. The confirm button stays disabled with no explanation of WHY, and there is no guidance that this is a fabricated schedule the coach will re-confirm. First-timers can dead-end here believing the coach is unbookable.
findings.rootCause: The generic-availability fallback advertises slots the lead-time rule then removes, and the disabled CTA gives no reason; the empty-slot message doesn't tell the user the schedule is provisional.
findings.userImpact: Users abandon a bookable coach thinking no times exist; the disabled CTA with no hint violates 'help users recover from errors'.
findings.recommendation: In bookingCard: when `generic` is true and `slots.length===0`, replace the terse empty-slot copy (line 398) with an actionable line ("Este coach confirmará el horario contigo — elige otro día o envíale un mensaje") and surface the existing "Enviar mensaje" CTA inline. Additionally, when the confirm button is disabled (ready===false), render a one-line hint under it stating the missing step ("Elige paquete, día y hora para continuar").
findings.file: app/lib/scr-marketplace.ts → bookingCard (slots empty branch line ~398; disabled CTA line ~411) + slotsFor/availRows interaction (lines 122-156)

findings.problem: The filter zone stacks up to 8 specialty chips (renderGrid:239 slice(0,8)) PLUS three dropdowns (idioma / precio / orden, lines 253-269) PLUS the conditional clear button — on a marketplace that, per the data model and demo, holds a small number of coaches. That is a lot of decision surface (Hick's) for a short list, and the spec chips duplicate filtering intent with no search box, which would be the higher-value control.
findings.rootCause: All filter primitives were added up-front rather than scaled to the catalog size; there is no free-text search even though it is the most-expected marketplace affordance (Jakob's).
findings.userImpact: First-time users face choice overload before seeing value; the controls occupy ~2 rows above the fold, pushing cards down. With few coaches, most filters return the same set.
findings.recommendation: Simplify: (1) collapse the three selects into a single ordering control plus an optional filter popover, OR (2) at minimum hide the spec-chip row behind a 'Filtros' toggle until the catalog exceeds ~9 coaches (data-driven: `all.length`). Add a single text search input (name/specialty) as the primary control — it absorbs the spec chips (Tesler's). Keep [COG-07] clear-filters logic.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-marketplace.ts → renderGrid (specs chips line 249-251; selects line 252-269)

findings.lens: Exploratory QA (double-escape) — confirmed extra instance of known bug
findings.problem: The systemic `&` double-escape (known bug #1) has a live instance in this cluster: coach specialty "Lincoln-Douglas & Policy" renders as "Lincoln-Douglas &amp; Policy" in the card specialty badges (coachCard:206) and the profile specialties (renderProfile:465). The values arrive already HTML-escaped from the data layer and esc() escapes the ampersand a second time. Per instructions this is the known systemic bug, flagged here as an additional confirmed surface.
findings.rootCause: Specialty strings are pre-escaped at the source and re-escaped by esc() on interpolation (same class as the leaderboard fix in a062cd5).
findings.userImpact: Users see a literal `&` in coach specialties — a visible correctness defect on a trust-sensitive surface (the marketplace).
findings.recommendation: Fix at the systemic source (un-escape-once or stop double-escaping in the data layer) rather than per-screen; the specialty split/render at coachCard:206 and renderProfile:465 are downstream symptoms. Verify the same fix resolves langBadges and headline.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-marketplace.ts → coachCard specialties (line 206), renderProfile specialties (line 465) — root fix in the data layer (DB.marketplace source)

findings.problem: When a coach has a photo, the avatar is rendered as a CSS background-image on a `<span>` with the initials set to `color:transparent` (coachCard:186). The photo conveys identity but is invisible to assistive tech (background images get no alt), and the fallback initials are deliberately hidden, so AT users get nothing for that coach's visual identity. The same pattern is absent on the profile hero, which uses C.avatar (initials) — inconsistent.
findings.rootCause: Photo support was bolted on as a background-image hack over the initials avatar instead of an ``; no text alternative was provided.
findings.userImpact: Screen-reader users lose the coach's photo identity cue; minor but it is a flat WCAG 1.1.1 miss and a component-drift inconsistency with the rest of the avatar system.
findings.recommendation: Either render coach photos as a real `` inside the avatar, or keep the background but add `role="img" aria-label="${esc(c.name)}"` to the span and do NOT hide the initials from AT. Prefer routing photo support through C.avatar so the avatar component owns the alt contract.

findings.problem: Two minor design-system contrast nits: the discount badge `.badge.ok` (used at bookingCard:384 `-${p.discountPct}%`) is #1E8C16 on #E1F2DE = 3.73:1, under AA 4.5 for its 11.5px bold text; and the `.badge.warn` consent badge text relies on --otr-gold-text which is fine, but the warn alert icon/title path should be spot-checked. The discount badge is the one that fails.
findings.rootCause: badge.ok pairs the dark-green text token with the pale-green tint at a ratio just below AA for small text.
findings.userImpact: The discount percentage (a conversion-relevant figure) is slightly hard to read for low-vision users.
findings.recommendation: Darken `.badge.ok` text to --otr-green-text (#176B11 → 4.6:1 on #E1F2DE) in app.css:202, or increase its font size/weight. Confirm with a contrast check after the change.

findings.problem: The entire Skill Graph attribution feature is keyboard-inoperable. Each skill row is a clickable <div data-lpskill> with style="cursor:pointer" but NO role, NO tabindex, and the mount() handler binds only a 'click' listener (scr-lifetime.ts line 174 + line 428). A keyboard or screen-reader user cannot expand 'Qué lo movió' (the per-skill event history), and the expand/collapse state change is never announced.
findings.rootCause: Clickable-div pattern without keyboard affordances. The codebase already solved this elsewhere — scr-teacher.ts:243 and scr-community.ts:145 attach a keydown Enter/Space handler with role/tabindex on equivalent clickable rows — so this is local drift, not a missing convention.
findings.userImpact: ~15-20% of users (keyboard-only, switch, screen-reader, many older users) cannot reach the single interactive disclosure on the page; the radar's whole 'with history' value proposition is silently gated behind a mouse.
findings.recommendation: In skillRows() add role="button" tabindex="0" aria-expanded="${open}" to the <div data-lpskill> wrapper (and an id on the expanded panel + aria-controls). In mount(), alongside the click listener, add el.addEventListener('keydown', e=>{ if(e.key==='Enter'||e.key===' '){ e.preventDefault(); el.click(); } }) — mirroring scr-teacher.ts:243.

findings.lens: Nielsen (user control / consistency) / Accessibility / Design system
findings.problem: confirmModal() (scr-lifetime.ts line 562) — the downgrade-to-Free confirmation — is not keyboard-accessible and drifts from the house modal pattern: it has role="dialog" but NO aria-modal, NO aria-labelledby, NO Escape-to-close, NO focus trap, and never moves focus into the dialog on open. The destructive 'Cambiar a Free' action sits behind a modal you cannot dismiss or operate with a keyboard.
findings.rootCause: A bespoke modal builder that didn't reuse the cluster's own better pattern. The sibling openReportModal() in scr-parent.ts:654-665 already wires document keydown Escape + scrim-click close; confirmModal omits all of it.
findings.userImpact: Keyboard/AT users hit a focus-less trap on the one destructive action in the cluster; sighted mouse users can still cancel, but the dialog isn't announced as modal and Escape does nothing — fails WCAG 2.1.2 (No Keyboard Trap intent) and 4.1.2.
findings.recommendation: Add aria-modal="true", aria-labelledby pointing at the <h3>, focus the [data-ok] (or Cancelar) button on open, add a document 'keydown' Escape handler that resolves(false) and removes the scrim (clean it up in done()), and restore focus to the triggering button on close. Extract a shared modal helper so confirmModal and scr-parent's openReportModal share one implementation.

findings.problem: The Elite tier card is fully disabled — 'Próximamente', price 'Muy pronto', a locked 'Próximamente' button, and FOUR muted feature bullets ('Todo Pro', 'Créditos de coaching incluidos', 'Acceso temprano a torneos', 'Contenido premium') that promise nothing buyable. It occupies a full third of the pricing grid (tierCards(), eliteCard, line ~544) and competes visually with the only two actionable plans (Free, Pro).
findings.rootCause: A roadmap placeholder shipped as a first-class pricing column. The actual decision is binary (Free vs Pro); Elite adds a third Hick's-law option with zero current action.
findings.userImpact: Users scanning to upgrade burn attention parsing a dead column and four phantom benefits; the 'Recommended' Pro CTA loses prominence by being 1-of-3 instead of 1-of-2. Applying the disappearance test: if Elite vanished tomorrow, no user could miss it (nothing is purchasable).
findings.recommendation: Remove the full Elite card from the g-3 grid; if the roadmap signal matters, collapse it to a single one-line teaser strip below the two real plans ('Elite — créditos de coaching y torneos, próximamente. Avísame'), with no fake price and no feature list. This also lets Free+Pro use a 2-up grid where Pro reads as the clear recommendation.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-lifetime.ts — tierCards() eliteCard (line ~544) and the g-3 wrapper (line ~558)

findings.problem: In skillRows() the score number for skills below 60 is colored var(--warn) = #C8920C on a white card (scr-lifetime.ts line 178). #C8920C on #FFF is ~3.6:1, below the 4.5:1 AA minimum for this small (13.5px) tabular text. tokens.css line 19 itself flags --otr-gold-lo/#C8920C as 'NO texto sobre pálido: 2.4:1' — the same hue family is being used as text here.
findings.rootCause: Reusing the gold/warn accent as a foreground text color on white without checking it as text (it was designed as an icon/fill color).
findings.userImpact: Low-vision users and anyone on a dim/glare screen can't reliably read the very numbers that flag a weak skill — i.e. the most decision-relevant scores are the least legible.
findings.recommendation: Use a darker token for the sub-60 score text — --warn-text / a #7c5614-class value (the codebase uses --otr-gold-text #5A4206 for gold-on-pale; for text-on-white pick something ≥4.5:1 such as #8a5a00 or reuse --text). Keep the warn hue only on the bar/icon, not the readable digits.

findings.lens: Content reduction / Visual hierarchy / Minimalist aesthetic
findings.problem: Plan status is stated three times on one short screen: the hero H2 'Tu plan: OTR Free', the hero 'Plan vigente' badge, and the 'En este plan desde…' line (membershipHero, lines ~488-501), then reinforced again by the disabled 'Tu plan actual' button inside the tier card. The hero also carries a sales sentence ('Tu plan decide cuánto entrenas…') that competes with the tier cards which make the same point concretely.
findings.rootCause: The dark hello-card hero was reused to carry both identity (current plan) and a marketing pitch, duplicating signals the pricing cards already deliver.
findings.userImpact: The first ~5 seconds are spent on restated status instead of the upgrade decision; the visual weight of the dark hero pulls focus away from the actual primary action (the 'Pasar a Pro' CTA below it).
findings.recommendation: Shrink the hero to one line of identity ('Tu plan: OTR Free · desde octubre 2025') and drop either the 'Plan vigente' badge or the prose sentence (the cards sell the value). Reserve the eye-catching dark treatment for the Pro card, not the status hero.

findings.problem: The Public Profile toggle — the one privacy-sensitive, consequential control on the page — is the last card in the right rail, below Performance and Credentials (S.lifetimeProfile.render(), line ~409-412). On mobile the rail stacks last, so 'share my whole profile publicly' lands at the very bottom of a long scroll, after the journey timeline. There is also no in-page anchor or section nav.
findings.rootCause: Rail ordering treats the share/privacy control as a minor footer item rather than a primary account-level decision; the page is one long unsegmented scroll with no jump targets.
findings.userImpact: A student (or parent reviewing a minor's account) who wants to find or verify the public-profile state must scroll past everything; discoverability of the consent control is poor, which is exactly the control that most needs to be findable.
findings.recommendation: Either move publicProfileCard to the top of the right rail (above performance/credentials) so the privacy control is reachable first, or surface a small 'Perfil público: Activo/Privado' status chip in the identityHero with an anchor link to the card. Low effort, high payoff for the consent UX.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-lifetime.ts — S.lifetimeProfile.render() rail order (line ~409) and identityHero() (line ~91)

findings.problem: In tierCards() the optimistic in-flight states are inconsistent and lossy. The downgrade/upgrade buttons set btn.textContent = 'Actualizando…' (mount(), line ~611), which on the Pro button overwrites the IC.flame icon markup and on failure is restored as plain text 'Pasar a Pro' / 'Cambiar a Free' WITHOUT the icon (lines ~622). The styled white Pro button and the disabled 'Tu plan actual' state also diverge in markup, so a failed-then-retried plan switch silently degrades the button's appearance.
findings.rootCause: textContent mutation on buttons whose label originally contained icon HTML; the restore path rebuilds only the text, not the original innerHTML.
findings.userImpact: Minor visual regression after a failed plan change (icon disappears); not a blocker, but it's the kind of polish a Stripe/Linear pricing page would not ship. No status spinner/aria-busy means AT users get no in-flight feedback either.
findings.recommendation: Instead of mutating textContent, capture btn.innerHTML before the call and restore it on error; set aria-busy="true" during the request; or simpler, disable the button + rely on repaint() (which already rebuilds from DB) and skip the textContent dance entirely.

findings.problem: Section titles across the page are <b style="font-size:15px"> not real headings (skillGraphCard line ~199, performanceCard ~265, credentialsCard ~292, journeyCard ~339, publicProfileCard ~378). The page has a single <h2> in identityHero and then no heading structure — every card title is bold text. The empty-state cards do use <h4>, so the heading hierarchy is also internally inconsistent (h4 inside an empty card, but <b> for the populated equivalent).
findings.rootCause: Visual styling chosen over semantic markup for card titles; the 'eyebrow + bold title' lockup was built with <div>/<b> rather than <h3>.
findings.userImpact: Screen-reader users get no heading outline to navigate this long, multi-section profile (no way to jump between Skill Graph, Performance, Journey, etc.); fails WCAG 1.3.1 / 2.4.6 best practice for a content-heavy page.
findings.recommendation:
Promote each card's bold title to

(keep the visual size via class), and make the empty-state titles

too for consistency. Keep the eyebrow as a

/

kicker. Purely additive, no layout change.

findings.problem: The Ledger renders 7 KPI tiles in a 4+3 split (ledgerTiles(), lines ~217-231): Cursos completados, Lecciones terminadas, Debates competidos, Victorias, Sesiones asistidas, Torneos, Horas de coaching. Seven undifferentiated big-number tiles sit between the Skill Graph and the Journey with no grouping logic beyond 'top row / bottom row', and several overlap conceptually (Debates competidos vs Victorias vs Torneos; Lecciones vs Cursos).
findings.rootCause: A flat dump of every available counter, hitting the top of Miller's 7±2 with no prioritization of which numbers actually tell the trajectory story.
findings.userImpact: The stat band reads as a scoreboard wall rather than a narrative; users scan all seven to find the two or three that matter. The behavioral capture already flagged that 4 tall KPI tiles are heavy on mobile — this is 7.
findings.recommendation: Cut or merge to the 4 highest-signal counters (e.g. Cursos, Debates, Victorias, Horas de coaching) and fold the rest into a single 'detalle' line, or label the two rows with a kicker ('Aprendizaje' / 'Competencia') so the grouping carries meaning. Verify the win-rate isn't more useful than raw Victorias.

findings.problem: The participant filter chips (Todos / Estudiantes / Coaches / En riesgo) are bare <span class="chip"> with a click-only handler — no role, no tabindex, no keydown. A keyboard or screen-reader user cannot operate the only mechanism for narrowing the roster. This is doubly jarring because the SAME cluster does it correctly: the teacher screen's 'En riesgo' chip (#tr-risk) has role="button" tabindex="0" plus an Enter/Space keydown handler.
findings.rootCause: In S.participants.render the chips are emitted as plain spans (scr-teacher.ts lines 626-629) and S.participants.mount (lines 675-682) binds only a 'click' listener on #pt-filters, unlike S.teacher which adds tabindex/role and a keydown branch (lines 89, 242-243).
findings.userImpact: Keyboard-only and assistive-tech coaches cannot filter the participant list at all; mouse users are unaffected, masking the defect in casual testing.
findings.recommendation: Mirror the teacher pattern: add role="button" tabindex="0" (and aria-pressed reflecting .active) to each [data-filter] chip in render(), and in mount() add a keydown listener on #pt-filters that triggers the same activeFilter switch on Enter/Space. Better: extract a shared makeChipKeyboardable(el) helper so teacher and participants share one implementation.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-teacher.ts (S.participants.render chips lines 626-629; S.participants.mount filter handler lines 675-682)

findings.lens: Exploratory QA (data honesty) + Nielsen #1 status/match real world
findings.problem: The roster's '7 días' sparkline column does not plot real data. Each row renders one of exactly three hardcoded bar arrays chosen by a single s.trend flag ([40,55,50,68,72,80,88] for up, a mirrored array for down, a flat array otherwise). Two students both flagged 'up' get a byte-identical chart. The column header promises a 7-day activity trend; it delivers a decorative glyph keyed off one boolean.
findings.rootCause: scr-teacher.ts line 101: spark(s.trend==='up'?[...]:s.trend==='down'?[...]:[...]) — the array is literal, not derived from any per-day series on the student record.
findings.userImpact: A coach making 'who is slipping' decisions will read these as real weekly engagement and be misled; identical bars across distinct students erode trust the moment anyone notices.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) feed a real 7-point series from the data layer (e.g. s.trend7:[...]) and plot that, or (b) if no daily data exists, remove the sparkline column entirely and keep the textual Engagement pill + Últ. acceso, which already carry the signal. Given SIMPLIFICATION priority and that the data does not exist, prefer removal: drop the 7 días and the corresponding spark(...), and delete the now-unused spark() helper (line 7).
findings.file: app/lib/scr-teacher.ts (header line 93 col '7 días'; cell line 101; helper line 7)

findings.problem: Course name is interpolated RAW into data attributes in two places: data-name="${c.name}" in the manage card 'Configuración' button (scr-extra.ts line 233) and the courseBuilder hero 'Configuración' button (line 277). A course whose name contains a double-quote breaks the attribute and the surrounding markup; a name containing & participates in the known systemic & class. The same value is correctly escaped elsewhere (participants uses data-name="${esc(s.n)}", scr-teacher.ts lines 605/613/614).
findings.rootCause: Missing esc() wrapper on c.name in the data-name attribute at scr-extra.ts:233 and :277; the value flows into openEditCourse(id, name) (Aula.tsx:649) as the prefilled 'Nombre del curso' field, so a broken/escaped value also corrupts the edit form prefill.
findings.userImpact: Any coach who names a course with a quote or ampersand (common: 'Oratoria & Speaking' already exists in the catalog per the capture) gets a corrupted edit button and a mangled prefill; worst case the row's action buttons stop working.
findings.recommendation: Wrap both occurrences: data-name="${esc(c.name)}". Confirm openEditCourse receives the unescaped logical name (it reads from DB.teacherCourses by id at Aula.tsx:651, so the data-name is only a fallback — escaping it is safe and correct).
findings.file: app/lib/scr-extra.ts (S.manage card line 233; S.courseBuilder hero line 277)

findings.problem: The teacher screen stacks THREE distinct full surfaces on one route: (1) group tracking (4 KPIs + roster table + risk rail + pending-grading card), then (2) a SECOND four-KPI strip (Cursos/Módulos/Lecciones/Exámenes) plus (3) a read-only course tree that duplicates what 'manage' / courseBuilder already own. That is 8 KPI tiles and two page-heads on a single scroll. The course tree here is non-editable (its only real actions are quiz/video, which also live in the builder), so it is largely a redundant preview of the manage cluster.
findings.rootCause: S.teacher.render appends this.managePanel(...) (scr-teacher.ts line 132); managePanel (lines 136-200) re-derives course/module/lesson/quiz counts and re-renders the whole tree that S.manage + S.courseBuilder already provide.
findings.userImpact: First-time coaches face a long, two-identity page ('Seguimiento del grupo' AND 'Estructura del curso') and must scroll past tracking to reach content management that is ALSO reachable from the nav — violating recognition-over-recall and inflating cognitive load for no added capability.
findings.recommendation: Remove the managePanel block from S.teacher entirely and keep teacher as a pure tracking dashboard. Move the 4 content-count KPIs (Cursos/Módulos/Lecciones/Exámenes) to the top of S.manage where they belong. The 'Editor completo' and 'Recurso (archivo)' buttons should live in manage's header. This eliminates ~65 lines and one of the two competing page identities.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-teacher.ts (S.teacher.render line 132 call; S.teacher.managePanel lines 136-200) → relocate KPIs to app/lib/scr-extra.ts S.manage.render

findings.problem: The courseBuilder hero crams six controls into one row: back-link 'Mis cursos', save-chip, 'Modo edición: ON/OFF', 'Publicar curso / Pasar a borrador', 'Configuración', and 'Vista previa (como alumno)'. The primary objective of this screen — build the course (add sections/activities) — has NO primary button in the hero; the real primary action ('Añadir actividad') is buried per-section below. The 5-second test fails: the eye lands on a wall of equally-weighted ghost/soft buttons.
findings.rootCause: scr-extra.ts S.courseBuilder hero (lines 273-279) gives every control btn-sm with btn-ghost/btn-soft weights and no grouping; the edit-mode toggle, publish toggle and config all compete. 'Publicar' and 'Configuración' overlap (publish state is ALSO settable inside Configuración via the 'Estado' field, Aula.tsx:661-662) — two paths to the same setting.
findings.userImpact: Power users adapt, but a non-technical coach scanning the hero cannot tell what the screen is for or which button advances them; the duplicated publish path invites confusion about which is canonical.
findings.recommendation: Demote and group: keep ONE prominent primary in the hero ('+ Añadir sección' when sections exist, promoting the bottom action upward), collapse 'Configuración' + 'Vista previa' into a quiet overflow/secondary cluster, and drop the standalone publish toggle (it is redundant with Configuración → Estado) OR remove Estado from the Configuración modal and keep only the hero toggle — pick one publish surface, not two.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-extra.ts (S.courseBuilder hero lines 273-279); de-dup publish vs app/components/Aula.tsx openEditCourse line 661-662

findings.problem:
None of the four screens emit a real top-level heading. The visible page title is
(a styled div, app.css:116), and the eyebrow above it is a

. Inside the page, cards jump straight to

/

(e.g. 'Requieren atención' h3, empty-state h4) with no

/

ancestor. Screen-reader heading navigation (a primary AT shortcut) finds no document title and a broken outline; the teacher screen additionally has TWO 'page-title' divs (Seguimiento del grupo AND Estructura del curso) so even visually there is no single H1.
findings.rootCause: page-head markup uses <div class="page-title"> across the cluster (scr-teacher.ts lines 69, 183; scr-extra.ts lines 215, 263) rather than a semantic heading element; the cluster has no <h1> at all.
findings.userImpact: Screen-reader users lose the single most-used navigation affordance (jump-to-heading) and cannot orient; fails WCAG 1.3.1 and the kind of audit Stripe/Linear pass before shipping.
findings.recommendation: Promote the per-screen page-title div to a real <h1> (keep the class for styling), and ensure exactly one per screen. Verify [A11Y-*] markers elsewhere actually rely on a heading being present. For the teacher screen, after COACH-04 removes the second page-head, only one title remains — resolving the dual-H1 issue automatically.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-teacher.ts (lines 69, 183) and app/lib/scr-extra.ts (lines 215, 263); style hook app/styles/app.css:116

findings.problem: Each participant row carries three competing action affordances of three different visual weights: 'Adjudicar' (btn-soft), 'Evaluar' (btn-ghost) and a 30x30 icon-only message button. The icon button is below a comfortable touch target and its only label is a title tooltip (invisible to touch/AT). Adjudicar (rubric → moves Glicko rating) and Evaluar (eval-skills) are both heavyweight authoring actions surfaced at equal prominence on every row, creating per-row choice overload across the whole roster.
findings.rootCause: scr-teacher.ts studentRow (lines 612-616) renders all three buttons inline per row; the message button is icon-only at 30x30 (style="width:30px;height:30px") with no visible/accessible name beyond title.
findings.userImpact: On a long roster the row becomes a dense control strip; touch and AT users get an unlabeled 30px message target; the visual noise slows scanning of the actual data (name/level/XP).
findings.recommendation: Collapse the two rare authoring actions into a single row overflow menu ('•••' → Adjudicar ronda / Evaluar skills), keeping at most one inline primary if any. At minimum give the message icon-button an aria-label and bump it to >=36px to match --ctrl-h. Apply the same overflow pattern consistently across roster tables.

findings.problem: scr-teacher.ts line 8 defines trendIc — a tangled one-liner (it even contains a no-op `.replace('chevD','') && ...` short-circuit) — that is never called anywhere in the cluster. It is pure dead code / visual debt sitting at the top of the file.
findings.rootCause: trendIc (line 8) was superseded by the inline eng-pill + spark rendering and never removed; grep confirms zero call sites.
findings.userImpact: No user-facing impact, but it is confusing maintenance debt and the convoluted expression invites a future copy-paste bug.
findings.recommendation: Delete line 8 (trendIc). If COACH-02 is accepted, also delete the spark helper (line 7) in the same pass.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-teacher.ts (line 8; and line 7 if COACH-02 lands)

findings.problem: The third KPI tile is labeled 'Engagement promedio' but is fed k.onTime, and the source comment (lines 79-80) admits the value derives from Enrollment.engagement buckets (Alto/Medio/Bajo→%), NOT from on-time-vs-dueAt. The variable name onTime contradicts the visible label, and a coach could reasonably read 'Engagement %' as a punctuality/on-time-submission metric.
findings.rootCause: DB.teacherKpis.onTime is reused for an engagement metric; the tile label was corrected to 'Engagement promedio' but the underlying key/name still says onTime, leaving an internal contradiction documented inline at scr-teacher.ts:79-80.
findings.userImpact: Low day-to-day, but the label/metric mismatch is a latent misread and a maintenance trap (the next dev wires onTime to a punctuality feature and silently breaks the engagement tile).
findings.recommendation: Rename the data key to k.engagement (data layer) so label and field agree, OR add a one-line tooltip/subcaption on the tile clarifying it is the Alto/Medio/Bajo engagement average. Keep the visible label 'Engagement promedio'.

findings.problem: The empty-state CTA reads "Ver mi perfil en el marketplace" but its handler is data-go="explore", which routes to the marketplace BROWSE list of all coaches (screens.ts: explore → screen 'marketplace', crumbs ['Marketplace','Coaches']), not the coach's own profile. The coach is promised a view of their own listing and instead lands on the generic shopping list. Note the very next empty state (viewAvailability) does this correctly with data-go="coach" (the own-profile editor).
findings.rootCause: Wrong route target: the button points at the consumer marketplace route instead of the supply-side own-profile route 'coach'.
findings.userImpact: A brand-new coach with zero bookings clicks the one CTA offered, expecting to see/check their public listing, and is dumped into a list of competitors — confusing and a dead end for the intended task.
findings.recommendation: In scr-coachwork.ts viewAgenda() empty state, change data-go="explore" to data-go="coach" (matching viewAvailability's empty-state CTA). Optionally keep wording as "Editar mi perfil de coach" to match the 'coach' route's actual content.

findings.lens: Design system / Visual hierarchy / Mobile cognitive walkthrough
findings.problem: The PENDING status is rendered as a full sentence inside a pill badge: …Esperando consentimiento del padre. .badge is height:22px, font-weight:700, border-radius:pill (app.css:197) — it is built for 1-2 word states (Confirmada, Completada). A ~30-char string in a fixed-height pill either overflows, wraps into a lumpy multi-line pill, or pushes the row layout, especially in the wrap row on mobile. It also out-weights the actual status it sits next to.
findings.rootCause: A status badge component is being overloaded to carry an explanatory sentence; the explanation belongs in the row subtext, not the pill.
findings.userImpact: On mobile the agenda row (already wrap with avatar + name + slot + badge + action) becomes visually noisy and the pill renders awkwardly; the long heavy label competes with the 'Rechazar reserva' action for attention.
findings.recommendation: In statusBadge() set the PENDING pill to a short label (e.g. "Pendiente") and move "Esperando consentimiento del padre" into the faint subtext line of bookingRow() (the line that already shows slot · pkg · amount), or render it as a quiet inline note under the row. Keep the pill to ≤2 words like every other state.

findings.problem: The 'Añadir franja' control is three bare
findings.rootCause: Labels were conveyed purely by visual layout/order; no accessible name on any of the three selects.
findings.userImpact: Keyboard/AT users cannot tell which select is day vs start vs end; this is the screen's only data-entry form, so the failure is on the core supply-side task.
findings.recommendation: Add aria-label to each select in viewAvailability() ('Día de la semana', 'Hora de inicio', 'Hora de fin') or wrap each in a <label>. Make the 'a' separator aria-hidden. This is a localized, low-risk fix.

findings.problem: addBtn handler only validates endMin > startMin. It does not check the new franja against existing ones, so a coach can add a duplicate or overlapping window (e.g. two Lunes 4–6 PM, or 4–6 plus 5–7). The list then shows redundant/overlapping rows and the marketplace booking surface inherits the ambiguity.
findings.rootCause: Client-side validation is minimal; overlap/duplicate detection (a system-absorbable check, Tesler) was not implemented.
findings.userImpact: Coaches silently create messy, conflicting availability; the only recovery is to spot it and delete one — extra cognitive load on a non-technical user.
findings.recommendation: In the #cw-av-add handler in scr-coachwork.ts, before calling the API, scan p.availability for same-weekday overlap (startMin < existing.endMin && endMin > existing.startMin) and toast a warn ('Esa franja se solapa con una existente') instead of posting. Mirror this server-side if not already enforced.

findings.problem: The internal tab bar (subTabs) renders plain <button class="tab"> with only data-cw-tab and a CSS .active class. There is no role="tablist"/role="tab", no aria-selected, no aria-controls pointing at #cw-body, and no roving tabindex. The active tab is communicated to AT only by color (.tab.active → --action-hover), which is also a contrast-only signal. Same drift exists in scr-debate.ts (shared pattern).
findings.rootCause: The window.__x tab pattern was copied from scr-debate without ARIA semantics.
findings.userImpact: Screen-reader users get an unlabeled button group with no indication of which view is active or that switching swaps the panel content; keyboard arrow navigation between tabs is unavailable.
findings.recommendation: In subTabs(), add role="tablist" on the .tabs container and role="tab" + aria-selected="true|false" + aria-controls="cw-body" on each button; give #cw-body role="tabpanel". Since scr-debate shares the exact pattern, consider a small shared helper in components.ts to fix both centrally.

findings.lens: Status visibility / Jakob's convention / Exploratory QA
findings.problem: Tab state lives in a global window.__cwTab that is never reset on screen entry and is not reflected in the URL/route. If a coach opens 'Ingresos', navigates away (window.go elsewhere), then returns to coachwork via nav, the screen silently reopens on Ingresos rather than the default Agenda — there is no breadcrumb/URL cue explaining why. The global is also a collision risk shared across the SPA lifetime.
findings.rootCause: Local view state stored on window with no lifecycle reset and no URL sync; render() reads activeTab() which trusts the stale global.
findings.userImpact: Re-entry lands on an unexpected tab with no status cue (breaks recognition>recall and the convention that a screen reopens at its top); minor but disorienting, and not shareable/bookmarkable.
findings.recommendation: Either reset window.__cwTab to 'agenda' when the coachwork screen is (re)mounted from the nav, or persist the tab in the route/hash so the header/crumbs reflect it. Lowest-effort: in S.coachwork.mount, if entering via a fresh navigation (not an internal repaint), default the tab.

findings.problem: The escrow/take-rate message is repeated three+ times: (a) Ingresos tile sub 'se libera al completar' + 'después del take rate 18%', (b) the Ingresos info alert 'OTR retiene 18%… se libera vía escrow cuando marcas la sesión como completada', and (c) the Disponibilidad info alert 'Sesiones y pagos siempre dentro de OTR — los fondos quedan en escrow y se liberan al completar cada sesión'. The Disponibilidad escrow alert is content unrelated to managing time slots/packages and is pure reassurance noise on that tab.
findings.rootCause: Reassurance copy was duplicated per tab rather than stated once where the money lives.
findings.userImpact: Adds vertical length and repetition; the Disponibilidad alert in particular dilutes the tab's actual job (edit franjas/packages).
findings.recommendation: Remove the escrow info alert from viewAvailability() (it belongs in Ingresos), and de-duplicate: keep the single Ingresos 'Transparencia total' alert and drop the redundant take-rate restatement in the payout tile sub, or vice-versa. Target the noise, not the one canonical explanation.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-coachwork.ts › viewAvailability() escrow alert (lines ~375-377) + viewEarnings() tiles/alert (lines ~254-262)

findings.problem: The same student renders with two different avatar identity colors depending on tab: Agenda rows force bg: var(--otr-navy) (black) on the avatar (bookingRow line ~186), while the Ingresos history table uses C.avatar with no bg, falling back to the default --otr-sky-lo green (avatar CSS line 211). A student seen as a black avatar in Agenda becomes a green avatar in Ingresos.
findings.rootCause: One call site overrides the avatar background and the other does not; no shared rule for booking-student avatar color.
findings.userImpact: Subtle but real visual inconsistency; identity color should be stable for the same person across the screen (Linear/Notion would keep it constant).
findings.recommendation: Pick one avatar background for booking-student avatars and use it in both places — either drop the bg override in bookingRow() to match the table default, or add bg: var(--otr-navy) to the C.avatar call in viewEarnings()'s table row.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-coachwork.ts › bookingRow() avatar (line ~186) + viewEarnings() table avatar (line ~271)

findings.problem: The right rail stacks SIX cards (Seguridad y consentimiento, Mensajes de coaches, Membresía y facturación, Reporte mensual, Vincular otro estudiante) plus three SEPARATE per-child control lists scattered across cards: skill/attendance in the main child cards, the per-child approval-threshold selects inside the Membership card, and the per-child public-profile toggles inside the Security card. A parent who wants to adjust 'what my child can do' must hunt across three different cards. This blows past Miller's 7+-2 for a single scannable surface and there is no single 'per-child controls' home.
findings.rootCause: Controls are grouped by OTR's internal domain (billing, security, profile) rather than by the parent's mental model (per child). scr-parent.ts builds three independent kids.filter(k=>k.id).map(...) lists in three different card builders (childCard, membershipCard's thresholds at lines 330-340, and the public-profile block at lines 442-448).
findings.userImpact: A non-technical parent (the exact target audience) cannot form a mental model of where to manage a given child; approval threshold, public profile and progress for the same child live in three visually distant cards. Decisions that belong together are fragmented.
findings.recommendation: Consolidate per-child controls. Move the per-child approval-threshold select (membershipCard) and the per-child public-profile toggle (security card) into a single collapsible 'Controles de {child}' section inside each childCard in scr-parent.ts, leaving the Membership card for the plan/billing only and the Security card for the static reassurance bullets only. This removes two whole per-child lists from the rail and gives each child one self-contained control surface.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-parent.ts (childCard L124-186; membershipCard thresholds L319-354; public-profile block L437-449)

findings.problem: Form controls are not programmatically labeled. (1) The per-child approval-threshold <select data-threshold-child> (L338) has NO <label> and NO aria-label; its only identification is the adjacent visible child-name <span>, which is not associated. A screen-reader user hears 'combo box, Aprobar cada reserva' with no idea which child it governs. (2) The link-email <input id=gp-email> uses <label class=label> with no for= and the input has no id-matched label or aria-label (L191-192).
