(3) The report child <select id=pr-child> has the same unassociated <label> (L226-227).
findings.rootCause: Labels are rendered as visually-adjacent <label class="label"> blocks without a for= attribute, and the threshold select has no label element at all because its name lives in a sibling span. esc()/markup correctness was prioritized over the label-for-control association.
findings.userImpact: Screen-reader and voice-control users cannot reliably identify or target these controls; the threshold select in particular is unusable since the governed child is invisible to AT. Fails WCAG 2.1 AA 1.3.1, 3.3.2, 4.1.2.
findings.recommendation: In scr-parent.ts add aria-label to the threshold select e.g. aria-label="Umbral de aprobación para ${esc(k.name)}" (L338); add aria-label to the public-profile toggle similarly; and either add a for=/id pair or wrap the input in its <label> for #gp-email (linkForm L191-192) and #pr-child (reportCard L226-227).
findings.file: app/lib/scr-parent.ts (threshold select L338; linkForm L188-196; reportCard L224-230)

findings.problem: The monthly-report modal (openReportModal, L643-668) sets role="dialog" but is missing aria-modal="true" and any accessible name (no aria-label/aria-labelledby pointing at the report title). It also performs no focus management: focus is never moved into the dialog on open, there is no focus trap, and focus is not returned to the #pr-open trigger on close. Background page content is not inert, so Tab leaks behind the scrim.
findings.rootCause: The modal is hand-built as a string injected into a div with no focus lifecycle. This is a house-wide pattern (same gap in scr-lifetime.ts L566, scr-debate.ts L627, scr-teacher.ts L265), so it is systemic visual/a11y debt rather than parent-specific, but it still fails here.
findings.userImpact: Keyboard and screen-reader users opening a child's report land with focus stranded on the page behind the dialog, can tab out of the dialog into hidden content, and on close are dumped at document top instead of back at the button. AA failure (4.1.2 name, 2.4.3 focus order).
findings.recommendation: Add aria-modal="true" and aria-labelledby tied to the report title (give the <b> title in reportBody an id and reference it). On open, store document.activeElement, call modal.focus() (add tabindex="-1" to .modal), trap Tab within the scrim, and restore focus on close(). Best done as a shared modal helper reused by all the scr-*.ts string modals.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-parent.ts openReportModal L643-668 (+ reportBody title L266)

findings.problem: Two selects on the same screen use different base classes and sizing. The report child selector uses class="input" (L227) while the per-child threshold selector uses class="select" (L338) and additionally overrides the design-system control height by setting style="...padding:5px 26px 5px 10px", which drops the standard --ctrl-h (36px) height and the .select dropdown-chevron background. The result is one full-width 36px control and one ad-hoc shrunk control in the same view.
findings.rootCause: Inline style overrides and inconsistent class choice (input vs select) instead of using a single select component. The .input/.select rule in app.css L153-164 already defines height:var(--ctrl-h) and the chevron; the inline padding override on L338 defeats both.
findings.userImpact: Visual inconsistency that reads as unpolished against an otherwise mature design language; the threshold select also loses the chevron affordance, hurting discoverability that it is a dropdown. Would not pass a Stripe/Linear visual review.
findings.recommendation: Standardize on class="select" for both dropdowns in scr-parent.ts; drop the inline padding override on the threshold select (L338) and instead let it size via the design-system rule, using a width modifier class if a narrower control is needed rather than killing the height.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-parent.ts (threshold select L338; report select L227)

findings.problem: The threshold-change handler (L577-605) shows a success toast but provides no inline confirmation on the control itself, AND on failure it does not revert the <select> to its previous value — the dropdown keeps displaying the option the user picked even though the backend rejected it and local state was never updated. The parent is left believing 'Auto hasta $50' is active when it is not.
findings.rootCause: The change handler disables/re-enables the select and toasts on error (L599-603) but never resets sel.value back to the prior thresholdValueFor(child) on the catch path; the visible selected option diverges from the actual saved state.
findings.userImpact: A parent can think they raised/lowered the auto-approval ceiling for a child's spending when the change silently failed — a money/safety control showing a false state. This is the highest-stakes control on the screen.
findings.recommendation: In the catch block of the data-threshold-child handler (scr-parent.ts L599-601) capture the pre-change value before the await and restore sel.value to it on failure, or call repaint() so the select re-derives from thresholdValueFor(child). A toast alone is insufficient for a financial control.

findings.problem: The page carries heavy redundant reassurance copy. The hero sub 'El progreso real de tu hijo/a, cada sesión segura y cada peso bajo tu control' (L392), the Security card's three bullets (L431-435), the pending-approvals subtitle 'Ninguna sesión con coach se confirma sin tu aprobación' (L419), the Messages card subtitle, and the Membership threshold helper all re-state the same 'everything is safe / you approve everything / payments in escrow' message three to four times across the screen.
findings.rootCause: Each card was written to stand alone with its own trust paragraph, so the 'proof and peace of mind' tone is repeated rather than stated once. Classify: hero sub = Useful; security bullets = Useful (concrete); pending-approvals subtitle, messages subtitle reassurance, threshold helper = Optional/Noise duplication.
findings.userImpact: The duplicated reassurance dilutes scanability and pushes the actionable controls (approvals, thresholds, report) further down. A returning power-parent re-reads the same promise on every visit.
findings.recommendation: Cut the pending-approvals subtitle (L419) since the Security card already states the same guarantee; trim the threshold helper (L351) to one short line; keep the Security bullets as the single source of the trust message. Target ~40-50% reduction of the reassurance copy across the screen.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-parent.ts (L419 subtitle; L351 helper; hero sub L392)

findings.problem: There are two ways to add a child and they are far apart and asymmetric: the empty state embeds the full link form (L363), but once children exist the link form only lives at the very BOTTOM of the right rail as the 6th card 'Vincular otro estudiante' (L458-461) — below membership and the report. A parent who just linked one child and wants to add a sibling must scroll past everything to find it.
findings.rootCause: linkForm() is placed last in the rail stack rather than near the top-level 'Hijos vinculados' KPI or next to the child cards. Placement is by build order, not by task frequency.
findings.userImpact: Adding a second/third child (a core onboarding-completion action) is buried, hurting discoverability of a primary task. New parents with multiple kids are most affected.
findings.recommendation: Promote the 'Vincular otro estudiante' affordance: either move the link card to the top of the right rail, or add a compact 'Vincular hijo/a' button into the page-head / next to the 'Hijos vinculados' KPI in scr-parent.ts (head L388-393 / KPI row L406-411) that scrolls to or opens the form.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-parent.ts (rail link card L458-461; head L388-393)

findings.problem: The per-session 'Cancelar' button inside Próximas sesiones (childCard L181) uses a two-tap arm pattern with a 4s timeout (mount L516-528), but the destructive consequence is not explained: tapping once changes the label to '¿Cancelar? Tocar de nuevo' with no mention that cancellation may affect payment/escrow or notify the coach. Meanwhile the high-value Reject-approval button (consentRow L119) has NO confirmation at all.
findings.rootCause: Two different destructive actions use two different confirmation models (2-tap for cancel, none for reject) with no consequence copy on either.
findings.userImpact: Inconsistent destructive-action handling: a parent can reject a booking with a single misclick, while cancelling a confirmed session is guarded — the inverse of the actual stakes is plausible. Elderly/first-time users get no information about what cancelling does to their money.
findings.recommendation: Align the two flows in scr-parent.ts: add a one-line consequence to the armed cancel state (e.g. '¿Cancelar? El pago se reembolsa') and apply the same lightweight 2-tap (or a small confirm) to the Reject button in consentRow/its handler so both destructive actions are consistent and explained.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-parent.ts (cancel handler L516-546; consentRow reject L119 + handler L487-511)

findings.lens: Content reduction / Match real world (Nielsen) / Hick's
findings.problem: The role <select> offers FIVE options including BOTH 'Profesor / Coach' (TEACHER) and 'Coach' (COACH) as separate, distinct choices. The whole app treats them as the same thing: isCoachRole(r)=>r==='TEACHER'||r==='COACH', the coach KPI counts TEACHER+COACH together, the 'Coaches' filter chip maps to both, and the backend (route.ts L60) merges them. An admin is forced to choose between two options that mean the same role with no stated difference — a textbook ambiguous/duplicate choice.
findings.rootCause: ROLE_OPTS in scr-admin-users.ts (L26-32) exposes the raw DB enum, which carries a legacy COACH value alongside the canonical TEACHER. The UI surfaces internal data-model drift as a user-facing decision.
findings.userImpact: Admin cannot predict which value to pick; picking COACH vs TEACHER silently changes the stored role with no visible difference, risking inconsistent data (some coaches stored as COACH, some as TEACHER) that downstream filters then have to paper over.
findings.recommendation: Remove the 'COACH' option from ROLE_OPTS so the picker only offers STUDENT / Familia / Profesor (Coach) / Administrador (4 options). Keep COACH in ROLE_LABEL only so existing COACH rows still render a badge correctly. Normalize on TEACHER as the single coach role the UI can assign. File: app/lib/scr-admin-users.ts symbol ROLE_OPTS.

findings.problem: The role filter chips are clickable <span class="chip" data-au-role> with no role="button", no tabindex, and no keydown handler — they are completely keyboard-inoperable. This is the ONLY chip-as-filter in the app that breaks the established pattern: marketplace uses <button class="chip"> (scr-marketplace.ts L250/392/397) and scr-teacher.ts uses <span class="chip" role="button" tabindex="0"> (L89). The same component drift hides the entire role-filter feature from keyboard and screen-reader users.
findings.rootCause: viewBody/render in scr-admin-users.ts builds chips as bare <span> (L134) and the mount handler binds a click listener only (L225-226), with no keyboard affordance, diverging from the two existing in-repo conventions.
findings.userImpact: Keyboard-only and screen-reader admins cannot filter the user list by role at all; WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) and 4.1.2 (Name/Role/Value) failures.
findings.recommendation: Make the chips <button class="chip"> (matches marketplace) or add role="button" tabindex="0" + an Enter/Space keydown handler (matches scr-teacher). Prefer <button> for least code. File: app/lib/scr-admin-users.ts render() chips template (L133-134) + the [data-au-role] handler (L225-226).

findings.lens: Cognitive walkthrough / Match real world / Recognition>recall
findings.problem: A report card identifies its target only by a raw opaque ID rendered as a chip: ${r.targetId} (reportCard L63). For targetType 'user'/'coach'/'message'/'booking' the moderator sees e.g. 'ckp9f2a0x0001' with no name, no link, no preview. To decide whether to suspend a user the admin must recognize a human, but the screen only offers a database key to recall.
findings.rootCause: reportCard renders r.targetId verbatim instead of a resolved label (targetName) and provides no navigation to the reported entity. The data layer likely already has reporterName, so a targetName/targetLabel is the missing piece.
findings.userImpact: Moderation decisions (including the destructive 'Suspender al usuario') are made blind — the admin cannot tell WHO or WHAT is being actioned, which is unsafe for a one-tap suspend and makes the queue nearly unusable for real triage.
findings.recommendation: Replace the raw targetId chip with a human label: show targetName when present and demote the raw id to a faint monospace sub-line (or drop it). Add a 'Ver' link that navigates to the target (window.go) where a route exists (user profile / coach profile / booking). If the API does not yet return targetName, add it server-side; otherwise gate the suspend button until a name is shown. File: app/lib/scr-admin.ts symbol reportCard (L54-88).

findings.lens: Error prevention / User control & undo / Accessibility
findings.problem: The destructive 'Suspender al usuario' uses an inline two-tap arm pattern: first tap rewrites the same button's text to '¿Confirmar suspensión? Tocar de nuevo' and a 4s timeout reverts it (suspendUser L215-239). There is no aria-live region, so a screen-reader user never hears that the button is now armed or that it disarmed; the confirmation is purely visual. Compounding this, the very long confirm string in a small btn-sm with no min-width causes the button to reflow/jump, shifting the card layout.
findings.rootCause: Confirmation is implemented by mutating btn.textContent with no aria-live announcement and no fixed button width; the timeout-based disarm is also invisible to AT.
findings.userImpact: Screen-reader admins get no feedback that a destructive action is one tap from firing (or that it disarmed), and sighted users see a layout jump that can cause mis-clicks on an irreversible suspension.
findings.recommendation: Announce arm/disarm via an aria-live="polite" status node (or set aria-pressed + a visually-hidden live message), and give the suspend button a fixed min-width so the confirm text doesn't reflow the row. Preferred: use the existing toast()/confirm convention instead of in-button text. File: app/lib/scr-admin.ts symbol suspendUser (L215-239).

findings.problem: On the admin's OWN row the role <select> still lists non-admin options and the Suspender button is fully enabled, but the backend rejects both (route.ts L94 'No puedes quitarte tu propio rol' and L99 'No puedes suspenderte a ti mismo'). The UI invites an action it knows will fail, surfacing the guard only as a red error toast after the round-trip.
findings.rootCause: userCard (scr-admin-users.ts L47-88) has no concept of 'this is me' — it renders identical controls for every row regardless of whether u.id === current admin's id.
findings.userImpact: Admin attempts a legitimate-looking action (demote/suspend self) and gets a cryptic failure toast instead of the control being disabled or absent — error prevention failure (Nielsen #5), and it normalizes red toasts so real errors get ignored.
findings.recommendation: Pass the current admin id into render (state likely has it), and in userCard disable+title the role <select> options other than ADMIN and disable the Suspender button when u.id === me, with a small 'Tú' badge for orientation. File: app/lib/scr-admin-users.ts symbol userCard (L47-88) + render() to thread the self id.

findings.lens: Flexibility & efficiency / User control / Cognitive walkthrough
findings.problem: Search requires an explicit click on 'Buscar' (or Enter) and there is NO way to clear it: once st.q is set, the input keeps the term and the only reset is manually deleting the text and searching again on an empty string. The empty-results state ('Sin resultados / Ningún usuario coincide') gives no 'Limpiar búsqueda' affordance (viewBody L104-111). Combined with role chips, an admin can land in a filtered+searched dead-end with no one-tap escape.
findings.rootCause: render()/mount in scr-admin-users.ts implement search as input+button only, with no clear button and no reset action in the no-results empty state.
findings.userImpact: Power admins lose time; non-technical admins can get 'stuck' on an empty list and conclude there are no users, not realizing a stale filter/search is active.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'Limpiar' (x) button in the search row that resets q (and optionally role) and reloads, and add a 'Limpiar búsqueda' button inside the no-results empty state. File: app/lib/scr-admin-users.ts render() search row (L155-161) + viewBody empty branch (L104-111).

findings.problem: None of the interactive controls have programmatic labels. The per-row role (L157) relies on placeholder only with no
findings.rootCause: Controls are emitted as bare elements in userCard/render with no aria-label or label association, unlike the [A11Y-*] markers present elsewhere in the codebase.
findings.userImpact: Screen-reader admins cannot reliably tell which user a 'Suspender'/'Verificar coach'/role control belongs to, and the search field announces no accessible name beyond placeholder (which some AT ignore). WCAG 1.3.1 / 4.1.2 / 3.3.2.
findings.recommendation: Add aria-label to each control including the user name, e.g. role select aria-label=`Rol de ${u.name}`, suspend aria-label=`Suspender a ${u.name}`, verify aria-label=`Verificar a ${u.name}`; give #au-search an aria-label 'Buscar usuarios' (or a visually-hidden <label for>). File: app/lib/scr-admin-users.ts symbol userCard (L51-62) + search input (L157).

findings.problem: Role change, coach verification, and suspension all fire immediately on change/click with only a success toast and no undo. A role <select> in particular is a single accidental change away from, e.g., promoting a student to Administrador, with no confirmation and no one-tap revert. The destructive 'Suspender al usuario' in adminConsole DOES get a two-tap confirm (ADM-04), so the two admin screens are inconsistent about how dangerous mutations are guarded.
findings.rootCause: patch() in scr-admin-users.ts (L235-258) applies all mutations optimistically with toast-only feedback and no confirm/undo, while scr-admin.ts guards its destructive action — inconsistent treatment of comparable risk.
findings.userImpact: An accidental role change (especially to/from ADMIN) is silent and irreversible from the UI; the admin must manually set the old value back, and may not notice the misclick at all.
findings.recommendation: At minimum, add a confirm step for role changes that target or leave ADMIN (the highest-blast-radius transition); ideally add an 'Deshacer' action to the success toast that re-PATCHes the prior value. Align the guard model with adminConsole's two-tap pattern. File: app/lib/scr-admin-users.ts symbol patch + the [data-user-role] change handler (L260-266).

findings.problem: The moderation queue has no way to filter or distinguish OPEN vs REVIEWED vs by target type — every report renders as an equal-weight card in one flat list, and once 'Marcar revisado' is tapped the card stays in the list (only DISMISSED leaves). The header KPI says 'Reportes abiertos' but the list mixes open and reviewed with no segmentation, so a busy queue forces the admin to scan every card to find the actionable (OPEN) ones. Stripe/Linear-grade moderation tooling segments the actionable from the resolved.
findings.rootCause: viewBody (scr-admin.ts L91-115) renders st.reports as one undifferentiated stack with no status filter chips and no sort that floats OPEN to the top; REVIEWED items are kept inline by design but never grouped.
findings.userImpact: On a real queue the admin loses the signal (what still needs action) in the noise (already-reviewed items), reducing throughput and increasing the chance an OPEN report is missed.
findings.recommendation: Add status filter chips ('Abiertos' / 'Revisados' / 'Todos') mirroring the role chips in adminUsers (reuse that pattern, with keyboard support per ADM-02), default to Abiertos, and/or sort OPEN before REVIEWED in viewBody. File: app/lib/scr-admin.ts symbols render() (L118-137) + viewBody (L91-115).

findings.lens: Accessibility (WCAG 2.1 AA) — keyboard operability of clickable divs
findings.problem: In renderStudentSelf the 'Mis programas' rows are clickable <div class="card card-pad lift" style="cursor:pointer" onclick="go('course')"> with NO role, NO tabindex, and NO keyboard handler (scr-profile.ts line ~301). The codebase's [A11Y-01] keyboard-activation handler in Aula.tsx (~line 960) only fires for elements matching [role="button"][tabindex]. These rows match neither, so keyboard and screen-reader users literally cannot open their own enrolled courses from their profile — the single primary action of the student profile body.
findings.rootCause: The clickable row was authored as a bare div with an onclick (the same anti-pattern [A11Y-01] was created to fix elsewhere) but was never given the role/tabindex/aria-label that the central keyboard handler requires to function.
findings.userImpact: Keyboard-only and screen-reader students cannot navigate into any of their courses from the profile; the rows are invisible to assistive tech and unreachable by Tab. This fails WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) and 4.1.2 (Name/Role/Value).
findings.recommendation: Add role="button" tabindex="0" and an aria-label (e.g. `aria-label="Abrir ${esc(c.name)}"`) to the clickable course <div> in renderStudentSelf (scr-profile.ts ~line 301), matching the [A11Y-01] pattern used for lesson rows elsewhere. The existing Aula.tsx onKey handler will then make Enter/Space work for free.

findings.lens: Accessibility — heading order; Design system consistency
findings.problem: No screen in the cluster has a programmatic top-level heading. progress and badges use a non-semantic <div class="page-title"> (scr-profile.ts lines 77, 141). profile/coach skip straight to <h2> for the person's name (lines 206, 279, 359) with no h1 above it. Screen readers get a heading tree that starts at h2 (or has no heading at all on progress/badges), and the document outline has no page title.
findings.rootCause: page-title was implemented as a styled div rather than a heading element, and the profile/coach hero never establishes an h1, so the visual title and the accessibility tree diverge.
findings.userImpact: Screen-reader users navigating by heading land on an orphaned h2 (the name) or find no page heading at all, with no context for what page they are on. Fails WCAG 1.3.1 (Info and Relationships) and 2.4.6 (Headings and Labels).
findings.recommendation: Promote the visual page title to a real heading: make the .page-title element an <h1> in progress/badges, and on profile/coach wrap the page in an h1 (visually hidden or the existing title) so the name <h2> sits under a proper h1. Keep one h1 per screen.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-profile.ts (page-title divs lines 77 & 141; profile/coach <h2> name lines 206, 279, 359)

findings.lens: Design system / brand tokens; Jakob's law (convention)
findings.problem: starsRO() (scr-profile.ts line 12) and the clickable review .star buttons (line 463) render rating stars in `color:var(--otr-sky)` which tokens.css aliases to the brand GREEN #2CAA20. Per the OTR brand book and tokens.css comments, green is reserved for ACTION/interactive and gold (--otr-gold #F2B814) is the LOGRO/achievement color. Star ratings are an achievement/quality signal and are universally rendered gold/amber. Green stars are both a token-semantics violation and a convention break.
findings.rootCause: starsRO and the star button template hardcode var(--otr-sky) instead of the achievement token, so when the legacy alias was re-skinned to green, rating stars inherited the action color.
findings.userImpact: Users subconsciously read green stars as a clickable/positive-action element rather than a rating; the rating loses the instant 'gold = good score' recognition (Jakob's law). It also dilutes the green-only-for-action discipline that makes the rest of the product scannable.
findings.recommendation: Change starsRO fill and the review .star button color from var(--otr-sky) to var(--otr-gold) (and the lit/.on state likewise) in scr-profile.ts (lines 12 and 463). This aligns rating UI with the achievement token and with the gold already used for the certificate seal and badge medals in the same file.

findings.problem: renderCoachSelf shows the coach's rating and review count THREE times on one screen: in the hero inline row (line 210-211), in the hero KPI strip (lines 222-223, Rating + Reseñas), and again in the right-rail 'Valoración general' card (lines 254-256). The student-facing coach screen repeats it twice (hero row + 'Valoración' card). The same two numbers are restated with no added information.
findings.rootCause: The hero KPI strip and the right-rail valoración card were designed independently and both default to surfacing rating+reviews, on top of the inline stars already in the header.
findings.userImpact: Redundant numbers add visual weight and scanning cost without information gain (violates content-reduction and minimalist-aesthetic); the screen reads busier than it is and the truly unique KPI (Programas count) gets diluted.
findings.recommendation: Drop Rating and Reseñas from the hero KPI strip in renderCoachSelf (scr-profile.ts lines 222-223) — keep only Programas there — and let the right-rail 'Valoración general' card own the rating/review summary. Optionally remove the inline stars+count from the hero row too, since the rail card already shows them.

findings.lens: Accessibility — ARIA on custom controls; error announcement
findings.problem: The review rating is a row of 5 individual <button class="star"> elements (scr-profile.ts line 463). Each has an aria-label ('N estrellas') but the group has no radiogroup/label semantics and the buttons carry no aria-pressed; the selected rating exists only as a .on CSS class. A screen-reader user gets five unrelated buttons, no announced current value, and no indication that picking one is required. On submit, validation is a visual toast ('Selecciona una calificación', Aula.tsx leaveReview line 753) with no aria-live, so the error is not announced.
findings.rootCause: The star widget was built as five styled buttons toggling a CSS class, without the role="radiogroup"/role="radio"+aria-checked structure that a rating selector needs, and the toast system is not wired to an aria-live region for this form.
findings.userImpact: Screen-reader users can technically click a star but cannot perceive the current selection, that one is required, or why submission failed — they hit a silent dead end. Fails WCAG 4.1.2 and 4.1.3 (Status Messages).
findings.recommendation: Wrap #review-stars in role="radiogroup" aria-label="Calificación" and give each .star role="radio" with aria-checked toggled alongside the .on class (update the toggle in Aula.tsx ~line 938 and mount() in scr-profile.ts ~line 421). Ensure the validation toast renders into an aria-live="assertive" region.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-profile.ts (#review-stars / .star buttons line 462-463; mount paint() line 421) + app/components/Aula.tsx (star toggle line 938, leaveReview toast line 753)

findings.lens: Match real world / recognition over recall; IA
findings.problem: In renderCoachSelf the two header buttons are 'Editar perfil' and 'Perfil de marketplace' (scr-profile.ts lines 216-217). The second label names a place, not an action, and it is unclear how 'marketplace profile' differs from the profile you are already editing — both open edit modals (openEditProfile vs openEditCoachMarketplace, Aula.tsx 931-932). A coach cannot predict which fields live behind which button.
findings.rootCause: The button label was derived from the internal surface name ('marketplace profile') rather than from the user task it performs.
findings.userImpact: Coaches must open both modals by trial and error to discover where a given field (bio vs pricing/visibility) lives — a recognition-over-recall and match-the-real-world miss that adds friction to a low-frequency but important task.
findings.recommendation: Relabel the second button to the concrete task, e.g. 'Editar oferta del marketplace' or 'Precios y disponibilidad' (whichever matches what openEditCoachMarketplace actually edits), in scr-profile.ts line 217. If the two modals overlap heavily, consider merging them into one tabbed edit modal (Tesler: absorb the split).

findings.problem: In renderStudentSelf the right-rail 'Tu rango' card prints `${(DB.xpNext - DB.xp)} XP para el siguiente nivel` (scr-profile.ts line 321) with raw number concatenation, while every other XP figure in the cluster uses `.toLocaleString('es')` (e.g. line 96, 98, 324). If xpNext-xp reaches four digits it renders '1234' here but '1.234' two lines below in the same card. There is also no guard for the max-level case (if xpNext===xp it shows '0 XP para el siguiente nivel' rather than a 'nivel máximo' message like S.progress handles at line 98).
findings.rootCause: This card was written with inline arithmetic and skipped the toLocaleString helper and the nextLevel null-check that the canonical S.progress render already implements.
findings.userImpact: Inconsistent thousands formatting within one card looks unpolished, and a max-level student is told they need '0 XP para el siguiente nivel' (a nonexistent next level) — a small but real correctness/consistency defect.
findings.recommendation: In scr-profile.ts line 321, format the delta with `.toLocaleString('es')` and reuse the max-level branch from S.progress (show '¡Nivel máximo!' when there is no next level / delta<=0) so the student-profile rail matches the progress screen.

findings.lens: Visual hierarchy / scanability — radar competencies as a bar list
findings.problem: S.progress labels the competencies block 'Radar OTR' (scr-profile.ts line 102) but renders it as a vertical list of 6 horizontal bars (.comp-row, lines 107 / CSS screens.css 198-202), not a radar. The 6 fixed dimensions are exactly the kind of multi-axis comparison a radar/spider chart communicates at a glance; as a stacked bar list it reads as six unrelated metrics and the 'shape' of a debater's profile (the whole point of the OTR radar) is lost.
findings.rootCause: The label promises a radar but the implementation reused the generic horizontal bar component, so naming and visual form diverge.
findings.userImpact: Students and coaches cannot see balance/imbalance across the 6 skills at a glance — the comparative insight a radar gives is flattened into a list they must read row by row. Minor, since the data is still legible, but it under-delivers on the stated 'Radar' and on scanability.
findings.recommendation: Either rename the block to 'Competencias' and drop the 'Radar OTR' eyebrow (scr-profile.ts line 102) to stop over-promising, or implement an actual SVG radar for the 6 SKILL_DIMS. Cheapest correct fix: rename now, schedule the radar as a separate enhancement.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-profile.ts (S.progress 'Radar OTR' eyebrow + comp-row list lines 102-107)

findings.lens: Exploratory QA / Nielsen (status visibility, match real world)
findings.problem: The 'Imprimir' button calls window.print() but there is NO print stylesheet scoped to the diploma. The only @media print block in screens.css (lines 361-366) targets #pr-print (the admin report modal). Printing the certificate therefore renders the entire app chrome — dark sidebar, topbar, breadcrumbs, and the page-head action row containing the 'Imprimir'/'Volver' buttons themselves — around the diploma. A diploma is the one artifact users will actually print/PDF, so this is the highest-visibility failure on the screen.
findings.rootCause: window.print() with no diploma-scoped print rules; the existing @media print block is hard-scoped to body:has(#pr-print) only.
findings.userImpact: User prints/saves their official diploma and gets the LMS navigation furniture printed around it (including the print button), producing an unusable, unprofessional document.
findings.recommendation: Add an @media print block in app/styles/screens.css that, when the certificate is on screen, hides the shell and the page-head action row and shows only .diploma-wrap. Concretely: give the diploma container a stable hook (e.g. id="cert-print" on .diploma-wrap in scr-certificate.ts) and add `@media print{ body:has(#cert-print) .app-sidebar, body:has(#cert-print) .topbar, body:has(#cert-print) .page-head{display:none!important} body:has(#cert-print) #cert-print{margin:0!important} }` mirroring the existing #pr-print pattern. Mark the page-head .row of buttons with data-noprint.
findings.file: app/styles/screens.css (@media print) + app/lib/scr-certificate.ts (S.certificate.render, .diploma-wrap container + print button row)

findings.problem: scr-certificate.ts references three CSS classes that are not defined anywhere in app/styles/*.css: .cert-seal (the ceremonial seal wrapper, line 14), .cert-name (the recipient name, line 86), and .diploma (the card, line 62). The seal and name therefore render only with their inline styles; the .cert-seal wrapper has no layout/centering rules of its own, so the seal's placement is entirely incidental. This is unmaintained component drift — anyone editing the diploma layout via CSS will find no rules to edit.
findings.rootCause: Markup was written expecting companion CSS classes that were never authored (or were removed); only inline styles carry the layout.
findings.userImpact: Fragile rendering: the seal/name depend on inline-style accidents rather than a real stylesheet, and the layout cannot be tuned or made print-safe via CSS without first reverse-engineering the inline styles.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) add real definitions for .cert-seal (display:flex;justify-content:center;margin:0 auto), .cert-name, and .diploma in screens.css, co-located with the new @media print rules from CERT-01, or (b) drop the orphan class names from scr-certificate.ts and keep the inline styles as the single source of truth. Pick one; do not leave dangling class hooks.

findings.lens: Nielsen (visibility of system status, error prevention) / Exploratory QA
findings.problem: Notification preferences (the 4 toggles in NOTIF) persist ONLY to localStorage (notifOn/toggle handlers, lines 23-31, 127-136) and the toast says 'Notificación activada' as if it were saved to the account. There is no disclosure that these settings are device-local and will not follow the user to another browser/device, nor that they do not actually gate any backend notification. The leaderboard toggle right beside them DOES hit PATCH /api/profile, so two visually identical switches have completely different persistence semantics with no signal to the user.
findings.rootCause: MVP shortcut (localStorage-only notif prefs) presented with the same affordance and the same confirmation language as the real backend-persisted leaderboard toggle.
findings.userImpact: User toggles 'Recordatorios de sesiones' off on their laptop, trusts the confirmation, then keeps getting reminders / sees the setting reverted on their phone — a silent broken-promise that erodes trust in the whole settings surface.
findings.recommendation: In scr-settings.ts add one faint line under the Notificaciones card title, e.g. 'Estas preferencias se guardan en este dispositivo por ahora.', and soften the toast to 'Preferencia guardada en este dispositivo'. Longer term, route these through PATCH /api/profile like the leaderboard toggle so the two switches behave identically. Lowest-effort honest fix is the disclosure line.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-settings.ts (NOTIF card in render, toggle mount handler toast)

findings.problem: Settings is built as 5 separate cards (Cuenta, Idioma, Notificaciones, Membresía, Privacidad) + a danger card, but three of the cards are single rows whose only content is a 'route elsewhere' button: 'Idioma' is one row, 'Membresía y facturación' is one row whose sole purpose is a 'Gestionar membresía →' button, and within Privacidad the 'Perfil público' row just routes to lifetime. Wrapping single routing rows in full titled cards inflates vertical length and dilutes the screen's real owned controls (language toggle, notif switches, leaderboard, password, logout).
findings.rootCause: One-card-per-PRD-section structure applied even to sections that own no actual setting, only a deep-link.
findings.userImpact: More scrolling and visual chunking than the content warrants; the genuinely-actionable controls are spread thin across tall cards, lowering scan efficiency.
findings.recommendation: Merge 'Idioma' and 'Membresía' rows into the existing cards rather than giving each its own card: e.g. put the language control as a row inside the 'Cuenta' card, and fold 'Tu plan' into the same card or into Privacidad/seguridad as a routing row. Reserve full cards for sections that contain >1 owned control. This is a card-count reduction in S.settings.render, not a redesign.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-settings.ts (S.settings.render — card() calls for Idioma & Membresía)

findings.problem: The entire 'Torneos' section is a non-owning mirror of the Debate Hub: tournamentRow's only interactive control is 'Ver e inscribirme →' (data-go-tournaments) and the section footer is another 'Ver todos en el Debate Hub →' — both route away to debate. The file's own header comment confirms tournament enrollment 'NO se duplica: vive en el Debate Hub'. So the screen shows a read-only list whose every action bounces the user to another screen to actually act. That is a redirect surface masquerading as a destination.
findings.rootCause: Events was given a Torneos card for completeness, but the canonical tournament flow lives in Debate Hub, leaving Events with list-without-action duplication.
findings.userImpact: User reads the tournament list, taps the only button, and is yanked to a different screen to do the thing — a pointless extra hop (Tesler/Jakob: the system surfaces complexity instead of absorbing it).
findings.recommendation: In scr-events.ts collapse the Torneos card to a single compact teaser: show the count + the next 1-2 tournament names and ONE 'Ver torneos en el Debate Hub →' link, dropping the per-row 'Ver e inscribirme' buttons (they all go to the same place anyway). Let Events be 'próximos eventos' first-class and tournaments a one-line pointer. Keeps the screen but removes the duplicated action rows.

findings.problem: The session-detail aside prints the raw uppercase enum for status: `${esc(b.status)}` (line 129) renders 'CONFIRMED' / 'PENDING' / 'COMPLETED' to the user. Everywhere else in the cluster the same statuses are humanized — scr-mybookings.ts statusBadge maps CONFIRMED→'Confirmada', PENDING→'Esperando consentimiento', etc. So the exact same booking shows a friendly Spanish badge in 'Mis reservas' and a raw English code in the room it links to.
findings.rootCause: room aside reuses the booking object but re-implements the Estado field with a bare esc(b.status) instead of the shared statusBadge mapping.
findings.userImpact: Inside the paid-session room, a non-technical/Spanish user sees an untranslated database code ('CONFIRMED'), breaking the otherwise-polished Spanish-first experience exactly where trust matters most (the delivered service).
findings.recommendation: In scr-room.ts import and reuse the statusBadge() helper from scr-mybookings.ts (or extract it to a shared helper) and render that in the Estado row instead of esc(b.status). At minimum, map the enum to the same Spanish labels used by statusBadge.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-room.ts (aside kv block, Estado row) reusing scr-mybookings.ts statusBadge

findings.lens: Error prevention / User control & undo (Nielsen) / Cognitive walkthrough
findings.problem: Cancel is a destructive, money-moving action (PATCH action:'cancel' → escrow REFUNDED) but the only guard is a 'tap the button again within 4s' arm pattern (upcomingRow line 90; mount lines 184-209). The button label changes to '¿Cancelar? Tocar de nuevo'. There is no modal stating the consequence (refund, slot lost), no reason capture, and the double-tap is easy to fire accidentally on touch (two quick taps). It is also shown for PENDING bookings the same way as CONFIRMED, with no distinction in stakes.
findings.rootCause: A lightweight inline 'arm' pattern substituted for a real confirmation on an irreversible financial action.
findings.userImpact: A double-tap (common on mobile) cancels a confirmed paid session and triggers a refund/slot loss with no clear consequence preview and no undo — a classic destructive-action-without-confirmation failure.
findings.recommendation: In scr-mybookings.ts replace the data-armed double-tap with the existing confirm modal (the codebase already has w.otrFormModal / a confirm dialog used elsewhere): show 'Cancelar esta sesión? Se reembolsará tu pago y se liberará el horario.' with explicit Confirmar/No. Keep the optimistic refresh on success. This aligns it with how other destructive flows are guarded.

findings.problem: Switching conversations does a full screen re-render: clicking a .convo sets window.__convo and calls go('messages') (mount lines 142-145), which rebuilds the entire msg-wrap. This drops the scroll position you were in, re-runs all fade-up animations, blurs the compose input, and re-fetches/rebuilds the whole list for what should be an in-place panel swap. Sent messages are optimistically appended with when:'ahora' but there is no delivered/read/sending state and no error surface if the POST fails (the fetch .catch is a no-op, line 155).
findings.rootCause: Conversation selection is modeled as a route navigation (go('messages')) rather than swapping the thread panel in place; send is fire-and-forget with swallowed errors.
findings.userImpact: Every conversation switch feels heavy (re-animates, loses scroll/focus); and if a send silently fails, the user sees their own bubble but the recipient never gets it, with zero indication — a trust gap in a messaging surface.
findings.recommendation: In scr-community.ts S.messages.mount, on .convo click swap only the .msg-thread innerHTML (re-run the thread builder for the new active index) instead of go('messages'); preserve compose focus. For send, surface a failure: on fetch .catch, mark the bubble with an error state + retry instead of swallowing. Also note this screen lives in scr-community.ts which is otherwise the OFF/disabled cluster — consider moving S.messages to its own file so the live messages route is not coupled to disabled forum code.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-community.ts (S.messages.mount — convo click handler + push() error handling)

findings.lens: Visual hierarchy / Cognitive walkthrough (first-time user) / Consistency
findings.problem: The onboarding footer presents two competing exits with near-equal weight: a 'Saltar' ghost button and the primary 'Guardar y entrar al hub' — both ultimately navigate to dashboard (ob-foot, lines 310-313). For a first-time user this muddies the single intended path. Additionally the student goals chips are bound twice with different state models: render() marks active via prefs.goals.includes (line 303) but mount() toggles purely with classList.toggle on click and then reads .chip.active on save (lines 320, 334) — workable but the 'Saltar' path discards everything silently with no acknowledgement that nothing was saved.
findings.rootCause: Skip and Save are placed as peer footer buttons; the screen does not make the recommended path visually dominant, and 'Saltar' gives no feedback.
findings.userImpact: New users (the exact audience here) face a two-choice fork at the most important moment, and the lower-effort 'Saltar' is visually competitive with the real CTA, lowering completion of the experience setup.
findings.recommendation: In scr-hub.ts onboarding.render, demote 'Saltar' to a quiet text link aligned left (or 'Saltar por ahora' as a small btn-quiet) and let only 'Guardar y entrar' carry primary weight; keep them apart (Fitts/grouping). Optionally add a toast on Saltar ('Puedes configurarlo luego en Ajustes') so the skip isn't a silent dead-end.

findings.problem: The 6 placement sliders are native range inputs styled ONLY with inline `accent-color:var(--otr-sky)` (scr-placement.ts line 59); there is NO .pl-range CSS anywhere in app/styles (confirmed by grep — .pl-range, .pl-out are undefined). So the draggable thumb is the browser default size (~12-16px) with no enlarged hit area, no visible custom focus ring matching the app's --ring, and inconsistent rendering across browsers. This is the single most important interaction on the screen (the whole task is dragging these), yet it gets the least design attention and the smallest target.
findings.rootCause: Range styling was left to the native control + one inline accent-color; the companion .pl-range/.pl-out classes referenced in markup were never authored in CSS.
findings.userImpact: Small native thumbs are hard to grab precisely (Fitts), especially on touch and for elderly/low-dexterity users, on the very screen designed around dragging; keyboard focus on the slider may not show the app's standard focus ring, hurting keyboard/AA operability.
findings.recommendation: Add .pl-range styling in app/styles/screens.css: enlarge the thumb (e.g. 22-24px) with ::-webkit-slider-thumb / ::-moz-range-thumb, a clear track, and a :focus-visible box-shadow:var(--ring) so keyboard focus is visible and consistent with the rest of the app. Also style the .pl-out value chip so the live readout reads clearly.

findings.lens: Error recovery / Nielsen (help users recover) / flexibility
findings.problem: The 'Cambiar contraseña' control depends on a global w.otrFormModal; if it is absent the handler bails with toast 'No disponible aquí' (lines 158-159). That is a dead end with no fallback path to change a password — a security-relevant action silently becomes unavailable with an unhelpful message, and the user has no alternative route.
findings.rootCause: Hard dependency on an optional global with only a terminal toast as the failure mode.
findings.userImpact: In any context where otrFormModal isn't mounted, the user simply cannot change their password and is told 'No disponible aquí' with no next step.
findings.recommendation: In scr-settings.ts ensure otrFormModal is reliably available where settings renders, or provide a fallback (e.g. route to a dedicated password screen / inline form) instead of a terminal toast. At minimum make the message actionable ('Recarga la página e intenta de nuevo').

findings.lens: Information architecture & discoverability; Nielsen (match real world, consistency, recognition>recall)
findings.problem: The coach onboarding empty-state button 'Activa tu perfil de coach' routes to data-go="coach", which is the read-only PUBLIC coach profile (S.coach / renderStudentSelf view). That route is registered with nav:'profile' and crumbs ['OTR','Coach'], so the coach lands on a non-editable page, the sidebar highlights 'Mi perfil' (implying it's their editable profile when it is not), and the breadcrumb reads a generic 'OTR / Coach' that doesn't match where they are. The coach's actual editable profile is r:'profile' (renderCoachSelf, per the explicit comment in shell.ts lines 61-62).
findings.rootCause: Two distinct concepts share confusingly-named routes: 'coach' = public read-only view, 'profile' = editable self. The coachwork CTA points at the public one. The 'coach' ROUTE also borrows nav:'profile', so any arrival there falsely activates the Perfil nav item.
findings.userImpact: A new coach clicks the one CTA meant to set up their profile and reaches a dead-end read-only view with no edit controls and a misleading active nav state — the single most important supply-side onboarding action fails.
findings.recommendation: In app/lib/scr-coachwork.ts line 304 change data-go="coach" to data-go="profile" so the coach lands on their editable profile (renderCoachSelf). Separately, in app/lib/screens.ts give the 'coach' route honest crumbs (e.g. ['Marketplace','Coach']) and consider nav:'explore' rather than nav:'profile' so viewing a public coach never highlights the viewer's own Perfil item.

findings.lens: Information architecture & discoverability; Content reduction; Hick's/Tesler; Enterprise benchmark
findings.problem: renderShell() mounts the topbar searchbox for every role (shell.ts lines 173-176), and Enter routes to the 'search' screen for everyone (Aula.tsx lines 953-956). But S.search (scr-extra.ts lines 293-308) only ever searches DB.catalog (student course catalog) and DB.students (a student roster), rendering course tiles and student avatars. For a coach/parent/admin this returns either nothing meaningful or a list of students they can't act on, and the course cards aren't even clickable. It is a prominent affordance that leads to a confusing or empty destination for 3 of 4 roles.
findings.rootCause: Search is a single student-centric screen wired into a shell chrome that is shared verbatim across all roles, with no role-awareness in the result set or in whether the box is shown.
findings.userImpact: Coach/parent/admin see a primary search control (universal 'I can find things here' signal), type, and get irrelevant student data or 'Sin resultados' — breaks Jakob's expectation that search returns things relevant to me, and adds noise to roles that should have a leaner shell.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) gate the searchbox in shell.ts to role==='student' (desk-only block lines 173-176) since only student data is searchable, OR (b) make S.search role-aware in scr-extra.ts: for teacher search their participants/courses, for parent search their children/coaches, for admin search users — and make course result cards navigable (data-go to the course/marketplace). Lowest-effort honest fix: hide the box for non-students until per-role search exists.

findings.lens: Information architecture (role-gating clarity); Nielsen (error prevention); Exploratory QA
findings.problem: renderApp() only role-gates routes that carry an explicit role field (Aula.tsx line 57). Student-shaped routes — progress, lifetime, badges, grades, course, catalog, debate, placement, certificate, membership, my-bookings — have NO role field, so a coach/parent/admin reaching them (via search, a stray go(), or a hand-edited #hash) renders student-only screens (XP/streak/skill-graph 'Niveles', 'Mi trayectoria', 'Mis calificaciones') inside their own shell. These concepts explicitly don't belong to coach/parent (shell.ts lines 66-70 say Niveles is not the coach's concept), yet nothing prevents arrival.
findings.rootCause: The guard is opt-in per route (def.role) rather than a positive allow-list of what each role may see. Most student routes simply omit role, so they are globally reachable.
findings.userImpact: Non-students can land on screens built around a mental model that isn't theirs (student progression UI), with no nav item active and no clear way the page applies to them — confusing and inconsistent role boundaries.
findings.recommendation: Tag the student-only routes in app/lib/screens.ts with role:'student' (progress, lifetime, badges, grades, course, catalog, placement, certificate, plus the student-only course sub-routes), so renderApp's existing guard (Aula.tsx line 57) redirects non-students to ROLE_HOME. Keep shared routes (membership, explore/marketplace, messages, settings, profile, dashboard, events, debate where intended) ungated.
findings.file: app/lib/screens.ts (add role:'student' to student-only ROUTES); guard already in app/components/Aula.tsx line 57

findings.lens: Nielsen (visibility of system status, error recovery); Cognitive walkthrough
findings.problem: renderApp() does `let def = ROUTES[r]; if (!def) return;` (Aula.tsx lines 53-54) — clicking a link to a non-existent route does nothing at all: no toast, no 404 surface, no console hint. Live, this is reachable from disabled screens that still emit dead links (scr-hub.ts data-go="arsenal", scr-community.ts go('forum')/go('forum-thread')); those screens are OFF by design, but the silent-no-op behavior is the actual IA risk because any future typo'd route or stale link dead-ends invisibly.
findings.rootCause: No fallback branch for missing routes; the engine assumes every emitted go()/data-go target is registered.
findings.userImpact: If a link is ever wrong, the user clicks and nothing happens — the worst feedback state (looks broken, no recovery). Silent failures also hide regressions during development.
findings.recommendation: In app/components/Aula.tsx renderApp, replace the bare `if (!def) return;` with a graceful fallback: in dev console.warn the missing route, and for the user either toast('Esa sección no está disponible') and stay put, or redirect to ROLE_HOME[state.role]. This guarantees navigation always produces visible feedback.

findings.lens: Information architecture (grouping, predictable placement); Visual hierarchy
findings.problem: The coach nav puts a lone 'Coaches' (explore/marketplace) item under group 'Principal' (shell.ts lines 48-50), then the real coach home (Panel de coach, Reservas, Gestionar, Mensajes, Mi perfil) under 'Espacio de coach'. A single-item 'Principal' group pointing at the consumer-side marketplace is an odd anchor — the coach's primary section is the workspace, so 'Principal' reads as the top/home group but contains a secondary, cross-side destination.
findings.rootCause: The marketplace 'view my listing' entry was placed in a top group to mirror the student layout, but for the coach it is not the principal surface; the workspace is.
findings.userImpact: Mild mental-model friction: the highest visual slot in the coach sidebar is a marketplace browse link rather than their actual home, and a one-item group adds a redundant header.
findings.recommendation: In app/lib/shell.ts merge the lone 'explore' item into the 'Espacio de coach' group (e.g. as 'Mi perfil en marketplace' near 'Mi perfil') and drop the single-item 'group.main' for the teacher role, so 'Espacio de coach' becomes the unambiguous top section. Alternatively relabel it 'Marketplace' to match the student's grouping.

findings.problem: The student nav item for 'Niveles' declares a hardcoded badge:'Varsity' (shell.ts line 32), but navBadge() overrides it at runtime with DB.me?.level (shell.ts line 125), which per recent commit c1dcce4 is 'Novato' for new users. So the literal 'Varsity' string is dead source data — it never renders — but it is misleading to future maintainers and could resurface if the override logic changes.
findings.rootCause: Leftover static badge value not removed when the dynamic level-from-XP badge was introduced.
findings.userImpact: None to end users today (override wins); maintenance hazard / source-of-truth ambiguity only.
findings.recommendation: Remove `badge:'Varsity'` from the 'progress' nav item in app/lib/shell.ts line 32; the live badge is already sourced from DB.me.level in navBadge() (line 125), making the literal redundant and contradictory.

findings.problem: The systemic '&' double-escape (known bug #1) is a design-system contract failure, not a one-off. queries.ts escapes user data at the DATA layer (e.g. line 616 esc(pickLang(c.name,...)), line 790 esc(u.name), line 870 esc(p.specialties)), but the scr-*.ts builders have NO consistent rule about whether to re-escape: scr-core.ts:181/454 and :99/380 call esc(c.name) AGAIN, scr-marketplace.ts:194/206/298/441/465 call esc() again on names/specialties, scr-debate.ts:197 esc(nextEvent.name) — all producing literal '&'. Meanwhile scr-learn.ts:567-569 explicitly does NOT re-escape with a comment saying the data 'YA vienen esc() desde queries.ts', and scr-teacher.ts:600 has the same warning comment. So the same field is treated as raw in one file and pre-escaped in another.
findings.rootCause: No single, documented ownership of HTML-escaping. Both the data layer (queries.ts) and the view layer (scr-*.ts) escape, and builders disagree on who owns it, so any field containing & < > " ' double-encodes on roughly half the surfaces.
findings.userImpact: Course 'Oratoria & Speaking' and coach specialty 'Lincoln-Douglas & Policy' render the literal entity to users on primary commercial surfaces (catalog cards, course tabs/title, marketplace cards + coach profile + program packages). Looks like a broken/un-QA'd product on the exact screens that sell courses and coaches.
findings.recommendation: Pick ONE layer and make it the rule. Recommended: keep escaping in queries.ts (the data layer) and STOP escaping in builders for any field that comes from queries.ts — i.e. delete the redundant esc() at scr-core.ts:99,181,380,454 and scr-marketplace.ts:194,206,298,381,403,441,462,465,481 and scr-debate.ts:197. Then add a one-line contract comment at the top of esc.ts and queries.ts stating 'data from queries.ts is already HTML-safe; never esc() it again in scr-*.ts'. Grep guard: any esc(<field-from-queries>) is now a bug.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-core.ts (esc(c.name) at :181,:454,:99,:380), app/lib/scr-marketplace.ts (esc on names/specialties), app/lib/queries.ts:616/790/870 (escape ownership)

findings.problem: The on/off switch is hand-coded with inline styles in 3 places and there is no shared component or class in app.css. scr-settings.ts:28-31 (toggle) and :35-38 (lbToggle) are VERBATIM copies of each other, and scr-lifetime.ts:372 is a third variant. Worse, the colors drift: settings toggles use 'var(--otr-green)' (#2CAA20, bright) for the 'on' state, while lifetime's toggle uses 'var(--otr-sky-lo)' (#1E8C16, the dark green) for the identical 'on' semantic. Same control, two different greens, depending on which file rendered it.
findings.rootCause: No .switch / .toggle component exists in app.css, so each screen author re-implements it inline and picks the green token from memory.
findings.userImpact: A user toggling notifications in Settings and a privacy preference in Mi trayectoria sees two visibly different shades of green for the same 'enabled' state — subtle but exactly the kind of incoherence that erodes the 'premium/prestige' brand the tokens file is trying to enforce. Also triples maintenance surface for any future switch change (size, a11y, animation).
findings.recommendation: Add a single .switch component to app.css (track 44x25, thumb 19px, radius var(--r-pill), on=var(--otr-green), off=var(--n-200), role=switch already present) and a tiny shared switchHtml(key,on,label) helper. Replace scr-settings.ts:28 toggle, :35 lbToggle, and scr-lifetime.ts:372 with it. This also locks the 'on' color to one token.

findings.problem: btn-soft (green tint, 54 uses) and btn-ghost (white + border, 48 uses) are applied to the SAME secondary-action role with no discernible rule. Examples within one screen-cluster: 'Ver más coaches' = btn-ghost but 'Explorar coaches' = btn-soft; 'Ver índice' = btn-ghost but 'Ver progreso'/'Ver programa'/'Ver historial' = btn-soft; the 'Volver' back-action is rendered as btn-ghost in some places (scr-marketplace 'Volver al curso', course-builder examples) and btn-soft in others. The choice is author-by-author, not semantic.
findings.rootCause: No documented rule for when a secondary button is soft (tinted, draws some attention) vs ghost (quiet, outline). Both look 'secondary' so authors pick arbitrarily.
findings.userImpact: Buttons of equal importance get different visual weight from screen to screen, so the tinted btn-soft loses its meaning as an emphasis signal — users can't learn 'green-tinted = the suggested next action'. Mild visual incoherence that a benchmark product (Linear/Stripe) would not ship.
findings.recommendation: Write a one-line rule in app.css above the .btn block and apply it: btn-soft = the single suggested secondary action on a surface (the one you'd nudge toward); btn-ghost = neutral/back/dismiss and any repeated row action. Concretely, standardize all 'Volver'/back buttons to btn-ghost and reserve btn-soft for the primary 'explore/continue' secondary CTA. Codemod: most 'Volver' instances are btn-soft btn-sm and should become btn-ghost btn-sm.

findings.problem: .btn-accent (the green affirmative-action button) is fully defined in app.css:134-135 with hover, but is used 0 times in any of the 21 scr-*.ts builders — confirmed: the only 3 references to 'btn-accent' in the whole repo are the CSS definition and a comment. Affirmative CTAs (Continuar/Confirmar/Reservar/Guardar) all correctly use btn-primary (black) per the brand book.
findings.rootCause: The class was defined to express 'affirmative = green' but the brand book later settled on 'primary CTA = black, green lives in accents only', leaving btn-accent orphaned.
findings.userImpact: No direct user impact today. The risk is future: a dead, ready-to-use green button class invites a future contributor to ship a green primary CTA, silently violating the brand rule (black primary). It is a latent inconsistency.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) delete .btn-accent and its hover from app.css:134-135 to remove the trap, or (b) if green affirmative CTAs are intended for a specific case (e.g. 'Confirmar pago'), document that single allowed use in a comment and adopt it consistently. Given the brand book explicitly says primary=black, prefer deletion.

findings.problem: The ES/EN language pill toggle is implemented twice with copy-pasted inline styles: shell.ts:177-178 (topbar) and scr-settings.ts:76-78. Both are border-radius:100px containers with navy 'on' / transparent buttons and nearly identical padding/font rules, maintained independently.
findings.rootCause: No shared langToggle component; the topbar version was duplicated into Settings rather than reused.
findings.userImpact: Low — both render acceptably. But the two copies have already drifted slightly (font-size 11.5px vs 12.5px, padding 4px 9px vs 5px 15px), so the same control looks a touch different in the topbar vs Settings, and any future fix must be made in two places.
findings.recommendation: Extract a langToggle(lang) helper (or a .seg-pill class) and use it in both shell.ts:177 and scr-settings.ts:76. While there, replace the inline border-radius:100px with var(--r-pill) for token consistency.

findings.problem: Pill radius is written as a literal (border-radius:100px or 999px) inline in 6+ spots instead of var(--r-pill): scr-lifetime.ts:372, scr-placement.ts:72, scr-settings.ts:30/37/76/78, shell.ts:177/178. tokens.css already defines --r-pill:999px specifically for this.
findings.rootCause: Inline-styled controls bypass the radius scale; authors type a literal instead of referencing the token.
findings.userImpact: Negligible visually (100px and 999px both fully round these small controls). It is pure token-discipline debt: a future change to pill rounding won't propagate to these hand-written instances.
findings.recommendation: Replace the literal border-radius:100px / 999px with var(--r-pill) in the listed inline styles. Best folded into DS-02 and DS-05 since those controls are being componentized anyway.

findings.problem: The primary cards on two flagship screens are keyboard-inoperable. Marketplace coach cards render as <div class="tile click" data-coach="..."> (scr-marketplace.ts:189) and Debate Hub cards as <div class="tile click" data-debate="..."> (scr-debate.ts:225). Each gets a mouse-only click listener (scr-marketplace.ts:512 opens the coach profile; scr-debate.ts:490 opens the debate detail) but NO role="button", NO tabindex, NO keyboard handler. The global [A11Y-01] activator in Aula.tsx:960 only fires for elements matching '[role="button"][tabindex]', so these cards can neither be focused via Tab nor activated via Enter/Space. A keyboard or screen-reader user literally cannot open a coach profile or a debate.
findings.rootCause: The [A11Y-01] keyboard pattern (role=button + tabindex=0, relied on by the global Enter/Space handler) was applied to lesson <tr> rows (scr-core.ts:527) but never propagated to the .tile click cards that use bespoke data-* click delegation. The keyboard contract is opt-in per element and these cards never opted in.
findings.userImpact: Keyboard-only and screen-reader users on the marketplace and debate hub cannot reach or activate the main content cards — the core action of each screen is unavailable to them (WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard, Level A failure).
findings.recommendation: Add role="button" tabindex="0" to the coach card div (scr-marketplace.ts:189) and the debate card div (scr-debate.ts:225); also add an aria-label (e.g. `aria-label="Ver perfil de ${esc(c.name)}"` / `aria-label="Abrir debate ${esc(h.title)}"`). With role=button+tabindex present, the existing Aula.tsx:960 handler already fires .click() on Enter/Space — no JS change needed. Apply the same pattern to any other .tile click / clickable div using data-* delegation.

findings.screen: Course (Mi aprendizaje, module accordion) + Trayectoria (lifetime skill rows)
findings.problem: Two more primary interactions are keyboard-inoperable for the same reason. (1) The course module accordion header is <div class="module-head" data-acc> (scr-core.ts:415) with a mouse-only toggle (scr-core.ts:493) and no role/tabindex — modules cannot be expanded/collapsed by keyboard. (2) The trajectory skill rows are <div data-lpskill="..." style="cursor:pointer"> (scr-lifetime.ts:174) that expand a 'what moved this skill' panel on click, again with no role/tabindex/keyboard path.
findings.rootCause: Same root cause as A11Y-01: clickable <div> containers wired through data-* click delegation were never given the role=button + tabindex contract that the global [A11Y-01] Enter/Space activator depends on.
findings.userImpact: Keyboard users cannot expand course modules (so they cannot reach lessons inside collapsed modules via keyboard) nor open the skill-detail expanders on their own progress page (WCAG 2.1.1 Level A).
findings.recommendation: On scr-core.ts:415 make the accordion header keyboard-operable: add role="button" tabindex="0" plus aria-expanded reflecting the open state (toggle it in the data-acc handler at scr-core.ts:493) and aria-controls pointing at .module-items. On scr-lifetime.ts:174 add role="button" tabindex="0" aria-expanded="${open}" and an aria-label. Both then activate via the existing Aula.tsx:960 handler.

findings.screen: All screens (systemic) — esp. Debate, Marketplace, Coach panel, Parent, Admin, Trayectoria
findings.lens: Accessibility (WCAG 2.1 AA) · semantic markup / heading order
findings.problem: The page's main title is rendered as a non-semantic <div class="page-title"> in 42 places; only 2 screens use a real <h1>. Worse, scr-debate.ts, scr-marketplace.ts, scr-teacher.ts, scr-parent.ts, scr-admin.ts and scr-lifetime.ts contain NO <h1> at all and jump straight to <h3>/<h4>. Screen readers building a heading outline find either no top-level heading or an out-of-order one for the screen's own title.
findings.rootCause: page-title was styled purely visually (.page-title font-size token in app.css) and the builders emit it as a <div>; there is no shared heading helper enforcing one <h1> per screen, so each builder freelances its heading level.
findings.userImpact: Screen-reader users lose the single most useful navigation primitive — jumping to the page's H1 / the heading outline — and encounter skipped heading levels, failing WCAG 1.3.1 (Info & Relationships, Level A) and 2.4.6 (Headings & Labels, Level AA).
findings.recommendation: Change the page-title element from <div class="page-title"> to <h1 class="page-title"> (the CSS class already carries all visual styling, so appearance is unchanged) everywhere it represents the screen title; for the heading-less screens (debate/marketplace/teacher/parent/admin/lifetime) introduce an <h1 class="page-title"> as the screen title and demote the current top heading accordingly so levels don't skip. Best done via a shared pageHead() helper so future screens inherit one <h1>.
findings.file: app/lib/scr-core.ts (page-title ~L361,L513) + scr-debate.ts, scr-marketplace.ts, scr-teacher.ts, scr-parent.ts, scr-admin.ts, scr-lifetime.ts

findings.screen: Badges/status indicators (global) — badge.ok, badge.danger, eyebrow, active tab
findings.problem: Several small-text-on-tint token pairs fall below the 4.5:1 AA threshold for normal/bold text. Measured from tokens.css values: .badge.ok (#1E8C16 on #E1F2DE) = 3.73:1; .badge.danger (#C2453C on #F7E0DE) = 3.96:1; .eyebrow (green #2CAA20, 11px bold) = 3.05:1 (77 uses); .tab.active label (--action-hover #1E8C16 on white) = 4.36:1; .sky text class (#2CAA20) = 3.05:1 (15 text uses). All are <14pt or bold-<14pt, so the 4.5:1 normal-text rule applies, not the 3:1 large-text rule.
findings.rootCause: Brand green (#2CAA20) and its -lo (#1E8C16) variants were tuned to pass on the pale tints used as badge backgrounds in the OTRBRANDBOOK palette, but the chosen text shade was not darkened to the green-text #176B11 token that the brand book itself created precisely for AA on pale tints (badge.sky already uses it and passes at 5.71:1).
findings.userImpact: Low-vision users may not reliably read status badges (ok/danger), eyebrow labels, or the active-tab label. Since badges/eyebrows are usually paired with an adjacent text label, the information is rarely color-only, which caps the severity at medium.
findings.recommendation: In app.css/tokens.css: set .badge.ok text to a darker green (use --otr-green-text #176B11 → 5.71:1, matching .badge.sky), darken .badge.danger text to ~#9A2F28 (≈5:1 on its soft), set .eyebrow and the .sky text class to #176B11 instead of #2CAA20, and set .tab.active color to the darker --ok/#176B11. No layout impact — these are color-only swaps reusing existing tokens.
findings.file: app/styles/app.css (.badge.ok L202, .badge.danger L204, .eyebrow L118, .tab.active L232, .sky L24)

findings.screen: All create/edit modals with a rich-text field (Course Builder, lesson/resource editors)
findings.problem: In formModal (Aula.tsx:161-167) the rich-text field's <label for="${fid}"> targets a contenteditable <div id="${fid}" data-rich="1">. A native <label for> only binds to labelable form controls — it does not associate with, nor click-focus, a contenteditable div. The div also lacks role="textbox" and an accessible name, and the formatting toolbar buttons rely on title="" (not aria-label) for their names.
findings.rootCause: The rich field reuses the same wrap()/label markup as real <input>/<select> controls, but contenteditable is not a labelable element so the for/id link silently no-ops.
findings.userImpact: Screen-reader users get no programmatic label on the rich editor (announced as a generic editable region), and clicking its visible label does not move focus into it. Affects coaches authoring lesson/resource content; not the most common path, hence low.
findings.recommendation: On the contenteditable div add role="textbox" aria-multiline="true" and aria-labelledby pointing at the label (give the <label> an id), instead of relying on for/id. Optionally wire the label's click to focus the editor. Keep title on toolbar buttons but add matching aria-label for SR exposure.

findings.lens: Information architecture & discoverability / Hick's law / Tesler's
findings.problem: The student 'Centro de progreso' nav group has FOUR items — 'Mi trayectoria' (lifetimeProfile), 'Niveles' (progress), 'Mis calificaciones' (grades), 'Logros' (badges) — and three of them overlap heavily. lifetimeProfile already renders the skill graph (radar SVG + per-skill attribution), a 7-stat ledger, performance/rating, credentials AND the journey timeline. progress re-renders the same 6-skill radar + a level track + a streak grid. badges re-renders certificates + a badge collection grid. The user has to learn which of 4 screens holds 'my radar / my level / my certificate'. Disappear-test: if 'Niveles' and 'Logros' vanished as standalone destinations, almost nothing unique is lost — the level track is the only non-duplicated element on progress, and the badge/cert grid is the only one on badges.
findings.rootCause: Each progress facet (skill graph, levels, badges, lifetime) was built as its own screen during separate PRD waves; lifetimeProfile (PRD §8) was added later as the 'moat' and silently became a superset without retiring the older single-purpose screens.
findings.userImpact: Students bounce between 4 sidebar items hunting for the same numbers; the menu reads as cluttered and the redundancy makes the product feel less considered than Linear/Notion, which expose one progress home.
findings.recommendation: Collapse the Progress Center to TWO nav items: 'Mi trayectoria' (lifetime, the superset) and 'Mis calificaciones' (grades, genuinely distinct = course scores+feedback). Fold the unique pieces of the other two INTO lifetime: add the horizontal level-track ladder (from S.progress, scr-profile.ts:81-92) and the certificates+badge grid (from S.badges, scr-profile.ts:140-169) as sections of lifetimeProfile. Remove the 'progress' and 'badges' items from NAV.student.progress group in app/lib/shell.ts:30-35 (keep the routes registered so deep links/dashboard CTAs still resolve, exactly as the codebase already does for forum/arsenal). Net: 4 progress destinations → 2.
findings.file: app/lib/shell.ts (NAV.student 'group.progress', lines 30-35) + app/lib/scr-lifetime.ts (S.lifetimeProfile.render) absorbing level-track from app/lib/scr-profile.ts S.progress and cert/badge grid from S.badges

findings.lens: Content reduction / minimalist aesthetic / Nielsen consistency
findings.problem: The dashboard Achievements card (scr-core.ts:291-309) ends with a button that is a byte-for-byte copy of the hero's primary CTA: onclick="${na.onclick}">${na.ic} ${na.cta} — the same 'Continuar lección' / 'Reservar sesión' action already shown at the very top of the same page in the hero (scr-core.ts:125). The page therefore shows the identical primary action twice within one scroll. The accompanying comment ([CNV-05]) justifies it as 'closing the gamification gap', but it duplicates the single most prominent button on the screen.
findings.rootCause: Each dashboard card was given its own CTA for engagement; the achievements card reused the hero's next-action object verbatim instead of a card-specific action (e.g. 'Ver logros').
findings.userImpact: Redundant primary buttons dilute the 'exactly ONE obvious next action' principle the dashboard's own header comment (scr-core.ts:54) commits to, and add visual noise to an already dense page.
findings.recommendation: Replace the duplicated CTA in the achievements card (scr-core.ts:308) with the card's natural destination — 'Ver logros' → go('lifetime') (or go('badges') until SIMP-01 lands). Keep the XP-to-next bar and next-badge label; drop the second copy of the hero action so the hero owns the single primary CTA.

findings.problem: The Events screen (scr-events.ts) has no action it owns. Its 'Torneos' section explicitly routes every CTA to the Debate Hub ('Ver e inscribirme' and 'Ver todos en el Debate Hub' both fire data-go-tournaments → go('debate'), scr-events.ts:41,66,77-78) and its file header states 'La inscripción a torneos NO se duplica: vive en el Debate Hub'. Its 'Próximos eventos' list is the same DB.events the dashboard right-rail already shows as 'Próximas sesiones' fallback (scr-core.ts:250-256). So Events is a read-only mirror of two surfaces with a redirect button.
findings.rootCause: Events was added to satisfy the PRD §3.1 '📅 Events' nav slot, but the actual event/tournament data and actions already live in dashboard + Debate Hub, leaving Events as a thin aggregator.
findings.userImpact: An extra top-level nav item (and mobile tab pressure) for a page that can't do anything you can't do faster from the dashboard or Debate Hub; reinforces the 'too many places' problem from SIMP-01.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) eliminate the Events nav item and fold 'seminars/live sessions' into the dashboard upcoming rail + tournaments into Debate Hub (lowest-cost, highest-clarity), OR (b) if Events must stay per PRD, give it the ONE thing it lacks — actual seminar/workshop RSVP that exists nowhere else — so it earns its slot. Do not keep it as a pure redirect. Recommend (a): remove the events entry from NAV.student 'group.main' (shell.ts:23) and let go('debate') own tournaments.

findings.screen: Coach: Panel de coach (teacher) + Mis cursos (manage) + Constructor de curso (course-builder)
findings.problem: The coach has THREE overlapping course-structure surfaces. S.teacher.managePanel (scr-teacher.ts:136-200) renders the full Cursos→Módulos→Lecciones→Examen tree read-mostly with quiz/video buttons AND a 4-KPI strip (Cursos/Módulos/Lecciones/Exámenes). S.manage (scr-extra.ts:212-242) renders the same course list as cards that each open the builder. S.courseBuilder (scr-extra.ts:248-291) renders the same tree again, editable. The managePanel even links to S.manage via 'Editor completo' (scr-teacher.ts:187), and S.teacher's own page-head 'Calificar' + this embedded panel make the teacher dashboard do double duty as a course manager.
findings.rootCause: The Moodle-style builder (manage + course-builder) was added after the original teacher tracking panel already embedded a structure tree; the older managePanel was never trimmed back to just tracking.
findings.userImpact: The coach sees their course structure in three visually different layouts and must learn that 'real' editing only happens in course-builder; the duplicated KPI strip and tree on the tracking dashboard bloat it and blur the panel's purpose (student tracking).
findings.recommendation: Demote S.teacher.managePanel from a full structure tree to a single compact 'Tus cursos' summary card (count + 'Ir a Mis cursos' button) so the teacher dashboard is purely tracking (roster + at-risk + grading queue). Let manage (list) + course-builder (edit) be the one authoring path. Removes a ~65-line duplicated tree + a redundant 4-KPI grid from scr-teacher.ts.

findings.problem: The 'mark lesson complete' step is a manual action the system already has the signal to automate in two of three activity types. On a quiz, passing it already returns res.courseProgress and marks the lesson done server-side (the results screen shows 'Lección completada', scr-learn.ts:540-541). But on a video player and a text lesson the student must separately click 'Marcar como completada' (scr-learn.ts:593, scr-core.ts:611-612) even after submitting an assignment for that same lesson (scr-learn.ts S.assignment submit) — leaving lessons that are clearly 'done' still showing as pending and blocking the auto-advance the player's 'Siguiente' relies on.
findings.rootCause: Completion is modeled as an explicit user toggle per lesson, predating the quiz/assignment flows that already produce a stronger completion signal (a submitted assignment / passed quiz).
findings.userImpact: Students finish an assignment or quiz, then have to remember a second 'mark complete' click on a different screen; their course % and 'next lesson' suggestion silently lag reality, which reads as the system not tracking them.
findings.recommendation: Auto-mark a lesson complete when its terminal action succeeds: on successful /api/submissions POST for an assign/mic lesson (scr-learn.ts:349-359) flip doneByMe like the quiz flow already does.
