(2) The link-email <input id=gp-email> uses <label class=label> with no for= and the input has no id-matched label or aria-label (L191-192). (3) The report child <select id=pr-child> has the same unassociated <label> (L226-227).
confirmedHigh.rootCause: Labels are rendered as visually-adjacent <label class="label"> blocks without a for= attribute, and the threshold select has no label element at all because its name lives in a sibling span. esc()/markup correctness was prioritized over the label-for-control association.
confirmedHigh.userImpact: Screen-reader and voice-control users cannot reliably identify or target these controls; the threshold select in particular is unusable since the governed child is invisible to AT. Fails WCAG 2.1 AA 1.3.1, 3.3.2, 4.1.2.
confirmedHigh.recommendation: In scr-parent.ts add aria-label to the threshold select e.g. aria-label="Umbral de aprobación para ${esc(k.name)}" (L338); add aria-label to the public-profile toggle similarly; and either add a for=/id pair or wrap the input in its <label> for #gp-email (linkForm L191-192) and #pr-child (reportCard L226-227).
confirmedHigh.file: app/lib/scr-parent.ts (threshold select L338; linkForm L188-196; reportCard L224-230)

verdict.reason: Substantiated in source. All three controls cited in scr-parent.ts lack programmatic labels: (1) threshold <select class="select" data-threshold-child=...> at L338 has NO <label> and NO aria-label — the governed child name lives only in an unassociated sibling <span> at L337, so AT users cannot tell which child it controls (the worst case, since it governs money/consent); (2) #gp-email at L191-192 uses <label class="label"> with no for= and does not wrap the input; (3) #pr-child at L226-227 uses the same unassociated <label class="label">. Line numbers are exact. No centralized fix covers these: the A11Y MutationObserver in Aula.tsx (L967-1014) only enhances .modal-scrim (aria-modal/aria-labelledby/focus-trap), and the [A11Y-06] form builder that correctly emits <label for=> (Aula.tsx L161-165) is used only for dynamically-generated modal forms, not this hand-written markup. The project even establishes the correct pattern elsewhere (scr-placement.ts L46 <label for=>, scr-settings/scr-lifetime toggles use aria-label), so these are genuine omissions, not a false positive. Genuine WCAG 2.1 A failures (1.3.1, 3.3.2, 4.1.2). Minor overreach: the recommendation's "public-profile toggle" (L445) is a <button> with visible text ("Habilitar"/"Despublicar") that already has an accessible name and does not need aria-label.

confirmedHigh.lens: Content reduction / Match real world (Nielsen) / Hick's
confirmedHigh.problem: The role <select> offers FIVE options including BOTH 'Profesor / Coach' (TEACHER) and 'Coach' (COACH) as separate, distinct choices. The whole app treats them as the same thing: isCoachRole(r)=>r==='TEACHER'||r==='COACH', the coach KPI counts TEACHER+COACH together, the 'Coaches' filter chip maps to both, and the backend (route.ts L60) merges them. An admin is forced to choose between two options that mean the same role with no stated difference — a textbook ambiguous/duplicate choice.
confirmedHigh.rootCause: ROLE_OPTS in scr-admin-users.ts (L26-32) exposes the raw DB enum, which carries a legacy COACH value alongside the canonical TEACHER. The UI surfaces internal data-model drift as a user-facing decision.
confirmedHigh.userImpact: Admin cannot predict which value to pick; picking COACH vs TEACHER silently changes the stored role with no visible difference, risking inconsistent data (some coaches stored as COACH, some as TEACHER) that downstream filters then have to paper over.
confirmedHigh.recommendation: Remove the 'COACH' option from ROLE_OPTS so the picker only offers STUDENT / Familia / Profesor (Coach) / Administrador (4 options). Keep COACH in ROLE_LABEL only so existing COACH rows still render a badge correctly. Normalize on TEACHER as the single coach role the UI can assign. File: app/lib/scr-admin-users.ts symbol ROLE_OPTS.

verdict.reason: I attempted to refute this but the core claim holds up against the source. ROLE_OPTS in app/lib/scr-admin-users.ts L26-32 genuinely exposes BOTH "Profesor / Coach" (TEACHER, L29) and "Coach" (COACH, L30) as separate, distinct, selectable options. The role <select> (L53) renders all 5 options unconditionally with no disambiguation, so an admin is faced with two near-identical choices and no stated difference — a real ambiguous/duplicate-choice problem (Hick's / Match-real-world). Every supporting citation checks out: isCoachRole = r==='TEACHER'||r==='COACH' (L34), the coach KPI merges both (L124), the "Coaches" filter chip maps to both (route.ts L60), and the backend ROLES set (route.ts L17) explicitly accepts COACH as a valid PATCH value (L92-96), so picking it DOES write the divergent role. No [A11Y-*]/[ENT-*] guard or comment addresses this duplication anywhere. The recommendation (drop COACH from ROLE_OPTS, keep it in ROLE_LABEL for badge rendering of legacy rows, normalize on TEACHER) is correct and low-risk. If anything, the underlying problem is slightly worse than stated: the app does NOT uniformly treat COACH==TEACHER — the SPA shell (Aula.tsx L25 isTeacher = role==='TEACHER'), the settings role label (scr-settings.ts L64, COACH would label as "Estudiante"), and staff checks in resources/debates/quizzes/skills/submissions routes all match TEACHER only, so a user stored as COACH would silently lose coach UI/permissions. The only thing I can correct is severity: this is an ADMIN-only, low-traffic, expert-user control, COACH is a legacy enum with likely zero/near-zero rows in Fase 1 (coach auto-registration is OFF per register/route.ts:29), and the mistake is fully recoverable by re-picking TEACHER. That makes "high" overstated; medium is the honest level. The finding is real and accurately located; I cannot refute it.

confirmedHigh.problem: The role filter chips are clickable <span class="chip" data-au-role> with no role="button", no tabindex, and no keydown handler — they are completely keyboard-inoperable. This is the ONLY chip-as-filter in the app that breaks the established pattern: marketplace uses <button class="chip"> (scr-marketplace.ts L250/392/397) and scr-teacher.ts uses <span class="chip" role="button" tabindex="0"> (L89). The same component drift hides the entire role-filter feature from keyboard and screen-reader users.
confirmedHigh.rootCause: viewBody/render in scr-admin-users.ts builds chips as bare <span> (L134) and the mount handler binds a click listener only (L225-226), with no keyboard affordance, diverging from the two existing in-repo conventions.
confirmedHigh.userImpact: Keyboard-only and screen-reader admins cannot filter the user list by role at all; WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) and 4.1.2 (Name/Role/Value) failures.
confirmedHigh.recommendation: Make the chips <button class="chip"> (matches marketplace) or add role="button" tabindex="0" + an Enter/Space keydown handler (matches scr-teacher). Prefer <button> for least code. File: app/lib/scr-admin-users.ts render() chips template (L133-134) + the [data-au-role] handler (L225-226).

verdict.reason: The core technical defect is real and verifiable in the cited source. In app/lib/scr-admin-users.ts the role-filter chips are built as bare `<span class="chip ..." data-au-role="...">` (render(), L133-134) with no role="button", no tabindex, and no keydown affordance; the mount handler binds only a click listener (L225-226). `.chip` has no special focusability (app/styles/app.css L206-208), and the global `:focus-visible` ring (L19) cannot apply because a bare span is not focusable. So these chips are genuinely keyboard- and AT-inoperable — a legitimate WCAG 2.1.1 / 4.1.2 issue. HOWEVER, the finding's distinctive framing is inaccurate on two points, which is why I downgrade severity rather than accept it at "high": (1) It claims adminUsers is "the ONLY chip-as-filter in the app that breaks the established pattern." False — scr-teacher.ts's Participantes filter chips (L626-629) are the identical pattern: bare `<span class="chip" data-filter>` with a click-only delegated handler (L675-681) and no role/tabindex/keydown. So this is not a lone deviation. (2) The cited scr-teacher.ts L89 `<span class="chip" role="button" tabindex="0">` is NOT a filter chip — it is a single "En riesgo" toggle control with its own keydown handler (L243); citing it as the contrasting filter-chip convention is a category error. Only the marketplace comparison (<button class="chip">, L250/392/397) is a valid keyboard-accessible reference. Severity is corrected to medium: the screen is admin-only, the filter is a non-essential convenience whose function is fully duplicated by the keyboard-accessible search input (#au-search, L157/L217-222), and an equivalent bare-span filter pattern already ships elsewhere in-repo, so this is not an isolated high-impact regression. The recommended fix (switch to <button class="chip">) is correct and low-effort.

confirmedHigh.lens: Cognitive walkthrough / Match real world / Recognition>recall
confirmedHigh.problem: A report card identifies its target only by a raw opaque ID rendered as a chip: ${r.targetId} (reportCard L63). For targetType 'user'/'coach'/'message'/'booking' the moderator sees e.g. 'ckp9f2a0x0001' with no name, no link, no preview. To decide whether to suspend a user the admin must recognize a human, but the screen only offers a database key to recall.
confirmedHigh.rootCause: reportCard renders r.targetId verbatim instead of a resolved label (targetName) and provides no navigation to the reported entity. The data layer likely already has reporterName, so a targetName/targetLabel is the missing piece.
confirmedHigh.userImpact: Moderation decisions (including the destructive 'Suspender al usuario') are made blind — the admin cannot tell WHO or WHAT is being actioned, which is unsafe for a one-tap suspend and makes the queue nearly unusable for real triage.
confirmedHigh.recommendation: Replace the raw targetId chip with a human label: show targetName when present and demote the raw id to a faint monospace sub-line (or drop it). Add a 'Ver' link that navigates to the target (window.go) where a route exists (user profile / coach profile / booking). If the API does not yet return targetName, add it server-side; otherwise gate the suspend button until a name is shown. File: app/lib/scr-admin.ts symbol reportCard (L54-88).

verdict.reason: The core defect is substantiated in source. scr-admin.ts L63 does render the reported entity as a raw opaque cuid: `${esc(r.targetId)}`, and app/api/reports/route.ts GET (L75-85) returns targetType/targetId/reason/reporterName but NO targetName — so there is genuinely no resolved label for the reported entity. The cited file/symbol/lines (reportCard, L54-63) are accurate. So this is a real recognition-over-recall gap, not fabricated. However the severity is inflated to 'high'. Three claims are overstated or wrong: (1) "the screen only offers a database key" / "nearly unusable" ignores that the card already shows a resolved human-readable target TYPE ("Usuario"/"Coach"/"Mensaje"/"Reserva", L55 via TARGET_LABEL L25-31), the FULL free-text reason the reporter wrote (L68, the primary triage signal), the reporter's name+avatar+date (L72-74), and a status badge — the moderator is far from "blind." (2) The finding's central safety argument — "unsafe for a one-tap suspend" — is factually contradicted by L215-242 [ADMIN-1], which implements an explicit two-tap arm/confirm guard ("¿Confirmar suspensión? Tocar de nuevo"); it is not one-tap. (3) The root cause ("data layer likely already has reporterName, so targetName is the missing piece") understates the work: reporterName is a trivial single-FK lookup, but targetId is polymorphic across 5 tables (user/message/conversation/booking/coach), and the recommended 'Ver' navigation is only feasible for coach (a `coach` route exists in screens.ts; there is no per-user/message/booking route). Real usability improvement, but medium, not high.

confirmedHigh.lens: Accessibility (WCAG 2.1 AA) — keyboard operability of clickable divs
confirmedHigh.problem: In renderStudentSelf the 'Mis programas' rows are clickable <div class="card card-pad lift" style="cursor:pointer" onclick="go('course')"> with NO role, NO tabindex, and NO keyboard handler (scr-profile.ts line ~301). The codebase's [A11Y-01] keyboard-activation handler in Aula.tsx (~line 960) only fires for elements matching [role="button"][tabindex]. These rows match neither, so keyboard and screen-reader users literally cannot open their own enrolled courses from their profile — the single primary action of the student profile body.
confirmedHigh.rootCause: The clickable row was authored as a bare div with an onclick (the same anti-pattern [A11Y-01] was created to fix elsewhere) but was never given the role/tabindex/aria-label that the central keyboard handler requires to function.
confirmedHigh.userImpact: Keyboard-only and screen-reader students cannot navigate into any of their courses from the profile; the rows are invisible to assistive tech and unreachable by Tab. This fails WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) and 4.1.2 (Name/Role/Value).
confirmedHigh.recommendation: Add role="button" tabindex="0" and an aria-label (e.g. `aria-label="Abrir ${esc(c.name)}"`) to the clickable course <div> in renderStudentSelf (scr-profile.ts ~line 301), matching the [A11Y-01] pattern used for lesson rows elsewhere. The existing Aula.tsx onKey handler will then make Enter/Space work for free.

verdict.reason: Attempted refutation failed; every claim checks out against source. scr-profile.ts:301 (renderStudentSelf, "Mis programas") is a bare clickable div: `<div class="card card-pad lift" style="padding:12px 14px;cursor:pointer" onclick="go('course')">` with NO role, NO tabindex, NO keyboard handler. The cited central handler is accurate: Aula.tsx:960 ([A11Y-01] onKey) only fires Enter/Space activation for `inp?.matches?.('[role="button"][tabindex]')`, which this div does not match. No rescue path exists: the row uses inline onclick (not the delegated [data-go] handler), the only setAttribute("tabindex") in Aula.tsx is for modal dialogs (line 984), and `.lift` (app.css:323-325) is purely cosmetic (transform/box-shadow). The diagnosis as an anti-pattern is correct: every other clickable div in the codebase carries role="button" tabindex="0" (scr-core.ts:176/390/527, scr-community.ts:101, shell.ts:113), and every other interactive element in scr-profile.ts is a native <button>. This row is the lone outlier and is genuinely unreachable by Tab / invisible to AT — a WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) and 4.1.2 (Name/Role/Value) Level A failure on the student profile's primary action. Recommendation (add role="button" tabindex="0" aria-label) is correct and would work for free via the existing onKey handler. Minor caveat on severity: the same destination is still reachable via the "Explorar"/catalog button and main nav, so impact is bounded to one screen — but it is a Level A failure on the screen's primary body action, so high stands.

confirmedHigh.lens: Accessibility — heading order; Design system consistency
confirmedHigh.problem: No screen in the cluster has a programmatic top-level heading. progress and badges use a non-semantic <div class="page-title"> (scr-profile.ts lines 77, 141). profile/coach skip straight to <h2> for the person's name (lines 206, 279, 359) with no h1 above it. Screen readers get a heading tree that starts at h2 (or has no heading at all on progress/badges), and the document outline has no page title.
confirmedHigh.rootCause: page-title was implemented as a styled div rather than a heading element, and the profile/coach hero never establishes an h1, so the visual title and the accessibility tree diverge.
confirmedHigh.userImpact: Screen-reader users navigating by heading land on an orphaned h2 (the name) or find no page heading at all, with no context for what page they are on. Fails WCAG 1.3.1 (Info and Relationships) and 2.4.6 (Headings and Labels).
confirmedHigh.recommendation: Promote the visual page title to a real heading: make the .page-title element an <h1> in progress/badges, and on profile/coach wrap the page in an h1 (visually hidden or the existing title) so the name <h2> sits under a proper h1. Keep one h1 per screen.
confirmedHigh.file: app/lib/scr-profile.ts (page-title divs lines 77 & 141; profile/coach <h2> name lines 206, 279, 359)

verdict.reason: Substantiated in source. progress (scr-profile.ts:77) and badges (:141) render the visual title as a non-semantic <div class="page-title">; profile/coach skip to <h2> for the person's name (:206, :279, :359). The shell (shell.ts renderShell, lines 118+) injects NO wrapping <h1>, role="heading", or aria-level around screen content — the render output drops straight into .main below a topbar with only search/nav/bell. CSS .page-title (app.css:116) is purely visual (font-size/weight/letter-spacing/color), conferring no semantics. No sr-only/visually-hidden heading and no [A11Y-*] guard for headings exists for this cluster (the A11Y-01/02/03/06 markers in Aula.tsx cover toasts, modals, clickable rows, and form labels only). So progress/badges have no page heading and profile/coach have an orphaned h2 — a genuine WCAG 1.3.1 / 2.4.6 gap. The codebase's own convention reinforces this: scr-core.ts:592 and scr-community.ts:64 already render .page-title as <h1>, so the profile cluster is an inconsistent outlier and the proposed fix is idiomatic and low-effort. Downgraded high->medium: it is a real A/AA failure but heading-structure only — no content is unreachable, no interaction blocked, visual users unaffected — milder than the codebase's other 'high' systemic bugs. Minor inaccuracy: the finding does not change that the issue is unhandled.

confirmedHigh.lens: Exploratory QA / Nielsen (status visibility, match real world)
confirmedHigh.problem: The 'Imprimir' button calls window.print() but there is NO print stylesheet scoped to the diploma. The only @media print block in screens.css (lines 361-366) targets #pr-print (the admin report modal). Printing the certificate therefore renders the entire app chrome — dark sidebar, topbar, breadcrumbs, and the page-head action row containing the 'Imprimir'/'Volver' buttons themselves — around the diploma. A diploma is the one artifact users will actually print/PDF, so this is the highest-visibility failure on the screen.
confirmedHigh.rootCause: window.print() with no diploma-scoped print rules; the existing @media print block is hard-scoped to body:has(#pr-print) only.
confirmedHigh.userImpact: User prints/saves their official diploma and gets the LMS navigation furniture printed around it (including the print button), producing an unusable, unprofessional document.
confirmedHigh.recommendation: Add an @media print block in app/styles/screens.css that, when the certificate is on screen, hides the shell and the page-head action row and shows only .diploma-wrap. Concretely: give the diploma container a stable hook (e.g. id="cert-print" on .diploma-wrap in scr-certificate.ts) and add `@media print{ body:has(#cert-print) .app-sidebar, body:has(#cert-print) .topbar, body:has(#cert-print) .page-head{display:none!important} body:has(#cert-print) #cert-print{margin:0!important} }` mirroring the existing #pr-print pattern. Mark the page-head .row of buttons with data-noprint.
confirmedHigh.file: app/styles/screens.css (@media print) + app/lib/scr-certificate.ts (S.certificate.render, .diploma-wrap container + print button row)

verdict.reason: Substantiated in source. scr-certificate.ts:57 calls window.print() with no diploma-scoped print stylesheet. The only @media print block in the codebase (screens.css:361-367) is hard-scoped to body:has(#pr-print) — the parent monthly-report modal — and cannot match the certificate screen; grep confirms no other @media print/@page/onbeforeprint anywhere in app/. The diploma container (.diploma-wrap, line 61) has no print id hook, and the page-head button row (lines 56-59 with Imprimir/Volver) has no data-noprint (the only data-noprint usages are in scr-parent.ts). shell.ts renders a persistent .sidebar (l148) and .topbar (l169) wrapped in .app, so window.print() prints the full LMS chrome plus the action buttons around the diploma. Real, user-facing, high-visibility defect (a diploma is the canonical print/PDF artifact); high severity is justified and effort is small. One minor inaccuracy: the recommendation says .app-sidebar but the actual class is .sidebar — this affects the proposed selector text only, not the validity of the problem.

confirmedHigh.lens: Information architecture & discoverability; Nielsen (match real world, consistency, recognition>recall)
confirmedHigh.problem: The coach onboarding empty-state button 'Activa tu perfil de coach' routes to data-go="coach", which is the read-only PUBLIC coach profile (S.coach / renderStudentSelf view). That route is registered with nav:'profile' and crumbs ['OTR','Coach'], so the coach lands on a non-editable page, the sidebar highlights 'Mi perfil' (implying it's their editable profile when it is not), and the breadcrumb reads a generic 'OTR / Coach' that doesn't match where they are. The coach's actual editable profile is r:'profile' (renderCoachSelf, per the explicit comment in shell.ts lines 61-62).
confirmedHigh.rootCause: Two distinct concepts share confusingly-named routes: 'coach' = public read-only view, 'profile' = editable self. The coachwork CTA points at the public one. The 'coach' ROUTE also borrows nav:'profile', so any arrival there falsely activates the Perfil nav item.
confirmedHigh.userImpact: A new coach clicks the one CTA meant to set up their profile and reaches a dead-end read-only view with no edit controls and a misleading active nav state — the single most important supply-side onboarding action fails.
confirmedHigh.recommendation: In app/lib/scr-coachwork.ts line 304 change data-go="coach" to data-go="profile" so the coach lands on their editable profile (renderCoachSelf). Separately, in app/lib/screens.ts give the 'coach' route honest crumbs (e.g. ['Marketplace','Coach']) and consider nav:'explore' rather than nav:'profile' so viewing a public coach never highlights the viewer's own Perfil item.

verdict.reason: Every claim is substantiated in source. app/lib/scr-coachwork.ts:304 — the Coach Workspace empty-state CTA "Activa tu perfil de coach" is `<button class="btn btn-primary" data-go="coach">`, shown by viewAvailability() when getProfile() is falsy (a coach with no profile = the onboarding case). app/lib/screens.ts:57 — `coach: { screen:'coach', nav:'profile', crumbs:['OTR','Coach'] }`. app/lib/scr-profile.ts:339 S.coach.render() is the PUBLIC read-only view: it reads DB.coachProfile, renders NO edit controls (no data-action="edit-coach"), and includes renderReviewBox which is labeled "solo cara STUDENT" (line 436) — i.e. a surface for a viewer to leave a review, not for the coach to edit. The actual editable self-profile is S.profile -> renderCoachSelf() (route 'profile') with `data-action="edit-coach"`/`edit-coach-market` buttons (scr-profile.ts:216-217), exactly as the explicit shell.ts:61-62 comment states. The misleading active-nav is also real: Aula.tsx:68 passes def.nav as activeNav, and shell.ts:131 marks `it.r===activeNav?'active'`; since the 'coach' route's nav is 'profile' and the teacher sidebar has item r:'profile' ("Mi perfil", shell.ts:63), landing on 'coach' falsely highlights "Mi perfil". No guard, no [A11Y] marker, no existing handling found. The fix (data-go="profile") is correct.

confirmedHigh.problem: The primary cards on two flagship screens are keyboard-inoperable. Marketplace coach cards render as <div class="tile click" data-coach="..."> (scr-marketplace.ts:189) and Debate Hub cards as <div class="tile click" data-debate="..."> (scr-debate.ts:225). Each gets a mouse-only click listener (scr-marketplace.ts:512 opens the coach profile; scr-debate.ts:490 opens the debate detail) but NO role="button", NO tabindex, NO keyboard handler. The global [A11Y-01] activator in Aula.tsx:960 only fires for elements matching '[role="button"][tabindex]', so these cards can neither be focused via Tab nor activated via Enter/Space. A keyboard or screen-reader user literally cannot open a coach profile or a debate.
confirmedHigh.rootCause: The [A11Y-01] keyboard pattern (role=button + tabindex=0, relied on by the global Enter/Space handler) was applied to lesson <tr> rows (scr-core.ts:527) but never propagated to the .tile click cards that use bespoke data-* click delegation. The keyboard contract is opt-in per element and these cards never opted in.
confirmedHigh.userImpact: Keyboard-only and screen-reader users on the marketplace and debate hub cannot reach or activate the main content cards — the core action of each screen is unavailable to them (WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard, Level A failure).
confirmedHigh.recommendation: Add role="button" tabindex="0" to the coach card div (scr-marketplace.ts:189) and the debate card div (scr-debate.ts:225); also add an aria-label (e.g. `aria-label="Ver perfil de ${esc(c.name)}"` / `aria-label="Abrir debate ${esc(h.title)}"`). With role=button+tabindex present, the existing Aula.tsx:960 handler already fires .click() on Enter/Space — no JS change needed. Apply the same pattern to any other .tile click / clickable div using data-* delegation.

verdict.reason: Could not refute; every checkable claim verified in source. (1) Coach card at app/lib/scr-marketplace.ts:189 is `<div class="tile click fade-up" data-coach="...">` with NO role/tabindex/keyboard handler. (2) Debate card at app/lib/scr-debate.ts:225 is `<div class="tile click fade-up" data-debate="...">`, same gap. (3) Both get mouse-only `click` listeners (scr-marketplace.ts:512-513 opens coach profile via repaint; scr-debate.ts:490-491 calls openDebateDetail) with no keydown handling. (4) The global [A11Y-01] activator at app/components/Aula.tsx:960 only fires `.click()` on Enter/Space for elements matching `[role="button"][tabindex]`, so these cards can be neither Tab-focused nor activated. (5) The opt-in pattern WAS applied elsewhere — lesson rows (scr-core.ts:390,527), and notably a sibling `<div class="tile course-card click" role="button" tabindex="0">` at scr-core.ts:176 — proving the contract is per-element opt-in that these two cards never adopted. (6) No global pass adds the attributes: the only MutationObserver (Aula.tsx mdlObserver) handles `.modal-scrim` only and never touches `.tile`/`[data-coach]`/`[data-debate]`. CSS app.css:175 only gives `.tile.click` a hover/cursor:pointer affordance, confirming mouse-centric design. Note: some other `.tile click` instances (Aula.tsx:277,429) are real `<button>` elements and thus natively keyboard-operable — but the two cited divs are not. This is a genuine WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard (Level A) failure on the primary action of two in-scope flagship screens. Recommendation (add role="button" tabindex="0" + aria-label) is correct and requires no JS change since Aula.tsx:960 already handles activation.

confirmedHigh.screen: Course (Mi aprendizaje, module accordion) + Trayectoria (lifetime skill rows)
confirmedHigh.problem: Two more primary interactions are keyboard-inoperable for the same reason. (1) The course module accordion header is <div class="module-head" data-acc> (scr-core.ts:415) with a mouse-only toggle (scr-core.ts:493) and no role/tabindex — modules cannot be expanded/collapsed by keyboard. (2) The trajectory skill rows are <div data-lpskill="..." style="cursor:pointer"> (scr-lifetime.ts:174) that expand a 'what moved this skill' panel on click, again with no role/tabindex/keyboard path.
confirmedHigh.rootCause: Same root cause as A11Y-01: clickable <div> containers wired through data-* click delegation were never given the role=button + tabindex contract that the global [A11Y-01] Enter/Space activator depends on.
confirmedHigh.userImpact: Keyboard users cannot expand course modules (so they cannot reach lessons inside collapsed modules via keyboard) nor open the skill-detail expanders on their own progress page (WCAG 2.1.1 Level A).
confirmedHigh.recommendation: On scr-core.ts:415 make the accordion header keyboard-operable: add role="button" tabindex="0" plus aria-expanded reflecting the open state (toggle it in the data-acc handler at scr-core.ts:493) and aria-controls pointing at .module-items. On scr-lifetime.ts:174 add role="button" tabindex="0" aria-expanded="${open}" and an aria-label. Both then activate via the existing Aula.tsx:960 handler.

verdict.reason: Both sub-claims verified against current source. (1) scr-core.ts:415 renders `<div class="module-head" data-acc>` with no role/tabindex/aria-expanded; the only handler is the global click delegate (Aula.tsx:945-946 toggles `.module`.open on click). (2) scr-lifetime.ts:174 renders `<div data-lpskill="..." style="cursor:pointer">` with no role/tabindex; its handler (scr-lifetime.ts:427-430) is click-only. The global keyboard activator at Aula.tsx:958-960 fires Enter/Space ONLY for elements matching `[role="button"][tabindex]` — neither element matches, so neither is focusable nor keyboard-activatable. This is a genuine WCAG 2.1.1 (Level A) failure and matches A11Y-01's pattern exactly: sibling clickables on the SAME page already carry the contract (scr-core.ts:176 .course-card, L390 .mitem, L527 index <tr> all have `role="button" tabindex="0"`), proving .module-head and [data-lpskill] are real omissions, not by design. Cited files/lines/handlers/root-cause all accurate. Could not find any guard, [A11Y-*] marker, or CSS that supplies these attributes. Kept just below full confidence because the recommendation also asks for aria-expanded/aria-controls/aria-label which are enhancements beyond the strict keyboard-operability defect, but the core defect is solid.

confirmedHigh.screen: All screens (systemic) — esp. Debate, Marketplace, Coach panel, Parent, Admin, Trayectoria
confirmedHigh.lens: Accessibility (WCAG 2.1 AA) · semantic markup / heading order
confirmedHigh.problem: The page's main title is rendered as a non-semantic <div class="page-title"> in 42 places; only 2 screens use a real <h1>. Worse, scr-debate.ts, scr-marketplace.ts, scr-teacher.ts, scr-parent.ts, scr-admin.ts and scr-lifetime.ts contain NO <h1> at all and jump straight to <h3>/<h4>. Screen readers building a heading outline find either no top-level heading or an out-of-order one for the screen's own title.
confirmedHigh.rootCause: page-title was styled purely visually (.page-title font-size token in app.css) and the builders emit it as a <div>; there is no shared heading helper enforcing one <h1> per screen, so each builder freelances its heading level.
confirmedHigh.userImpact: Screen-reader users lose the single most useful navigation primitive — jumping to the page's H1 / the heading outline — and encounter skipped heading levels, failing WCAG 1.3.1 (Info & Relationships, Level A) and 2.4.6 (Headings & Labels, Level AA).
confirmedHigh.recommendation: Change the page-title element from <div class="page-title"> to <h1 class="page-title"> (the CSS class already carries all visual styling, so appearance is unchanged) everywhere it represents the screen title; for the heading-less screens (debate/marketplace/teacher/parent/admin/lifetime) introduce an <h1 class="page-title"> as the screen title and demote the current top heading accordingly so levels don't skip. Best done via a shared pageHead() helper so future screens inherit one <h1>.
confirmedHigh.file: app/lib/scr-core.ts (page-title ~L361,L513) + scr-debate.ts, scr-marketplace.ts, scr-teacher.ts, scr-parent.ts, scr-admin.ts, scr-lifetime.ts

verdict.reason: I attempted to refute this but the source overwhelmingly substantiates it. Verified in /Users/wilserbatistamarcelino/repos/OTR_Academy: there are 40 `<div class="page-title">` plus only 2 `<h1 class="page-title">` across app/lib/scr-*.ts (=42 page-title usages, matching the finding's "42 places"; the finding's wording that all 42 are `<div>` is slightly off — 2 are `<h1>` — but immaterial). The 6 named screens genuinely lack a screen `<h1>`: grep shows scr-debate.ts (0 h1, 6 page-title divs, jumps to h3/h4), scr-marketplace.ts (0 h1, title is `<div class="page-title">Coaches` at L244), scr-teacher.ts (0 h1, `<div class="page-title">` at L69/L183/L621), scr-parent.ts (0 h1, `<div class="page-title">` L391), scr-admin.ts (0 h1, `<div class="page-title">` L127), scr-lifetime.ts (0 h1; primary title is `<h2 class="brand-font">` at L106 with h3/h4 below). The cited scr-core.ts lines are accurate: L361 and L513 are `<div class="page-title">`; the only in-scope `<h1>` (scr-core L592) is a per-lesson reader heading, not the page-title pattern, and the other `<h1>` (scr-community L64) is in an out-of-scope disabled screen. No shared pageHead() helper exists (each builder freelances), and neither shell.ts (topbar at L169 has no h1) nor Aula.tsx provides one. This is NOT already handled — the only A11Y marker in the codebase is [A11Y-09] (focus ring) in tokens.css; nothing addresses heading semantics. The .page-title class in app.css L116 carries font-size/weight/letter-spacing/color/line-height, so the recommended `<div>`→`<h1>` swap preserves appearance (only minor UA default margins to watch). This is a systemic WCAG 1.3.1 (Level A) + 2.4.6 (Level AA) failure of the primary screen-reader navigation primitive across the whole app; high severity is justified. The known systemic '&'/login-wave bugs are unrelated.

confirmedHigh.problem: S.teacher.render() concatenates two complete pages on one route: a tracking dashboard (page-head 'Seguimiento del grupo' + 4 KPIs + 7-column roster + 'Requieren atención' rail + 'Pendientes de calificar' card) and then managePanel() which is itself a full page (its own page-head 'Estructura del curso' + 4 more KPIs + the Cursos→Módulos→Lecciones→Examen tree). The coach lands on ~8 KPI tiles and two distinct primary objectives (monitor students vs. author content) with no single obvious next action. This is the densest screen in the app and the only one with two page-heads.
confirmedHigh.rootCause: Two PRD jobs (group tracking and content management) were merged into one render to avoid a route, instead of being split into tabs or separate routes. managePanel is invoked inline at the end of render().
confirmedHigh.userImpact: First-time and time-pressed coaches must scan ~8 numbers and two headers to orient; the genuinely urgent items (at-risk students, ungraded submissions) compete with course-structure KPIs that are reference data, not actions. Heavy vertical scroll, unclear where to start.
confirmedHigh.recommendation: Split into two tabs on the teacher route ('Grupo' | 'Contenido') reusing the existing .tabs pattern (as debate/coachwork already do), OR move managePanel() to its own 'manage'/'course-builder' route (which already exists — there is a 'Editor completo' button to it) and replace the inline panel with a single compact link card. Either way render() should surface ONE primary action (Calificar entregas when pendingSubs>0, else first at-risk student). Drop the 4 'Estructura del curso' KPIs (Cursos/Módulos/Lecciones/Exámenes) from this screen — they are vanity counts, not decisions.

verdict.reason: The code claims are accurate: S.teacher.render() (app/lib/scr-teacher.ts:57-133) does concatenate the tracking dashboard with this.managePanel(...) inline at line 132, producing two page-heads ('Seguimiento del grupo' L67-74 and 'Estructura del curso' L181-189), 4+4=8 KPI tiles (L76-82 and L191-196), and the Cursos→Módulos→Lecciones→Examen tree. The 'manage' route DOES exist (app/lib/screens.ts:52) and the inline 'Editor completo' button (L187, onclick="go('manage')") already navigates to it, so the recommended split target is real. The .tabs pattern is genuinely used by coachwork and debate (scr-coachwork.ts:47, scr-debate.ts:155). So the underlying problem exists. BUT the severity is inflated from low to high. This exact issue was already filed in the project's own prior UX audit as COG-08 (docs/ux-audit/UX_AUDIT.md:62), where it was rated LOW after examination of the identical lines; the related authoring split was filed as PRD-05 at medium — neither at high. Severity is overstated for three substantive reasons: (1) the 'no single obvious next action' claim is wrong — render() already surfaces a primary action via the page-head 'Calificar' button (L72) and a prominent btn-primary btn-block 'Calificar entregas' CTA in the 'Pendientes de calificar' card (L126), with the at-risk rail (L110-120) prioritizing urgent items above content KPIs; (2) this is a low-frequency coach/supply-side power-user console, not a first-time-student onboarding surface, capping impact; (3) the two concerns are visually delimited — tracking lives in .split rail-320, then a separate .kit-section with margin-top:28px and its own 'Gestión de contenido' eyebrow — so it is a sectioned long page (a standard dashboard pattern), not an undifferentiated wall, and renders with zero console errors. The 'densest screen / only screen with two page-heads' framing is subjective and does not justify high. This is a defensible IA preference (one-stop coach console), not a high-severity defect.

rejectedHigh.lens: Nielsen (match real world, error prevention) / Exploratory QA
rejectedHigh.problem: currentQuiz() (lines 33-49) falls back, when the clicked lesson has no quiz, to the FIRST quiz with questions in ANY enrolled course — then a second fallback to any quizByLesson entry. A student who clicks 'Examen — Unidad 3' on a lesson whose quiz is not yet published silently gets served a DIFFERENT unit's exam, with that other exam's title, and an attempt POSTs to that quiz id. There is no signal it is the wrong exam.
rejectedHigh.rootCause: The fallback chain prioritizes 'show some quiz' over 'show the right quiz or nothing', conflating empty-state avoidance with correctness.
rejectedHigh.userImpact: Student takes and is graded on the wrong exam; priorQuizAttempt() matching by lesson title will also mis-associate the best score. Silent data/correctness defect on a graded, progress-gating surface.
rejectedHigh.recommendation: Scope currentQuiz() to the active lesson only: if window.__quizLesson resolves to a lesson with no quiz, return null and render the existing 'No hay examen disponible' empty state (already present at lines 372-384). Drop the cross-course and 'any quizByLesson entry' fallbacks, or guard them behind an explicit same-lesson check.

verdict.reason: The fallback chain is cited accurately (scr-learn.ts currentQuiz() lines 40-48 do fall back to the first quiz in any enrolled course, then to any quizByLesson entry), but the harmful scenario the finding builds on it is not reachable. Key refutations: (1) NO PUBLISH STATE EXISTS. The finding's trigger is "a lesson whose quiz is not yet published." prisma/schema.prisma model Quiz (lines 229-237) has no published/status/draft/releaseAt field, and queries.ts loads quizzes with db.quiz.findMany({ where: { lesson: { module: { courseId: { in: quizCourseIds } } } } }) (lines 365-368) — no publish filter. A quiz either exists for a lesson or it doesn't; there is no 'authored-but-withheld' state to create the mismatch. (2) NAVIGATION INVARIANT. Every path that routes to the quiz screen is destFor(it)==='quiz', which is true ONLY when it.type==='quiz' (scr-core.ts line 74), and in every such onclick (scr-core.ts lines 95, 390, 431, 527, 582; scr-learn.ts line 589) window.__quizLesson is set to that exact clicked lesson's id. So clicking 'Examen — Unidad 3' always sets __quizLesson=U3, and currentQuiz() line 36 (byLesson[lid]) — keyed by lessonId in queries.ts lines 500-507 — returns U3's own quiz. The cross-course fallback is never reached for any properly-authored quiz. (3) The fallback can only fire for a quiz-type lesson that has zero Quiz rows in the entire DB (a content-authoring edge), and the finding's own recommended fix (return null → render the existing empty state at lines 372-384) is reasonable hardening, but the asserted user impact — silently graded on the wrong exam, mis-associated best score — is not reachable on a real navigation path because __quizLesson and __lesson are atomically set to the clicked lesson. The 'high' severity (silent graded-data correctness defect) is therefore inflated; at most this is a low-severity defensive hardening of a fallback that never triggers in the real flow.

rejectedHigh.problem: The hub fans out into six top-level sub-tabs (Resumen, Mis debates, Práctica, Leaderboard, Torneos, Analytics) defined in TABS (line 27). Resumen already surfaces a 'Debates recientes' list (links to history), a 'Próximo evento' card (links to tournaments), and KPIs derived from history; Práctica embeds a leaderboard-derived 'rivales cerca de tu rating' finder plus a 'Ver leaderboard completo' link. So Overview, History, Practice, Leaderboard and Tournaments heavily overlap — the same data is re-presented across five destinations.
rejectedHigh.rootCause: Each PRD §6 capability was given its own tab rather than being composed; the result duplicates content and forces the user to learn six locations for ~3 conceptual jobs (my standing, my rounds, compete).
rejectedHigh.userImpact: First-time and non-power users face six choices with unclear differentiation (Resumen vs Mis debates vs Práctica all show debates/ratings), inflating learnability and cognitive load and slowing the find-the-action path.
rejectedHigh.recommendation: Merge to 3-4 tabs: fold 'Práctica' partner-finder into Leaderboard (it is just leaderboard rows filtered by rating), and consider merging Torneos into Resumen's 'Próximo evento' as an expandable section, OR drop the redundant Resumen 'Debates recientes' block since 'Mis debates' is one tab away. Re-evaluate each tab with 'if it disappeared, would users miss it?' — Práctica's unique asset is only the PF timer.
rejectedHigh.file: app/lib/scr-debate.ts — TABS array (line 27) + renderTab (line 447); viewOverview (line 161) recentCard/nextEventCard overlap

verdict.reason: Cited locations are accurate (TABS at line 27 with the six tabs as listed; renderTab at line 447; viewOverview at line 161 with recentCard/nextEventCard), but the core claim — "the same data re-presented across five destinations," harmful redundancy worth a high-severity merge — is overstated and not substantiated as a defect. Reading the actual tab builders shows the overlaps are the standard dashboard summary→detail idiom (Jakob's law), not duplication: (1) Resumen's "Debates recientes" is a 4-row READ-ONLY teaser with no ballot affordance, while Mis debates (viewHistory, 217) is the FULL history as clickable tiles that each open the judge's ballot modal (openDebateDetail) — different depth, different job; (2) Resumen's "Próximo evento" is a single teaser card linking to Torneos via data-dtab, while viewTournaments (349) is the full registrable list with status badges and POST-to-register — teaser→list, standard; (3) Práctica's finder reads lb.rows but serves MATCHMAKING (5 rivals within ±120 of your rating, diff-annotated), a distinct job from Leaderboard's global cohort ranking — and the finding itself concedes Práctica's unique asset (the live PF setInterval timer, mountPfTimer) is irreplaceable, so folding the finder would not remove a tab; (4) Analytics has zero overlap (the finding doesn't even allege it). The .tabs control (app.css:229) is a conventional horizontal tab bar (overflow-x:auto, nowrap), not a novel widget. The data-dtab cross-links ("Ver todos", "Ver leaderboard completo", "Ver torneos") are deliberate progressive-disclosure scaffolding that REDUCES the need to learn tab locations — opposite of the claimed harm. The six capabilities are explicit, separately-listed PRD §6 deliverables documented in docs/PRD_COVERAGE.md (lines 16/20/21/56/57), with an explicit DRY note that tournament registration "vive en el Debate Hub". Ground truth confirms all screens render with zero console errors. This is a taste-level IA preference (tab-per-capability vs merge-to-3-4 are both valid), not a defect; severity "high" is inflated to at most "low".

rejectedHigh.problem: Analytics record rows read the wrong key. recordRow (line 378) computes name via r.format || r.side || r.name, but the data contract documented at the top of the file (lines 6-11) and reinforced by the inline [auditoría] note (line 384) says byFormat rows are {name,wins,losses,draws}, NOT {format,...}. The fields actually shipped by queries/getAppData determine which branch wins. If the live rows carry {name,...} this happens to work; but the bySide cards rely on r.side which is also not in the documented {name,...} contract. The criteria card separately reads c.criterion || c.name (line 437) and the ballot scores read s.criterion (line 555) — three different key conventions for 'the label' inside one file.
rejectedHigh.rootCause: The DB→render contract drifted: the header comment documents {name}, the audit comment claims the live key is {format}/{side}, and the criteria/ballot code uses {criterion}. No single source of truth, so fallbacks paper over a real schema mismatch.
rejectedHigh.userImpact: If the live shape ever differs from the guessed order, format/side/criterion labels render blank (esc('')), giving silent empty rows in the premium Analytics surface with no error — the worst kind of data bug because it looks intentional.
rejectedHigh.recommendation: Pin the contract in one place: decide the canonical key (recommend {name}) and normalize at the data layer (queries.ts / getAppData) so recordRow can read a single r.name. Remove the speculative r.format||r.side fallback chain and the contradictory header vs audit comments. Add a non-null assertion or visible '—' placeholder so a missing label is detectable, not silent.
rejectedHigh.file: app/lib/scr-debate.ts — recordRow (line 378-388), critCard (line 437); align with queries.ts analytics shape

verdict.reason: The finding inverts the source of truth. It treats the stale header comment {name,wins,losses,draws} (scr-debate.ts lines 6-11) as the contract and the render fallbacks as a speculative guess. But the actual producer, app/lib/queries.ts (the only producer; data.ts and app/api/app-data/route.ts emit no analytics block), ships byFormat rows as {format,wins,losses,draws,total} (line 746), bySide rows as {side,...} (line 747), and criteria rows as {criterion,avg} (line 762). The render code reads exactly those live keys: recordRow uses r.format || r.side (line 385) and critCard uses c.criterion || c.name (line 437). So labels render correctly, not blank. The claimed 'three different key conventions for the label' is by design — format, side, and rubric criterion are three distinct dimensions; recordRow is shared by the format and side cards, so r.format||r.side is a deliberate union of the two row types it handles, not a hopeful guess. The inline [auditoria] note at line 384 already states the live key is {format}/{side}, proving the author aligned the render with the data layer. The only real defect is the stale header comment at line 10 still saying {name,...}, contradicted by both the audit note and queries.ts — a cosmetic documentation nit, not a silent high-severity data bug. There is no code path where the live shape differs from what the render reads since the same repo produces both.

rejectedHigh.lens: Nielsen (user control / consistency) / Accessibility / Design system
rejectedHigh.problem: confirmModal() (scr-lifetime.ts line 562) — the downgrade-to-Free confirmation — is not keyboard-accessible and drifts from the house modal pattern: it has role="dialog" but NO aria-modal, NO aria-labelledby, NO Escape-to-close, NO focus trap, and never moves focus into the dialog on open. The destructive 'Cambiar a Free' action sits behind a modal you cannot dismiss or operate with a keyboard.
rejectedHigh.rootCause: A bespoke modal builder that didn't reuse the cluster's own better pattern. The sibling openReportModal() in scr-parent.ts:654-665 already wires document keydown Escape + scrim-click close; confirmModal omits all of it.
rejectedHigh.userImpact: Keyboard/AT users hit a focus-less trap on the one destructive action in the cluster; sighted mouse users can still cancel, but the dialog isn't announced as modal and Escape does nothing — fails WCAG 2.1.2 (No Keyboard Trap intent) and 4.1.2.
rejectedHigh.recommendation: Add aria-modal="true", aria-labelledby pointing at the <h3>, focus the [data-ok] (or Cancelar) button on open, add a document 'keydown' Escape handler that resolves(false) and removes the scrim (clean it up in done()), and restore focus to the triggering button on close. Extract a shared modal helper so confirmModal and scr-parent's openReportModal share one implementation.

verdict.reason: Every defect this finding alleges is already handled by a centralized, intentional a11y layer the reviewer missed by reading only scr-lifetime.ts/scr-parent.ts and not the SPA engine. In app/components/Aula.tsx (the global useEffect that mounts once for the whole app), a [A11Y-03]-marked MutationObserver observes document.body for any added .modal-scrim and runs enhanceModal() on it. confirmModal() (scr-lifetime.ts:562-575) does exactly that: it builds a `.modal-scrim > .modal[role=dialog]` containing an `<h3>` and a `[data-x]` Cancelar button, then appends it to document.body — so it is automatically enhanced. Refuting each claim point-by-point against Aula.tsx: (1) "NO aria-modal" is false — line 978 sets dialog aria-modal="true"; (2) "NO aria-labelledby" is false — line 980 assigns the <h3> an id and sets aria-labelledby (the modal's <h3> exists at scr-lifetime.ts:567); (3) "NO Escape-to-close" is false — onModalKey lines 990-994 clicks [data-x] on Escape, and confirmModal's scrim click handler (line 573) resolves(false) on [data-x], so Escape correctly dismisses with false; (4) "NO focus trap" is false — onModalKey Tab handling lines 995-1000 wraps focus first<->last within the topmost scrim; (5) "never moves focus into the dialog on open" is false — enhanceModal lines 982-984 focus the first focusable (the Cancelar button). The recommendation's "restore focus to the triggering button on close" is also already implemented (line 981 stores __trigger = document.activeElement, lines 1006-1010 restore it on removedNodes). The recommendation to "extract a shared modal helper" is moot — the codebase already chose a superior systemic solution (observer over a shared builder, documented: "Un MutationObserver evita tocar los 6 sitios"). The destructive 'Cambiar a Free' path is therefore fully keyboard-operable and announced as a modal. No code change is warranted.

rejectedHigh.problem: Course name is interpolated RAW into data attributes in two places: data-name="${c.name}" in the manage card 'Configuración' button (scr-extra.ts line 233) and the courseBuilder hero 'Configuración' button (line 277). A course whose name contains a double-quote breaks the attribute and the surrounding markup; a name containing & participates in the known systemic & class. The same value is correctly escaped elsewhere (participants uses data-name="${esc(s.n)}", scr-teacher.ts lines 605/613/614).
rejectedHigh.rootCause: Missing esc() wrapper on c.name in the data-name attribute at scr-extra.ts:233 and :277; the value flows into openEditCourse(id, name) (Aula.tsx:649) as the prefilled 'Nombre del curso' field, so a broken/escaped value also corrupts the edit form prefill.
rejectedHigh.userImpact: Any coach who names a course with a quote or ampersand (common: 'Oratoria & Speaking' already exists in the catalog per the capture) gets a corrupted edit button and a mangled prefill; worst case the row's action buttons stop working.
rejectedHigh.recommendation: Wrap both occurrences: data-name="${esc(c.name)}". Confirm openEditCourse receives the unescaped logical name (it reads from DB.teacherCourses by id at Aula.tsx:651, so the data-name is only a fallback — escaping it is safe and correct).
rejectedHigh.file: app/lib/scr-extra.ts (S.manage card line 233; S.courseBuilder hero line 277)

verdict.reason:
REFUTED. The finding's core technical premise is false because it ignores the data layer. teacherCourses[].name is ALREADY HTML-escaped server-side in queries.ts:1553 (`name: esc(c.name)`), and esc() (app/lib/esc.ts) escapes the double-quote to " (line 6). So by the time c.name reaches scr-extra.ts:233 (manage card) and :277 (courseBuilder hero), any `"` is already `"` and any `&` is already `&`. Both are valid, safe content inside a double-quoted HTML attribute — there is NO attribute break-out, NO markup corruption, and NO mechanism for the row's action buttons to stop working. The "high" severity (broken attribute / dead buttons) is therefore unsubstantiated in the current source.

The recommendation would INTRODUCE a bug, not fix one. Wrapping with esc(c.name) double-escapes already-escaped data: the real seed course "Oratoria & Speaking" (prisma/seed.ts:347) becomes `Oratoria & Speaking` after queries.ts:1553, then `Oratoria &amp; Speaking` after the recommended re-esc. When openEditCourse reads dataset.name and prefills the form, the user would see the mangled `Oratoria & Speaking` — the exact double-escape the finding claims to prevent.

The rootCause also misreads the consumption. Aula.tsx:653 sets the "Nombre del curso" prefill to `value: name` — the value passed FROM data-name (Aula.tsx:885 `editEl.dataset.name`), NOT the DB-loaded `c` object. `c` (Aula.tsx:651) only supplies the OTHER fields (format, modality, etc.). So the finding's claim that "data-name is only a fallback" is wrong; the raw (and already-correctly-escaped) data-name IS the prefill source.

The cited "correctly escaped" reference is itself the buggy pattern. base.students[].n is also pre-escaped at the data layer (queries.ts:1524 `n: esc(e.user.name)`), so scr-teacher.ts:613/614 `data-name="${esc(s.n)}"` actually RE-escapes already-escaped data — a latent double-escape, not a model of correctness. The finding holds the buggy lines up as the standard and demands the correct lines (233/277, which apply esc() zero times to once-escaped data) be made to match them.

The genuine (already-known, prompt-noted) systemic double-escape actually lives at scr-extra.ts:228/263/270 where esc(c.name) is applied to the already-escaped value for the VISIBLE title — lines the finding does not target. Net: as filed, the problem does not exist, the cited reference is wrong, the rootCause misreads the flow, and the proposed fix is harmful. Not a finding.

rejectedHigh.problem: The right rail stacks SIX cards (Seguridad y consentimiento, Mensajes de coaches, Membresía y facturación, Reporte mensual, Vincular otro estudiante) plus three SEPARATE per-child control lists scattered across cards: skill/attendance in the main child cards, the per-child approval-threshold selects inside the Membership card, and the per-child public-profile toggles inside the Security card. A parent who wants to adjust 'what my child can do' must hunt across three different cards. This blows past Miller's 7+-2 for a single scannable surface and there is no single 'per-child controls' home.
rejectedHigh.rootCause: Controls are grouped by OTR's internal domain (billing, security, profile) rather than by the parent's mental model (per child). scr-parent.ts builds three independent kids.filter(k=>k.id).map(...) lists in three different card builders (childCard, membershipCard's thresholds at lines 330-340, and the public-profile block at lines 442-448).
rejectedHigh.userImpact: A non-technical parent (the exact target audience) cannot form a mental model of where to manage a given child; approval threshold, public profile and progress for the same child live in three visually distant cards. Decisions that belong together are fragmented.
rejectedHigh.recommendation: Consolidate per-child controls. Move the per-child approval-threshold select (membershipCard) and the per-child public-profile toggle (security card) into a single collapsible 'Controles de {child}' section inside each childCard in scr-parent.ts, leaving the Membership card for the plan/billing only and the Security card for the static reassurance bullets only. This removes two whole per-child lists from the rail and gives each child one self-contained control surface.
rejectedHigh.file: app/lib/scr-parent.ts (childCard L124-186; membershipCard thresholds L319-354; public-profile block L437-449)

verdict.reason: The finding's factual substrate is accurate but it is a subjective IA preference dressed as a high-severity defect, not a real defect. Verified in app/lib/scr-parent.ts: three per-child lists do exist — childCard (L124), the membershipCard threshold list (L330: `(kids || []).filter(k=>k.id).map(...)`), and the public-profile toggle block (L442: `kids.filter(k=>k.id).map(...)`) — and the cited symbols/line numbers are essentially correct. However: (1) the screen renders with ZERO console errors (ground truth) and nothing is broken — this is a redesign proposal, not a bug. (2) The grouping is deliberate and PRD-documented, not accidental domain leakage: the threshold control is labeled PRD §11.3 (payer/billing) under a card whose own heading is 'Umbral de aprobación por hijo/a', and the public-profile toggle is §8.4 consent, sitting under a card explicitly titled 'Perfil público — requiere tu consentimiento' with explanatory copy. Each per-child list has its own heading and instructional text, so they are labeled, purposeful groups, not scattered scraps. (3) Miller's 7±2 is misapplied — it concerns unstructured working memory, not the count of titled cards on a scrollable rail (a standard scannable pattern). The 'SIX cards' count is also inflated; the rail has five distinct cards. (4) The recommended merge has real downsides the finding ignores: moving billing-threshold and consent-toggle into each childCard duplicates the consent/billing explanatory context per child, breaks the cohesive 'Seguridad y consentimiento' reassurance narrative central to the screen's 'peace of mind' tone, and scales poorly with multiple children. At most this is a debatable low-severity design suggestion, not a high-severity finding.

rejectedHigh.problem: The monthly-report modal (openReportModal, L643-668) sets role="dialog" but is missing aria-modal="true" and any accessible name (no aria-label/aria-labelledby pointing at the report title). It also performs no focus management: focus is never moved into the dialog on open, there is no focus trap, and focus is not returned to the #pr-open trigger on close. Background page content is not inert, so Tab leaks behind the scrim.
rejectedHigh.rootCause: The modal is hand-built as a string injected into a div with no focus lifecycle. This is a house-wide pattern (same gap in scr-lifetime.ts L566, scr-debate.ts L627, scr-teacher.ts L265), so it is systemic visual/a11y debt rather than parent-specific, but it still fails here.
rejectedHigh.userImpact: Keyboard and screen-reader users opening a child's report land with focus stranded on the page behind the dialog, can tab out of the dialog into hidden content, and on close are dumped at document top instead of back at the button. AA failure (4.1.2 name, 2.4.3 focus order).
rejectedHigh.recommendation: Add aria-modal="true" and aria-labelledby tied to the report title (give the <b> title in reportBody an id and reference it). On open, store document.activeElement, call modal.focus() (add tabindex="-1" to .modal), trap Tab within the scrim, and restore focus on close(). Best done as a shared modal helper reused by all the scr-*.ts string modals.
rejectedHigh.file: app/lib/scr-parent.ts openReportModal L643-668 (+ reportBody title L266)

verdict.reason: The finding's core claims are refuted by current source. There is a centralized [A11Y-03] handler in app/components/Aula.tsx (L967-1015): a MutationObserver on document.body that enhances every .modal-scrim. The parent report modal is appended to document.body (scr-parent.ts L666) with class "modal-scrim" set at L646, so it is caught. enhanceModal (L975-985) sets aria-modal="true" (L978) [refutes "missing aria-modal"], stores document.activeElement as __trigger (L981) and moves focus into the dialog's first focusable / the dialog itself with tabindex=-1 (L982-984) [refutes "focus is never moved into the dialog"]. onModalKey (L986-1001) implements a Tab focus trap on the topmost scrim [refutes "no focus trap... Tab leaks behind the scrim"] and Escape-to-close (L990-994). The removedNodes branch (L1006-1010) restores focus to __trigger on close [refutes "focus is not returned to the trigger"]. The recommendation to build "a shared modal helper reused by all scr-*.ts modals" already exists and is documented with that exact rationale (L967-970). The cited sibling instances (scr-lifetime L566, scr-debate L627, scr-teacher L265) are all .modal-scrim and thus also covered. The ONLY surviving sliver: the centralized aria-labelledby wires only when an <h3> exists (L979-980); this modal's title is a <b> (L266, no <h3>), so it gets no accessible name. That is a real but narrow gap — the dialog still has aria-modal and managed focus. The high severity (claiming AA 2.4.3 focus-order failure and "focus stranded behind the dialog") is factually wrong against current code and grossly inflated.

rejectedHigh.lens: Information architecture & discoverability; Content reduction; Hick's/Tesler; Enterprise benchmark
rejectedHigh.problem: renderShell() mounts the topbar searchbox for every role (shell.ts lines 173-176), and Enter routes to the 'search' screen for everyone (Aula.tsx lines 953-956). But S.search (scr-extra.ts lines 293-308) only ever searches DB.catalog (student course catalog) and DB.students (a student roster), rendering course tiles and student avatars. For a coach/parent/admin this returns either nothing meaningful or a list of students they can't act on, and the course cards aren't even clickable. It is a prominent affordance that leads to a confusing or empty destination for 3 of 4 roles.
rejectedHigh.rootCause: Search is a single student-centric screen wired into a shell chrome that is shared verbatim across all roles, with no role-awareness in the result set or in whether the box is shown.
rejectedHigh.userImpact: Coach/parent/admin see a primary search control (universal 'I can find things here' signal), type, and get irrelevant student data or 'Sin resultados' — breaks Jakob's expectation that search returns things relevant to me, and adds noise to roles that should have a leaner shell.
rejectedHigh.recommendation: Either (a) gate the searchbox in shell.ts to role==='student' (desk-only block lines 173-176) since only student data is searchable, OR (b) make S.search role-aware in scr-extra.ts: for teacher search their participants/courses, for parent search their children/coaches, for admin search users — and make course result cards navigable (data-go to the course/marketplace). Lowest-effort honest fix: hide the box for non-students until per-role search exists.

verdict.reason: The finding's file/line citations are accurate (shell.ts:173 mounts the searchbox with no role guard; Aula.tsx:953-956 routes all roles to 'search'; scr-extra.ts:296-297 queries DB.catalog + DB.students), but its severity-driving claim — that search "returns either nothing meaningful or a list of students they can't act on" for "3 of 4 roles" — is contradicted by the data layer in queries.ts. (1) DB.catalog is built from allCourses for EVERY role (queries.ts:1469, comment: "visibles para todos los roles") with enrolled flags, so coach/parent/admin all get a populated, relevant GLOBAL course catalog — the "returns nothing meaningful" premise is false. (2) DB.students is set ONLY `if (isTeacher)` (queries.ts:1522-1526), and isTeacher = TEACHER||ADMIN (line 192). So: a COACH's people-results are their own taught roster (taughtRoster) — i.e. relevant to them, refuting the Jakob's-law claim; a PARENT never receives DB.students (undefined → `(DB.students||[])` → empty), so there is no "list of students they can't act on." The "irrelevant student data for 3 of 4 roles" scenario does not exist in source: admin/coach get catalog + own roster, parent gets catalog only, all non-empty and topical. The only genuine, demonstrable defects are minor polish: the S.search course cards (scr-extra.ts:306) carry `class="tile click course-card"` but no data-go/onclick/role/tabindex, so they are not navigable (contrast scr-core.ts:176 which has `role="button" tabindex="0" onclick="go('catalog')"`); and the box lacks a role gate. These are low-severity nits, not a high IA failure built on a false "empty/irrelevant for most roles" premise. Severity is inflated; the data premise is unsubstantiated.

rejectedHigh.problem: The systemic '&' double-escape (known bug #1) is a design-system contract failure, not a one-off. queries.ts escapes user data at the DATA layer (e.g. line 616 esc(pickLang(c.name,...)), line 790 esc(u.name), line 870 esc(p.specialties)), but the scr-*.ts builders have NO consistent rule about whether to re-escape: scr-core.ts:181/454 and :99/380 call esc(c.name) AGAIN, scr-marketplace.ts:194/206/298/441/465 call esc() again on names/specialties, scr-debate.ts:197 esc(nextEvent.name) — all producing literal '&'. Meanwhile scr-learn.ts:567-569 explicitly does NOT re-escape with a comment saying the data 'YA vienen esc() desde queries.ts', and scr-teacher.ts:600 has the same warning comment. So the same field is treated as raw in one file and pre-escaped in another.
rejectedHigh.rootCause: No single, documented ownership of HTML-escaping. Both the data layer (queries.ts) and the view layer (scr-*.ts) escape, and builders disagree on who owns it, so any field containing & < > " ' double-encodes on roughly half the surfaces.
rejectedHigh.userImpact: Course 'Oratoria & Speaking' and coach specialty 'Lincoln-Douglas & Policy' render the literal entity to users on primary commercial surfaces (catalog cards, course tabs/title, marketplace cards + coach profile + program packages). Looks like a broken/un-QA'd product on the exact screens that sell courses and coaches.
rejectedHigh.recommendation: Pick ONE layer and make it the rule. Recommended: keep escaping in queries.ts (the data layer) and STOP escaping in builders for any field that comes from queries.ts — i.e. delete the redundant esc() at scr-core.ts:99,181,380,454 and scr-marketplace.ts:194,206,298,381,403,441,462,465,481 and scr-debate.ts:197. Then add a one-line contract comment at the top of esc.ts and queries.ts stating 'data from queries.ts is already HTML-safe; never esc() it again in scr-*.ts'. Grep guard: any esc(<field-from-queries>) is now a bug.
rejectedHigh.file: app/lib/scr-core.ts (esc(c.name) at :181,:454,:99,:380), app/lib/scr-marketplace.ts (esc on names/specialties), app/lib/queries.ts:616/790/870 (escape ownership)

verdict.reason: The underlying defect is technically real, but DS-01 is a re-filing of already-known bug #1 ("systemic '&' double-escape"), which the prompt says must NOT be re-reported as new — and the finding itself admits this ("known bug #1"). So as a net-new finding it does not stand. On the code facts: esc() (app/lib/esc.ts:11) is non-idempotent, so esc("&")→"&amp;". queries.ts escapes at the data layer (e.g. :612-624, :819, :870-883 specialties/headline, :1365/:1469 course name+coach), and scr-core.ts:99/181/380/454, scr-marketplace.ts:194/197/206/298/441/445/462-465 and scr-debate.ts:197 re-escape those same pre-escaped fields, producing literal "&". I confirmed the inconsistent contract the finding describes: scr-learn.ts:567 ("YA vienen esc() desde queries.ts") and scr-teacher.ts:600-601 ("no re-escapar (evita &amp;)") explicitly DON'T re-escape, while the other files do. Real seed data triggers it: course "Oratoria & Speaking" (prisma/seed.ts:347) and coach headline "Coach · Lincoln-Douglas & Policy" (seed.ts:1163). Caveats lowering it from new/high: (1) duplicate of known bug #1 per scope rules; (2) factual slips — the cited "specialty 'Lincoln-Douglas & Policy'" is the headline field, not specialties (seed.ts:1126 specialties = "Public Forum, Lincoln-Douglas, Oratoria", no "&"); queries.ts:790 cited as esc(u.name) is actually inside initialsFrom (real one is :798). (3) Impact is cosmetic text only (no XSS), limited to 2 ampersand-bearing fields in current data, so "high" is inflated.

rejectedHigh.lens: Information architecture & discoverability / Hick's law / Tesler's
rejectedHigh.problem: The student 'Centro de progreso' nav group has FOUR items — 'Mi trayectoria' (lifetimeProfile), 'Niveles' (progress), 'Mis calificaciones' (grades), 'Logros' (badges) — and three of them overlap heavily. lifetimeProfile already renders the skill graph (radar SVG + per-skill attribution), a 7-stat ledger, performance/rating, credentials AND the journey timeline. progress re-renders the same 6-skill radar + a level track + a streak grid. badges re-renders certificates + a badge collection grid. The user has to learn which of 4 screens holds 'my radar / my level / my certificate'. Disappear-test: if 'Niveles' and 'Logros' vanished as standalone destinations, almost nothing unique is lost — the level track is the only non-duplicated element on progress, and the badge/cert grid is the only one on badges.
rejectedHigh.rootCause: Each progress facet (skill graph, levels, badges, lifetime) was built as its own screen during separate PRD waves; lifetimeProfile (PRD §8) was added later as the 'moat' and silently became a superset without retiring the older single-purpose screens.
rejectedHigh.userImpact: Students bounce between 4 sidebar items hunting for the same numbers; the menu reads as cluttered and the redundancy makes the product feel less considered than Linear/Notion, which expose one progress home.
rejectedHigh.recommendation: Collapse the Progress Center to TWO nav items: 'Mi trayectoria' (lifetime, the superset) and 'Mis calificaciones' (grades, genuinely distinct = course scores+feedback). Fold the unique pieces of the other two INTO lifetime: add the horizontal level-track ladder (from S.progress, scr-profile.ts:81-92) and the certificates+badge grid (from S.badges, scr-profile.ts:140-169) as sections of lifetimeProfile. Remove the 'progress' and 'badges' items from NAV.student.progress group in app/lib/shell.ts:30-35 (keep the routes registered so deep links/dashboard CTAs still resolve, exactly as the codebase already does for forum/arsenal). Net: 4 progress destinations → 2.
rejectedHigh.file: app/lib/shell.ts (NAV.student 'group.progress', lines 30-35) + app/lib/scr-lifetime.ts (S.lifetimeProfile.render) absorbing level-track from app/lib/scr-profile.ts S.progress and cert/badge grid from S.badges

verdict.reason: The finding's structural facts check out but its core overlap claim is materially wrong, and what remains is a subjective IA preference, not a defect. Verified in source: the student 'group.progress' in app/lib/shell.ts:30-35 does hold four items (lifetime, progress, grades, badges), and routes stay registered in app/lib/screens.ts:40,41,43,80. lifetimeProfile (scr-lifetime.ts) is indeed a rich superset (radar SVG, 7-stat ledger, performance/sparkline, credentials, journey, public-profile toggle). BUT the claim that S.progress "re-renders the same 6-skill radar" is FALSE: S.progress (scr-profile.ts:54-133) renders NO radar SVG — it shows competency *bars* (.comp-row), and uniquely a horizontal level-track ladder, an XP-to-next progress bar, a 14-cell streak grid, and a "Subidas recientes" real-activity feed (DB.activity). None of those four surfaces exist in lifetimeProfile. The reviewer's own disappear-test concedes the unique pieces (level track on progress; cert+badge grid on badges, scr-profile.ts:140-169). So this is thematically-adjacent but distinct content, not redundancy. There is also live coupling the finding ignores: shell.ts:124-125 derives the nav badge for r==='progress' from DB.me.level, and the item carries a 'Varsity' badge label. The recommendation ("collapse to one progress home like Linear/Notion") is an explicit product/IA opinion, not a code defect — nothing is broken, there are zero console errors per the behavioral capture, no a11y marker is missing, and the structure is an intentional PRD-wave decomposition. The cited line numbers are accurate, but severity "high" is heavily inflated for a subjective merge proposal that rests on a mischaracterized duplicate.

clusters.area: Dashboard & core (dashboard, catalog, progress, badges, grades, search)
Learn flow (course, courseIndex, lesson, assignment, quiz, quizResults, player)
